ANNEX 1 GENERAL INFORMATION OF NEPAL 
1.1 E CONOMIC INFORMATION 
1.1.1 Economy of Nepal1 
GDP (Gross Domestic Product) in 2020/21 was USD 31.1 billion, and per capita GNI (Gross 
National Income) was approximately USD 1,043. The trend of GDP and GNI per capita in the 
recent years are shown in  Figure 1.1-1. The change in GDP growth rate by year is shown in  
Figure 1.1-2. 
The growth rate of GDP rate in 2016 was 0.4%, because delays in projects for the reconstruction 
work of Nepal Earthquake, which occurred in April and May 2015, and the impact of border 
closure by India after the release of constitutional in September 2015. However, it recovered 
with the growth rate of approx. 6.0% in 2017 owed to the reconstruction work caused by the 
Earthquake and the restoration of border with India. 
The composition ratio of GDP in Nepal is as follows: agricultural sector 26.4%, industrial sector 
12.5% and service sector 61.1% ( 2020/21), and 64% of the working population is engaged in 
agricultural sector. 
Nepal have also received ODA from the governments of various countries and an international 
organization. There was drop in ODA net accepted amount due to the earthquake in 2015. 
However, it recovered with the accepted amount of USD 1,685 million in 2021. The b asic 
economical indexes in Nepal are shown in Table 1.1-1.

1.1.2 Industries in Nepal2 
Percentage of GDP by sector, agriculture and forestry at 25.8%, followed by wholesale trade at 
15.7%, and real estate at 9.4%. Percentage and trends in GDP by sector is shown in Figure 1.1-3, 
Figure 1.1-4.  
The GDP share of the agriculture and forestry sector has shown a slight downward trend over 
the past decade. However, the agriculture and forestry sector remains the main industry in Nepal, 
and overall, there has been no significant change in the percentage of GDP by sector.

Table 1.1-3 shows GDP per capita for each sector. In Nepal, 64.4% of the working population 
is engaged in the agriculture and forestry sector. However, according to World Bank statistics3, 
the value added per person in agriculture and forestry in 2019 was 704.9 USD/year (2075.8 in 
India, 1568.9 in Bhutan, and 1284.2 in Bangladesh). From this, the low productivity of the 
agriculture and forestry sector is cited as a challenge.

According to ADB statistics, agriculture accounts for 26.8% of Nepal's GDP (compared to 
20.2% in India, 15.8% in Bhutan, and 13. 0% in Bangladesh , based on 2020 statistics ), the 
higher proportion of agriculture and the lower proportion of industry compared to South Asian 
Association for Regional Cooperation (SAARC).  
Nepal's traditional exports include carpets, pashmina, and textiles, but being a landlocked 
country, the country is burdened with import costs to raw materials. In addition, Nepal's textile 
products have not yet reached the level of international competitiveness. The time and cost of 
exporting products out of the country is also a handicap. 
As a result, there are challenges to growing on its own like in coastal countries (India, 
Bangladesh, etc.), such as the development of trade infrastructure. 
Nepal's trade volume (2020/21) was 1. 54 trillion NPR in imports compared to 141.1 billion 
NPR in exports (imports were 1 0.9 times greater than exports).  The trade deficit had been 
expanding year by year, but decreased in 2019/20. The trade deficit accounts for about 30% of 
GDP. 
Income of Nepal is characterized by many migrant workers.  From the start of survey in 1993 to 
the present, there are 5.88 million overseas migrant workers, of which 1.67 million people are from 
Malaysia, followed by Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and other Middle Eastern countries. 
Based on the Treaty of Peace and Friendship between Nepal and India, no work permit is required 
for employment in India  for Nepalese. Therefore, the number of migrant workers has been  not 
recorded. However, ILO report  in 2017 estimated that the annual number  of migrant  is 
approximately 1.0 million. 
The number of overseas migrants reached 527,814 in the 2013/14. But has been declining since 
then. On the other hand, the amount of hometown remittances in 2020/21 is 961.06 billion NPR, 
an increase from the previous year. This is equivalent to 22.5% of GDP.

The ratio of overseas remittances to GDP is 22.5%, which is prominent among South Asian 
countries. Nepal's economic structure is dependent on remittances from migrant workers. As a 
result, the broad money supply (M2)4 to GDP ratio is also relatively high at 107.3%, indicating 
that Nepal is a cash-rich economy relative to its GDP level. 
Below is a summary of Nepal's industrial structure. 
 
 Productivity of agriculture and forestry sector which account for majority in the working 
population are expected to be improved. 
 Delay of industrialization compared to South Asian countries. 
 Large trade deficits. 
 Large number of migrant workers and home remittances. 
Considering Nepal's future development, there is a need to improve productivity in the 
agriculture and forestry sector which is the main industry through the development of irrigation 
facilities, more efficient cultivation, and improved varieties. 
It is also assumed that development  of the industrial sector, construction of necessary 
transportation infrastructure and hydropower development to improve energy supply capacity 
are also important. 
On the other hand, in the short -term challenge is to obtain foreign currency. In addition, i t is 
assumed that the promotion of financing, including international development assistance, will 
become important.

1.2 NATUR AL ENVIRONMENT 
1.2.1 Topography 
Nepal is elongated from east to west. Although the distance from south to north is less than 
200km, the ele vation differs more than 8,000m with Terai Plain of the south to Himalayan 
Range of the north. The Himalayas and the Tibetan Plateau with an average elevation of 5,000 
are known as the roof of the world, which are the highest elevation region on the earth.  The 
geomorphy of Nepal is closely related to its geological structure, which is divided into 5 
geographical zones in an east-west belt (Figure 1.2-1). 
 
i) Trans Himalaya 
ii) High Himalaya 
iii) Lesser Himalaya 
iv) Siwalik Range 
v) Terai Plain 
The mountains include the High Himalaya which is narrowly defined as Himalayas, 
Mahabharat Mountains (2,000 ~ 3,000 masl.) at the southern end of the Lesser Himalayas and 
Siwalks Range (150 ~ 1,200  masl.). The High Himalayas are significantly higher than these 
southern mountains, which causes high impact on the climate of north -south regions by 
intercepting more than half of the troposphere (Figure 1.2-2). 
 
 
Source: modified after R.K.Dahal and Hasegawa S., 2008 
Figure 1.2-1  Regional Geographic Division Map of Nepal  
 
High Himalaya 
Lesser Himalaya 
Siwalks Range 
Trans Himalaya

Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan  Final Report 
in Nepal Annex 1; General Information of Nepal  
 
 
1-9 
 
 
Source: R.K.Dahal, 2012 
Figure 1.2-2  Schematic Section of Nepal in North-South Direction 
 
(1) Trans Himalaya 
Trans Himalaya is a mountain range located behind the northern side of the High Himalayan at 
an elevation of about 5,000 to 6,000 m, which is connected to the Tibetan Plateau in north. It is 
considered part of a mountain range on the edge of the Eurasian continent before the collision 
of the Indian subcontinent. Because the monsoon is intercepted by the High Himalayas, annual 
precipitation is less than 500 mm. The high altitude, cold climate, and low precipitation have 
resulted in an arid region with little vegetation and desolate landscape with exposed mountain 
surfaces. The large difference in temperature causes physical weathering and results in high 
sediment production. 
(2) High Himalaya 
The altitude of High Himalaya is 5,000 to 8,000  m above the vegetation limit (between 4,000 
to 5,000 m), without forests only with bushes and undergrowth. Gravel production by freeze -
fracturing is active on the exposed bedrock. River valleys are often U-shaped or cirque valleys 
by past glaciation. The Riverbed is covered with gravels, and glaciers may be subterranean. 
Glacial Lakes, i.e., natural dams of sediments (moraines) formed at the terminus of the pact 
glaciers filled with river water, are often foun d near the snow line elevation in the periglacial 
region of the High Himalayas. The areas above 5,000 m elevation are generally exceeds the 
snow line, where snow exists throughout the year and glaciers develop. Glacial erosion grinds 
bedrock smooth, and cliffs more than several hundred meters high are frequently seen in this 
area. In river valleys, ice caps cover up to near the headwaters. 
(3) Lesser Himalaya 
The southern part of the Lesser Himalaya is called as the Mid Land with areas of 1,000 to

Final Report Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan  
Annex 1; General Information of Nepal  in Nepal 
 
 
1-10 
 
2,000 m eleva tion, where the north of the Mahabharat Mountains. Major cities such as 
Kathmandu and Pokhara are located there and majority of the population is concentrated. 
Landslides are often seen on the mountain slopes, and the land with gentle slopes are used for 
terraced rice paddies. Terraces are well developed in the river valleys of large rivers, and 
settlements and agricultural lands are formed on these flat areas. On the other hand, as results 
of these land use, forested areas are very limited except in the western part of the country, which 
causes soil runoff and landslides. The Mahabharat Mountains in the south are steeper than the 
Mid Land, and settlements and roads are located on ridgelines rather than on the valley floors 
or hillsides. 
(4) Siwalks 
Siwalks is t he first topographic rises bordering the Terai Plain, also called as the Outer 
Himalayas. Although the average elevation is about 600 m, the steep slope makes difficult to 
obtain water, resulting few settlements, which makes remain many of natural forest in this area. 
The elevation tends to increase from east to west. The ridge of the Siwalk Hills in some areas 
splits into two, forming an elongated basin-like river valley between them. These river valleys 
are called Dun Valleys and some of these valleys for mulate own watersheds which are not 
connected to any rivers originate from Himalayas; typical examples are the Babai River in the 
west and the Rapti River which flows into the Kulekhani hydropower plant. 
(5) Terai Plain 
Terai Plain is the northern edge of the alluvial plain created by the Ganges River which ranges 
from 50~300 masl. The three major rivers of the Kashi, Gandaki, and Karnali, which descend 
from the Himalayas, flow southward from the Siwalk Hills, forming alluvial fans and 
meandering plains and join to the Ganges River in India. Although the Terai Plains cover only 
about 23% of Nepal, it is an important breadbasket for Nepal’s food self-sufficiency. 
1.2.2 Geology 
Most of Nepal ’s land is formed by mountainous regions represented by Himalayas, which 
originated from the collision of the Indian subcontinent with the Eurasian continent than began 
about 50 million years ago. This collision occurred as part of the subduction movement of the 
Indo-Australian plate against the Eurasian plate, and is still ongoing, c ausing the frequent 
earthquakes in Nepal.  
Prior to the Indian subcontinent collision, an ocean called Tethys Sea existed. The collision had 
ridden sediments and parts of the oceanic crust up onto the Eurasian plate, which has been 
uplifted and brought to the Trans -Himalayan and High Himalaya regions. This was followed 
by accretion of the crust of the Indian subcontinent, with the older Precambrian geologic bodies, 
which comprise the High Himalaya and the Lesser Himalaya. 
After continental closure, a basin-shaped valley between the Himalayas and India was formed 
with sediments from the Himalayas during the Tertiary. These areas were uplifted by the 
Quaternary thrust faults activities, which forms Siwalk Hills. At present the Ganges River forms 
an extensive a lluvial lowland on the south of the Siwalk Hills, which is also expected to be 
uplifted and added to the Himalayas in the distant future.

Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan  Final Report 
in Nepal Annex 1; General Information of Nepal  
 
 
1-11 
 
(1) Areal Geology 
1) Tethys Sediments 
The sedimentary sequence is mainly distributed in Trans Himalaya, and comprises shallow 
marine sediments during Cambria – Paleogene. Its thickness exceeds 10,000m in total. 
Sedimentary rocks of Tethys sediments are found around the top of Mt. Everest  and Mt. 
Annapurna, the geological boundary divided from High Himalayas is a detachment fault  
named South Tibetan Detachment System (STDS). The strata include abundant fossils of 
various marine lives in Tethys Sea. 
2) High Himalayas 
High Himalayas consist of metamorphic rocks or plutonic rocks in the main part of 
Himalayan Range. High Himalayas as ge ographic division roughly correspond to the 
geological division, on the other hand geologic bodies of High Himalayas are exposed at 
localities in Lesser Himalayas as nappe or klippe. Strata belonging to High Himalayas are 
elevated higher than Lesser Himala yas although Lesser Himalayas is stratigraphically 
upper than Higher Himalayas due to Main Central Thrust (MCT) which is the boundary 
fault. 
3) Lesser Himalayas 
Most areas of the Mid Land are comprised of geologic bodies belonging to Lesser 
Himalayas. The main lithofaces are non-metamorphic rocks or weakly metamorhosed rocks 
originated from sediments in shallow – semi-deep sea, such as shale, slate, sandstone, 
quartzite, limestone and dolomite. Most strata of Lesser Himalayas show relatively simple 
geostructures, whereas strata intruded with granite around Mahabharat Mountains show 
complex deformation. The sedimentary rocks of Siwalk group have relationship to Lesser 
Himalayas by uncomformity, but actually contact is along Main Boundary Thrust (MBT). 
4) Siwalk Group 
The geologic bodies are comprised of Siwalk Hills, and are sedimentary sequence deposited 
under shallow marine to fluvial environments during Paleogene – Neogene. Siwalk Group 
is divided into three members such as Lower, Middle and Upper, and consist of  mainly 
mudstone, sandstone and conglomerate, respectively. The gradual change of lithofaces 
reflects an environmental shift from marine to terrestrial chronologically. The Middle 
Siwalk Group produces plenty of mammal fossils, and these are strong evidenc e for that 
the timing of continental  closure between India subcontinent and Eurasia continent is in 
Cenozoic era. As aforementioned, Siwalk Hills tend to be the more elevated western ward, 
receive the stronger crust pressure. Hence the strata in western ward area harden more and 
show more remarkable deformation. Siwalk group contacts Tarai alluvium by Himalayan 
Frontal Thrust (HFT).  
5) The Present Fluvial Sediments and Alluvium 
Terai Plain is filled with enormous amount of unsolidated gravel, sand and clay transported 
from Himalayas. Similarly river channels passing through the Mid Land to Siwalk Hills are 
filled with a lot of clastics. River terraces are developed well in the Mid Land region and 
several terraces are found on river banks.  There are rich gravels on riverbeds in any rivers. 
Thus aggregated materials are normally mined in river channel or river terraces. On the

Final Report Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan  
Annex 1; General Information of Nepal  in Nepal 
 
 
1-12 
 
other hand, amounts of sediment supply are huge in any rivers.  That is the reason why 
river deposits are actually thicker than the river channel width even if steep gorge. In High 
Himalayas areas, moraines sometimes form natural dams. Moraines consist of 
unconsolidated layers of miscellaneous size compotions, and keep stable by barely balace 
of frozen layers in Moraines. In future, it is c oncerned that Global Warming facilitates 
recession of glacier and to reactivate river incising, then the natural dams break. 
(2) Geostructure 
When the Indian subcontinent has collided into the Eurasia continent, the Indo-Australian Plate 
has tended to subside below the Eurasian Plate. However because the subduction was not able 
to reach up to deep zone because the crust of the Indian subcontinent consists of mainly granite 
which is lighter than the Eurasian continent, and the resultant force between the compres sive 
force from India and the resistant force from Eurasia has made the back part of subduction zone 
lift up. The crust of the Indian subcontinent has been peeled off from the Indo-Australian Plate 
and has accreted to the Eurasian continent, whereas the collision has continued. Thus a new 
subduction zone has been formed on the side of India one after another (Arita, 1988) ( Figure 
1.2-3). The High Himalayan ridges, the Mahabharat Mountains, and the Siwalk Hills were 
brought by sequential lift promoted from the subduction process. The past subduction zones are 
now boundary thrusts of geological divisions. The first collision between the Indian 
subcontinent and the Eurasian c ontinent occurred along a large tectonic line called the Indus -
Zangbo Suture Zone (IZSZ) (Rowley, 1996). When approx. 20 million years elapsed after the 
first collision, the subduction zone shifted southward to the line of MCT in which the High 
Himalaya lifted. The crust of the Indian subcontinent was squeezed into deep areas which make 
metamorphosing of rocks. The metamorphosed crust rose above ground by buoyancy by partial 
melting proceeded under high temperature and high pressure, 15 million years ago (K izaki, 
1988). Then metamorphic rocks were remarkably folded and popped out upon Lesser 
Himalayas. That is the reason why nappes and klippes of the High Himalayas are distributed in 
Lesser Himalayas areas. After then the subduction zone shifted to the line of MBT and made 
the Mid Land rise and become plateau. Furthermore, in the middle of Quaternary the latest 
subduction zone was set along HFT and made the Siwalk Hills rise. At present the IZSZ and 
MCT are completely inactive as subduction, but the MBT and HFT are still active. All boundary 
thrusts found on ground converge with the Main Himalayan Thrust (MHT) corresponding to 
the present boundary between the crust and the main part of the Indo-Australian Plate.

in Nepal Annex 1; General Information of Nepal  
 
 
1-13 
 
 
Source: English translation and some additions to Arita,  1988 
Figure 1.2-3  Ascending Process of Himalaya and Faults 
(3) Geological Disaster 
In Nepal, the most of disasters originated from geologic phenomena is mass movement on slope 
such as landslide, slope collapse, etc. The disasters on slope are possible anywhere except Tarai 
Plain areas. Especially in Lesser Himalayas areas, in the addition with high population density, 
there are geological conditions easy to promote unstable on slopes because of wide distributions 
of highly anisotropic  argillaceous rocks or fractured  rocks (S. Hasegawa et al., 2008). 
Landslides of 87% recorded in the 1990s were triggered by heavy rain in monsoon season (K.J. 
Oven, 2005), and the disasters happen somewhere every year. Earthquakes are also significant 
factor for the disasters on slope. In the Gorka earthquake 2015 (Mw=7.8), damages by 
landslides spreaded over High Himalayas areas (T.R. Matha, et al., 2016). Landslides triggered 
by earthquake rarely happen, but give serious influences widely upon societies and economics 
in Nepal. Cut -slopes along Highway between main cities are easy to facilitate slope failures 
because of non-protection (K.J. Oven, 2009). Many issues to mitigate landslide damages pile 
up even on arterial roads in actual. 
1.2.3 Seismology 
(1) Earthquake Records 
Nepal is one of areas where earthquakes most frequently occur in the world, in order to situate 
on an active plate boundary between the Indian subcontinent and the Eurasian c ontinent. 
Therefore an earthquake monitoring network with 21 seismographic stations, 7 earthquake

Final Report Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan  
Annex 1; General Information of Nepal  in Nepal 
 
 
1-14 
 
accelerogram stations and 29 GPS stations has been under operation since the first 
establishment of seismographic station with cooperation of Universite de Paris in 1978. Figure 
1.2-4 shows distributions of epicenters and earthquake densities underground during 1994 –  
2005. According to the distribution map of earthquake, ge nerally earthquakes happen north 
areas from MBT, and in the central area of Nepal the epicenter distribution are narrowed in 
front of the High Himalayas although in the both of the eastern and western areas the 
distributions spread on the Lesser and High H imalayas. In the areas located HFT which 
corresponds to the front line of the present subduction zone, earthquakes rarely occur except  
Janakpur and the vicinities. Thus Terai Plain areas are the most stable for earthquake in Nepal. 
On the other hand, dense ly populated areas in Lesser Himalayas are the most severe for 
earthquake. That is reason why hypocenters concentrate along MHT which corresponds to the 
plate boundary at 10 – 20km deep due to easily brittle failures. 
Table 1.2-1 summarizes the records of historical large earthquakes (M > 7.0) in Nepal and 
surroundings. The latest huge earthquake is the Gorka  Earthquake in 2015. Although the 
epicenter was located westward from Kathmandu, human suffering in the capital areas was the 
most serious because ground acceleration amplified by soft ground comprised of lacustrine 
deposits and it promoted to collapse mas onry buildings without seismic strengthening. 
Infrastructures, especially roads were damaged widely by landslides or shallow collapses. 
Several hydropower projects under construction as of then were seriously influenced by the 
earthquake. In the Upper Tama koshi HEP (456MW) an access road to the construction sites 
was blocked by a landslide and transportation of construction materials got bogged down, 
consequently led up to behind schedule. Other examples for the projects involved with landslide 
damages include the Upper Trishuli 3A HEP (60MW) and Rasuwagadhi HEP (111MW). The 
most seriously damaged project by the earthquake was the Upper Bhote Koshi Power Plant 
under operation (45MW). A rock-fall broke the penstock and then a glacial lake outburst flood 
(GLOF) triggered by the earthquake swallowed up the opened power plant. This damage had 
forced to stop the operation for 4 years.

(2) Seismic Hazard Map 
Many scientific findings about earthquakes in the Nepal Himalayan region had been 
accumulated until the 2000s since the earthquake monitoring network started operation in 1994. 
A seismic hazard map in t he whole of Nepal was formulated on the basis of these findings 
(Figure 1.2-5). The hazard map adopts peak ground acceleration (PGA) as intensity of seismic 
motion, of which probabikistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA) calculated. The analysis 
conditions are the follows. 
 There is MHT as a detachment fault between the Indo -Australian Plate and the Eurasian 
Plate, the MHT model is Mid Crustal Ramp at the origin as MFT. It is assumption that the 
Indo-Australian Plate can be slipped along MHT but the point adjacent to the front of 
Himalayas is in locked zone. 
 The epicenter distributions between the central region and the western and eastern regions 
show different tendency, th us the MHT segments are divided at 82°E and 87°E of east 
longitude. 
 The slip rate of MHT is defined to 20 mm/year, and it is assumption that the earthquakes 
comparable to the Bihar Earthquake in 1934 (M=8.3) repeat once every 500 years.

Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan  Final Report 
in Nepal Annex 1; General Information of Nepal  
 
 
1-17 
 
 It is assumption that M5 earthquakes occur every year. 
 The attenuation model is adopted to a formula of Youngs et al (1997) which shows the most 
high correlation with measured PGAs in the Uttakashi Earthquake (Mw=6.8) and the 
Chamauli Earthquake (Mw = 6.6), as the follows, 
 
In(PGA) = 0.2418 + 1.414M – 2.552In(rrup＋1.7818e0.554M) + 0.00607H 
 
where, M : Moment magnitude 
 rrup : The shortest distance up to the fault (km) 
 H : Depth (km) 
 Earthquake emergences on each segment of MHT follow Poisson distribution, and the target 
earthquakes which are 10% exceedance probability of occurring within 50 years (1/500 
years earthquake).    
The International Commission On large Dam (ICOLD) defines earthquakes of 50% exceedance 
probability of occurring within 100 years (1/145 years earthquake) for operation basis 
earthquake (OBE) (ICOLD, 2010). Nepal ’s seismic hazard map adopts more severe 
earthquakes than the ICOLD standard. Thus small and middle scale ROR hydropower projects 
may be able to set PGA in the hazard map to design seismic coefficient immdediatry. However 
reservoir type hydropower projects in Nepal normally require a large dam higher than 100m, 
therefore the design seismic coefficient has to  be individually calculated by seismic hazard 
analysis in accordance with the ICOLD guideline. 
 
 
Source: Nepal Seismological Centre, 2002

1.2.4 Climate 
(1) Temperature 
Nepal lies between 26 and 32 degrees north latitude, which belongs to the mid- latitude zone. 
On the other hand, climatic zones varies from subtropical to temperate, continental in a narrow 
north-south width of 150-200 km because of the most mountainous region in the world. Figure 
1.2-6 presents the annual trend of temperatures in Birgun (Terai Plains), Kathmandu (Lesser 
Himalayas) and Camgyai (High Himalayas). Winter temperatures generally reach a minimum 
around January. The hottest season varies from region to region, and tends to be earlier in the 
lower latitudes. In the Terai Plains, although the temperature tends to vary slightly from region 
to region, it is not uncommon for maximum temperatures to exceed 40°C. Kathmandu, located 
in the Lesser Himalayas, can cool down to near zero at night during the winter, but daytime 
temperature exceed 15 °C. The annual maximum temperature is around 30°C, making the 
climate comfortable throughout the year. In the High Himalayas, the temperature drops below 
zero all day long during the cold season. The only months when the minimum temperature does 
not fall below zero are July and August. Higher areas than 5,000m, the climate is alpi ne, with 
temperatures below zero throughout the year, making it an inhospitable environment for plants 
and animals. As this way, Nepal is characterized by extremely large climatic differences even 
within one degree of latitude. 
 
 
Birganj : 27°00’N, EL.85m.  Kathmandu : 27°43’ N, EL.1,300m.  Camgyai : 28°02N , EL.3,250m

(2) Precipitation 
In South and Southeast Asia, the monsoon is often used to mean the rainy season. The 
Himalayan and Tibetan plateaus play an important role in formulating the monsoon. Although 
the trade winds that pass over the Pacific to Indian Oceans in the southern hemisphere are 
westward, the Trade Winds are deflected to the north on the Indian Ocean around July due to 
the development of an anticyclone zone over the African continent. These southwesterly winds 
then tilt to the east on the Arabian Sea due to Coriolis forces and are called monsoons. The 
monsoon is blocked by the Ghats Mountains on the western edge of the Indian subcontinent 
and brings rainfall to the southwest coast of India. The monsoon which passes over the Bengal

Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan  Final Report 
in Nepal Annex 1; General Information of Nepal  
 
 
1-19 
 
Bay without any interception and reaches the Himalayas and Southeast Asia. Warm air that has 
been replenished with large amounts of water vapor in the Bengal Bay rises in the Himalayas, 
becoming rain clouds that bring large amounts of rainwater to the southern foothills of the 
Himalayas (Figure 1.2-2). The monsoon, intercepted by the Himalayas, splits into east and west, 
with the eastward winds move to Southeast Asia, while the westward winds move westward 
toward the Karakoram Range in the westernmost  part of the Himalayas. The amount of 
precipitation is the largest in Pokhara, reaching 4,500 mm per year ( Figure 1.2-7). This is 
thought to be due to the southwesterly monsoon reaching Annapurna without bringing rainfall 
in southern Nepal due to a break in the Mahabharat Range, in addition to the monsoon from the 
east. The amount of precipitation on the west of the Kali Gandaki River tends to be lower. The 
rainy season (monsoon season) usually lasts about three months, from mid -June to mid -
September, and about 80% of Nepal's annual rainfall is concentrated during this period. The 
remaining 20% is due to the prevailing westerly winds. In winter, the westerly wind meanders 
from the north and passes over the southern foothills of the Himalayas in western Nepal, 
causing a front to develop, which results in rainfall mainly over western Nepal. 
 
 
Source: Department of Hydrology and Meteorology, 2015 
Figure 1.2-7  Distribution of Average Precipitation by Season in Nepal 
(3) Wind 
Figure 1.2-8 presents the wind speed distribution near the ground surface analyzed by DTU 
(Technical University of Denmark) using a database of wind speed observation records from 
around the world and a DEM (Digital Elevation Model). The areas with higher wind around 
Nepal are the northern foothills of the Himalayas and the Tibetan Plateau. The former is 
considered to be a dry southerly wind which blows down from the Himalayas after the monsoon

Final Report Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan  
Annex 1; General Information of Nepal  in Nepal 
 
 
1-20 
 
rains turning to fall near the tropopause. The Tibetan Plateau has thin air and strong radiant heat 
from sunlight, which causes a temperature difference of more than 20°C during the day, and 
strong winds blowing throughout the year. 
Although information on wind condition is insufficient in the southern foothills of the 
Himalayas, observations have been conducted at several locations (Table 1.2-2). According to 
these observations, the three sites of Dhankuta, Tank marpha, and Jumla, might be expected 
stable wind power generation. In general, wind speed tends to be higher throughout the year at 
elevations above 2,000 m. Especially in Mustan District on the upper reaches of the Kali 
Gandaki River, strong winds with monthly average of over 10 km/hr are observ ed. This is 
thought to be because the river valley of Kali Gandaki River is wide in the upper reaches and 
its basin is connected to the Tibetan Plateu where atmospheric pressure remarkably changes. 
Although two wind power generation plants were installed i n this area in 1987 with the 
assistance of the British government, both of them were blown away due to lack of tolerance 
to strong winds (Ushiyama, 1995).

(4) Solar Radiation 
Figure 1.2-9 presents the average solar radiation (direct normal irradiation and global horizontal 
irradiation). These maps were produced analytically by integrating satellite observation data, 
atmospheric information and digital elevation model. Direct normal irradiation is the vertical 
irradiance which is the amount of radiation from the sun and is highly dependent on atmospheric 
condition in troposphere, such as cloud cover, and is highly correlated with  the precipitation 
distribution. Generally, northwest part of the country has higher radiation, and lower values in 
the southeast are influenced by higher presence of aerosols and clouds in the atmosphere. 
Table 1.2-3 presents monthly total solar radiation in 6 locations in Nepal. It is noteworthy that 
the record of Pokhara of higher precipitation area, is higher radiation than Kathmandu and other 
eastern/central regions of Nepal. One of the reason for the lowest solar radiation in Kathmandu 
is the rapid progress of air pollution in recent years (K.R. Adhikari et al. 2014). 
In Biratnagar, located in the Terai Plains of eastern Nepal, temperatures rise to nearly 40°C in 
summer, but humidity is also the highest. In winter, average temperatures drop to about 10°C 
and fog is common. These conditions causes lower solar radiation in Biratnagar. Generally in 
Nepal with seasonal variation with a monsoon season, the solar radiation is the lowest in winder 
and the highest from April to June before the rainy season. 
Although t he highest solar radiation  is found in the Mustang D istrict with dry weather 
throughout the year, but it is also one of the strongest wind areas in Nepal, which makes it an 
unfavorable area for the installation of solar panels. Although Jumla and Simikot have high 
potential, their mountainous terrain makes large scale solar applications difficult. The most 
economically viable areas for solar power are the Terai Pl ains of western Nepal, such as 
Nepalgunj and Dhangadhi.

1.2.5 H ydrology 
(1) Major River Systems 
There are 4 major river systems in Nepal, which drain out of the country. The Sapta Koshi River 
in the east, Sapta Gandaki in the central, Karnali in the west and Mahakali in the far west. The 
three of the four river systems, Sapta Koshi, Sapta Gandaki and Karnali originate from the 
Tibetan plateau and cross Greater Himalayas. 
1) The Koshi River Basin ~ Sapta Koshi System 
The Koshi basin lies in eastern Nepal between latitude 26°21 and 28°13N, and longitude 
85°20 and 88°13E. It has seven major sub-basins, Tamor, Arun, Dudh Koshi, Likhu, Tama 
Koshi, Sun Koshi, and Indrawati. The Arun, Tama Koshi and Bhote Koshi -Sun Koshi 
Rivers originate in the Tibet Autonomous Region of China, and flow south to the Nepal 
Himalaya. All othe r tributaries originate within the territory of Nepal and also flow 
southwards. The Sun Koshi River generally flows from northwest to southeast. These seven 
basins flow together in the southeast of the Koshi basin and become the Sapta Koshi River. 
It flows southwards to India. The Sapta Koshi River is the biggest river of Nepal. The ‘Sapta’ 
means seven and ‘Koshi’ is taken after the name of Rishi Kaushik who used to live as a 
hermit on the bank of this river.  
The Koshi basin has a lot of glacial lakes upstream. It has about 40% of the total number 
of glacial lakes. The total area of those glacial lakes compromises about 40% of the total 
basin as well. In particular, there are a lot of potentially critical glacial lakes in this basin. 
2) The Gandaki River Basin 
The Gandaki basin lies in central Nepal between latitude 27°46 and 28°12E and longitude 
82°44 and 85°48E. It has five major sub-basins, Trishuli, Budhi Gandaki, Marsyangdi, Seti 
and Kali Gandaki. These five basins flow together in the southeast of the Gandaki basin 
and become the Narayani River. It flows southwards to India. In the Ganda ki basin, the 
sediment yield is larger than in other basins. It has few glacial lakes.

3) The Karnali River Basin 
The Karnali basin lies in western Nepal between latitude 29°04 and 30°27N and longitude 
80°33 and 83°41E. It has six major sub- basins, Bheri, Tila, Mugu, Humla, Kuwadi, and 
West Seti. Its river network includes the Bheri, Mugu Karnali, Humla Karnali, Kuwadi, 
Tila and West Seti. Generally, the rivers flow from north to south. The Humla Karnali River 
originates in the Tibet Autonomous Region of China.  
The number of glacial lakes upstream of the Karnali basin is the most out of the four major 
basins.  
4) The Mahakali River Basin 
The Mahakali basin lies in the far west of Nepal. It flows towards the southwest and forms 
Nepal’s western border with India. It has two main tributaries in Nepalese territory, the 
Chamelia River and the Surnagad River. The part of the Mahakali basin lying in Nepal falls 
between latitude 29°07 and 30°04N and longitude 80°08’ and 81°07’ E. 
 
 
Source: Glacial Lakes and Glacial Lake Outburs t Floods in Nepal, ICIMOD, 2011

(3) Sedimentation 
Sedimentation is essential issues for hydropower development especially in Nepal since many 
of rivers in Nepal produce large sediment. However, it is also difficult to set the precise value 
for the site. Therefore, many studies and monitoring have been conducted. In this Section, some 
of them are introduced based on existing reports (MP2014).  
1) Suspended Sediment Measurement 
Suspended sediment measurement has been conducted by DHM at several river flow 
gauging stations  
2) Specific Sediment Yield for Himalayan Geological Zones 
V . J.Galay, who has studied about sediment yield in Nepal, indicates that specific sediment 
yield correlates with the Himalayan geological zones based on the sediment yield in Nepal, 
India and Pakistan. 
As shown in Figure 1.2-12, the range of specific sediment yield is provided for five kinds 
of Himalayan geological zones, the Tibet Plateau, High Himalaya, High Mountain Zone, 
Middle Mountain Zone and Siwalik Zone. 
 
 
Source: MP2014 
Figure 1.2-12  S pecific Sediment Yield for Himalayan Geological Zones 
 
3) Specific Sediment Yield by NEA 
NEA (Nepal Electricity Authority) provides the specific sediment yield for three areas of 
Nepal: the eastern area, central area and western area based on the measurement data of 
sediment in some basins. 
In the case of the eastern area, the specific sediment yield for suspended load is estimated 
to be 3,000 t/km
2/year referring to the sediment yield data of the Arun basin, Dudh Koshi 
basin and Khimti basin. Assuming that the bed load is 10% of the suspended load, the 
specific sediment yield in the eastern area is estimated to be 3,300 t/km2/year.

Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan  Final Report 
in Nepal Annex 1; General Information of Nepal  
 
 
1-29 
 
In the case of th e central area, the specific sediment yield for the suspended load is 
estimated to be 4,000 t/km 2/year referring to the sediment yield data of the Marsyangdi 
basin and the Narayani basin. Assuming that the bed load is 10% of suspended load, the 
specific sediment yield in the central area is estimated to be 4,400 t/km2/year. 
In the case of the western area, the specific sediment yield for the suspended load is 
estimated to be 3,600 t/km2/year referring to the sediment yield data of the Karnali basin. 
Assuming that the bed load is 10% of the suspended load, the specific sediment yield in the 
western area is estimated to be 3,960 t/km2/year. 
(4) Glacial Lakes 
An inventory of glaciers and glacial lakes in Nepal was prepared by the ICIMOD (International 
Centre for In tegrated Mountain Development) in 1999/2000 with support from the United 
Nations Environment Programme, Regional Resources Centre for Asia and the Pacific 
(UNEP/RRC-AP). This inventory was based mainly on topographic maps published between 
1960 and 1982; for areas where no maps were available, satellite images dating from 1999 and 
2000 were used.  
ICIMOD summarized the survey results of 2001 and 2010 inventories in “ Glacial Lakes and 
Glacial Lake Outburst Floods in Nepal” in 2011. 
Figure 1.2-13 shows the distribution of identified glacial lakes. Table 1.2-7 shows the 
distribution of glacial lakes and their area in the river basins and sub- basins of Nepal. For the 
inventory, glacial lakes are defined as all lakes in a river basin that lie above 3,500m, are greater 
than 1,000 m
2 in area, and are fed by glacial melts. 
 
 
Source: Glacial Lakes and Glacial Lake Outburst Floods in Nepal,  ICIMOD, 2011 
Figure 1.2-13  Location of Glaciers and Glacial Lakes in Nepal

1.2.7 Waste 
In Nepal the collected amount of municipal waste is 1,768,977 ton per year (2016) 5. Among 
them, organic waste has majority (54 % of total municipal waste collected), and apart f rom 
organic waste, plastic and paper etc. are generated 6 . The methods of waste treatment and 
disposal are different at each municipality, which includes (1) landfilling at final disposal sites, 
(2) open burning, (3) open dumping and (4) manure making. Ther e are some municipalities 
where recycling activities have been introduced, whose amount is limited to approximately 4 % 
of total collected waste. 
                                                   
5 Source: What a W aste 2.0 (issued in 2018; World Bank)  
6 Source: Waste management Baseline Survey of Nepal 2020 (conducted from 2019 to 2020; Nepal Central Bureau of Statistics)

Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan  Final Report 
in Nepal Annex 1; General Information of Nepal  
 
 
1-33 
 
Waste collection ratio depends on each city; 87 % in Kathmandu city and the neighboring 
Bhaktapur city; 43% in Pokhara city and 25% in Bharatpur  city, which have the second and 
third largest population in Nepal7. 
1.2.8 P rotected Area 
In Nepal there are protected areas stipulated under the National Parks and Wildlife 
Conservation Act: 12 national parks, 1 wildlife reserve, 1 hunting reserve, 6 conservation areas 
and 13 buffer zones. The total protected area occupies 23.39% of the total land in Nepal. The 
definition and purposes etc. are described in the table below.  
 
Table 1.2-10 Protected Area in Nepal (definition and purposes etc.)  
No. Category Definition and purposes etc.  
1 National park Areas designated for conserving, managing and utilizing flora and fauna as well as natural 
resources. The entry into national parks is prohibited without entry permit or written permit 
from the relevant authorities.  
2 Wildlife reserve Areas designated for conserving and managing wildlife and their habitat.  
3 Hunting reserve Areas where hunting is allowed for the purpose of wildlife management.  
4 Conservation area Areas managed under integrated plan for conservation of natural environment, as well as 
balanced utilization of natural resources. 
5 Buffer zone The surrounding areas of national parks and reserves, designated for providing facilities for 
local communities’ utilization of forest resources.  
Source: Prepared by JICA Study Team based on National Parks and Wildlife Conservation Act 
The following acts are prohibited within national parks and reserves (wildlife reserves and 
hunting reserves).  
 
 To hunt wildlife; 
 To construct or possess house, hut, shelter, or any other structures of any material; 
 To occupy, clear, reclaim or cultivate any part or grow or harvest any crop; 
 To graze any domestic animal or bird, or feed water to it; 
 To cut, clear, fell, remove or block trees, plants, bushes or any other forest resources, or do 
anything to cause any forest resources dry, or set it on fire, or otherwise harm or damage it; 
 To dig mines, stones or remove any mineral, stone, boulder, eart h or any other similar 
material; 
 To cause damage to forest resources or wildlife or birds or any land; 
 To carry arms, ammunition or poison, or use them; 
 To take any domestic or any other kind of animal or trophy by persons other than 
government employees on deputation or visitors of the public paths within the national park 
or reserve; and 
 To block, divert any river or stream flowing through national park or reserve, or any other  
source of water, or use any harmful or explosive materials therein. 
In the case of any development within protected areas, permits need to be obtained prior to 
development. Activities to block or divert river stream are prohibited; and for hydropower 
development, maintaining flow is required. The protected areas located at the river downstream 
include; Koshi Tappu Wildlife Reserve at the downstream of Koshi river; Chitwan National 
Park at the downstream of Gandaki river; Bardiya National Park  at the downst ream of 
Kankaimai river, Rapti river and Babai river.

1.2.9 E cosystem 
Nepal has diverse ecosystem, from the lowland Tarai plains in the south to the Himalayas near 
the northern border. 31 KBA (Key Biodiversity Areas) 8  were identified in Nepal, which 
includes 27 IBA (Important Bird and Biodiversity Areas) 9.    
 
                                                   
8 KBA is defined as “Sites contributing significantly to the global persistence of biodiversity ” and is selected based on the standards 
stipulated in “Global Standard for the Identification of Key Biodiversity Areas” (issued by IUCN in 2016; 
https://portals.iucn.org/library/sites/library/files/d ocuments/ 2016-048.pdf).  
9 IBA is an area designated by an international bird conservation organization, Bird Life International, with the criteria below:  
① Globally threatened species (The site is known or thought regularly to hold significant numbers of a globally threatened species. ) 
② Restricted-range species (The site is known or thought to hold a significant population of at least two range -restricted species.) 
③ Biome-restricted species (The site is known or thought to hold a significant component of the  group of species whose distributions are 
largely or wholly confined to one biome -realm) 
④ Congregations (The site is known or thought to hold congregations of ≥1% of the global population of one or more species on a regular 
or predictable basis.)

incompatible with the survival of the species in the wild), III (species listed in Appendix III; species that are 
not threatened with extinction globally, but regionally)

set a goal to achieve a net zero of greenhouse gas emission by 2045. NDC ( Nationally

Framework Convention on Climate Change) in 2016 and 2020, which set a goal to increase the 
share of renewable energy in total energy demand to 15%.

2-2 
 
The country’s electricity business has a vertically integrated structure, with NEA (Nepal Electricity 
Authority) handling generation, transmission and distribution of electricity. In the generation sector, 
however, many IPPs (Independent Power Producers) have entered the market under the Electricity 
Act of 1992 by acknowledging the new entrants from private sector . In the transmission sector, 
RPGCL (Rastriya Prasaran Grid Company Limited) was established in 2015 as part of the power 
sector reform described below. In the distribution sector, CREEs (Community Rural Electrification 
Entities) set up in various regions for the purpose of promoting rural electrification are responsible 
for supplying electricity to rural households  under CRED (Community Rural Electrification 
Department) of NEA. The Butwal Power Company, which is a private company, also powers more 
than 36,000 homes in the Central and Midwest2, 3. Off-grid operations in remote areas are basically 
handled by AEPC (Alternative Energy Promotion Centre), but some are run by NEA4. 
2.1.1 Major Administrative Entities involved in the Power Sector 
(1) Ministry of Energy, Water Resources and Irrigation (MoEWRI) 
MoEWRI is the competent ministry for the electricity business in Nepal. It is r esponsible for 
formulating policies and laws related to the development, conservation and use of energy and water 
resources, developing river basin plans and master plans, developing power sources, developing 
laws and standards related to domestic and cross -border transmission systems and power 
transactions, identifying and implementing projects at the federal level or across multiple provinces 
for dam, multipurpose water resource use, irrigation and waterborne disaster management, and 
conducting surveys, research and technological development related to energy, water resources and 
irrigation.5 
The ministry was formed in March 2018 when the Ministry of Water Resources and the Ministry 
of Irrigation were abolished and the water and irrigation departments merged into the then Ministry 
of Energy6. It has three departments: DoED, Department of Water Resources and Irrigation, and 
Department of Hydrology and Meteorology7. Under its umbrella are WECS, AEPC, Groundwater 
Resources Development Board, Water Resources Research and Development Centre, NEA, VUCL 
(Vidhyut Utpadan Company Limited), RPGCL and Hydroelectricity Investment and Development 
Company Limited8. 
(2) Department of Electricity Development (DoED) 
DoED is a division of MoEWRI and responsible for practical matters related to electricity business 
policies, such as issuing power generation business licenses. The department’s main function is to 
ensure the transparency of the regulatory framework and promote private participation in the power 
sector through one-window service for various applications and procedures9. However, as we will 
discuss later, it is still in the process of realizing a one-window service. 
The department’s predecessor was the Electricity Development Center, e stablished in July 1993 
under the then Ministry of Water Resources to attract private investment in the power sector. It was 
renamed DoED in February 2000 and became the current department of MoEWRI  at the time of 
reorganization of ministries in March 2018.

(3) Water and Energy Commission Secretariat (WECS) 
WEC (Water and Energy Commission) was established in 1975 for the purpose of integrated and 
accelerated development of water and energy resources. Under the chairpersonship of Minister of 
MoEWRI, the commission is composed of ministers from relevant ministries, members of the 
National Planning Commission, external experts, etc., and meets more than twice a year11. 
The commission reviews multi- purpose, large and medium -sized water resources projects, 
formulates and conduct researches on policies and strategies in water and energy development, 
formulates policies and analyzes operations in bilateral and multilateral projects, and coordinates 
related policies12. 
Under MoEWRI, WECS was established in 1981 as the permanent secretariat of the WEC. It 
consists of the Water Resources Division, the Energy Planning Division, the Social, Economic and 
Environment Division, and the Legal and Institutional Arrangement Division
13. It has about 50 
staff14. 
(4) Alternative Energy Promotion Centre (AEPC) 
APEC was established in 1996 under MoEWRI  to promote the development and use of 
alternative/renewable energy. It functions as an intermediary between the private sector that 
operates alternative/renewable energy and related ministries and agencies, and is responsible for 
formulating and promoting implementation of policies and plans related to alternative/renewable 
energy and energy efficiency15. 
AEPC has implemented a number of alternative/renewable energy programs. These include 
NRREP (National Rural and Renewable Energy Programme), Nepal Renewable Energy 
Programme and the Waste to Energy Initiative. 
(5) Electricity Regulatory Commission (ERC) 
ERC is the regulatory and supervisory body for Nepal ’s electricity sector. It was created in 2018 
under the ERC Act, replacing the Electricity Tariff Fixation Commission 16 . It is in charge of 
approving PPA tariffs between NEA and IPP and retail electricity tariffs and setting standards for 
the construction of electricity facilities17. Under the chairpersonship and secretary, the commission 
consists of the Regulation and Supervision Department, the Tariff and Economic Analysis 
Department and the Technical and Engineering Department, according to its website.
18 
However, as of June 2022, only the chairperson, secretary, senior engineer, junior engineer, general 
affairs officer, finance officer, and 2 others were enrolled 19 . The Cabinet decides on the 
organizational structure and the Public Service Commission appoints officials20. At the beginning 
of 2022, there were reports of conflict with the government, with the government pointing to poor 
operations and demanding an explanation from the ERC chairperson, who refused to do so21. The 
case has now reached the Supreme Court, which has granted a stay of the gov ernment's decision

(6) Investment Board Nepal (IBN) 
IBN is a government agency chaired by the prime minister that was set up to promote domestic 
and foreign investment. It was established in 2011 under the Investment Board Act and 
reconstituted in 2019 under the PPP (Public Private Partnership) and Investment Act. It is 
responsible for approval of infrastructure development of more than 6 billion NPR . As of 
September 2022, the IBN Secretariat has jurisdiction over the following hydropower projects:

2.1.2 Major Corporate Entities involved in the Power Sector 
The following sections overview m ajor corporate entities involved in the p ower sector, namely 
NEA, VUCL, RPGCL and IPPs. 
(1) Nepal Electricity Authority (NEA) 
NEA is a vertically integrated state-owned utility that generates, transmits, distributes and retails 
electricity. It was established in 1985 under the NEA Act by merging the power division of the then 
Ministry of Water Resources with the Nepal Electricity Corporation24. 
An organizational chart of the NEA is shown in Figure 2.1-2. It consists of the G eneration 
Directorate, the Transmission Directorate, the Distribution and Consumer Services Directorate, the 
Planning, Monitoring and Information Technology Directorate, the Engineering Services 
Directorate, the NEA Subsidy Company Monitoring Directorate, the Project Management 
Directorate, the Finance Directorate and the Administration Directorate. The largest of these is the 
Distribution and Consumer Services Directorate , with about 7,000 staff, followed by the 
Transmission Directorate (about 2,000 staff) and the Generation Directorate (1,000-1,500 staff)25. 
It has more than 250 branches in Nepal and over 10,000 full-time employees26. 
The Board of directors, the highest decision- making body, is chaired by MoEWRI’s secretary of 
Energy and is composed of seven members: the president of NEA, the secretary of the Ministry of 
Finance, one representative each from the Federation of Nepalese Chambers of Commerce & 
Industry and consumer groups, and two electricity experts27.

(2) Vidhyut Utpadan Company Limited (VUCL) 
VUCL was established in 2006 to implement large-scale hydropower projects in PPP31. MoEWRI 
is the largest shareholder with a 20% stake, followed by the general public (17%) and NEA (10%)

2.1.3 Power Sector Reform 
Nepal’s power sector is in the midst of structural reforms to separate power generation and 
transmission from the vertically integrated system and encourage private participation. By early 
2022, a committee had been formed within MoEWRI to study power sector reform (in other words, 
the unbundling of NEA)48.  
According to NEA’s Corporate Development Plan 2019/20-2023/24, NEA will separate generation 
and transmission functions from it and split distribution function into seven provincial distribution 
companies during the period (legal separation). In all of these newly established companies, NEA 
will own 100% or a majority of the shares. NEA itself will be a holding company, but will retain 
functions such as subsidiary management, planning and investment, and transmission system 
operation. In the generation sector, NEA’s new development will be handled by its subsidiary, and 
the operation and maintenance of existing power plants will be transferred to the newly established 
power generation subsidiary (NEA GenCo). In the transmission sector, NEA will develop through 
a wholly-owned subsidiary or a JV with another company, but plans to link up with RGPCL, which 
was established in 2015 as a phased step toward separating generation and transmission functions, 
in planning and development. In addition, each power distribution company will procure electricity 
initially from NEA and eventually from the NEA -affiliated NPTCL (Nepal Power Trading 
Company Limited), which has been set up to handle domestic and cross -border power trading. 
Figure 2.1-4 shows the power utility system after these reforms. 
 
 
Source: N EA “Corporate Development Plan 2019/20 -  2023/24” 
Figure 2.1-4  Power Business Structure after Power Sector Reform (2019/20 - 2023/24)

electricity trading in December 2021 49. In addition, Nepal Power Exchange Limited was formed 
in 2019 with investments from several companies associated with IPPAN, and in January 2022, 
the company signed an MoU with Manikaran Power Limited of India for energy trading 50 . 
Manikaran Power Limited will acquire a 15% stake in Nepal Power Exchange Limited51. However, 
while the private sector, led by IPPAN, has been calling for permission to enter into the electricity

2.2.1 Laws and Regulations Governing the Power Sector in General 
The first act for the power sector in Nepal is the Nepal Electricity Corporation Act 2019, enacted 
in 1962. The act was abolished in August 1984 when the NEA Act, 2041 was legislated. Currently, 
the NEA Act prescribes various rules concerning the company ’s organization and its functions, 
authorities, obligations and operat ions. Also, it prescribes that the Government of Nepal  has the 
authorities to direct the company. 
                                                   
53 Here, the year shown next to the law/regulation name is the year per the Nepalese calendar, and the year in parentheses following it is the year 
per the Western calendar system. (Example) Electricity Act, 2049 (1992): Nepalese calendar 2049 (AD 1992)  
54 All dates taking effect are per the Western calendar system.

The overarching act for the power business is the Electricity Act, 2049. The Act liberalized the 
electricity market, which had been under NEA ’s monopoly, and allowed domestic and foreign 
corporations and individuals to enter the power generation, transmission and distribution business. 
The following year, by laws were established as the Electricity Rule, 2050 under Article 40 of the 
Act. 
With the aim of promoting the deregulation of the power sector, the draft of the Bill made for the 
Amendment and Consolidation of Laws related to Electricity and the Bill made for the ERC Act 
has been carried out since the early 2010s with the support of ADB (Asian Development B ank). 
The amended Electric Act sought to split NEA into separate companies for generation, 
transmission and distribution and to encourage competition in the electricity market. The ERC Bill, 
on the other hand, aimed to create an independent regulatory body to attract investment in the 
power sector 55 . However, parliamentary debate and approval of these two bills did not move 
forward amid political instability. 
Then, in September 2017, the ERC Act, 2074 came into effect, establishing the ERC as the 
regulatory and supervisory body. The ERC Rules, 2075 came into effect in 2018 and the Bylaws 
Relating to Purchase/Sale of Electricity and Conditions to be fulfilled by the Licensees, 2076 
(hereinafter ERC Bylaws) in 2019. These prescribes the organization structure of the ERC and 
various rules for its functions, authorities, duties and operation, as well as the establishment of a 
wholesale electricity market and multiple distribution companies. 
In July 2020, the amended Electricity Act, 2076) was submitted to the Parliament, and in August 
2021, the Minister of Energy tabled it in the Parliament. Although it passed the National Assembly 
(the upper house), it is unclear whether it will be considered by the House of Representatives (the 
lower house), and with a general election in November 2022, it is unlikely to pass for a while56.  
As of April 2024, the bill is still under deliberation as the "Electricity Bill, 2080." According to 
interviews with local stakeholders (government officials, local staff of international organizations, 
related law firms, etc.), it is expected to take more time (at least one more year) for the law to be 
enacted. 
Key components of the bill as of April 2024 are as follows: 
 
 Chapter 1  Preliminary 
 Chapter 2  Development and Operation of Electricity Project and Access to Electricity 
 Chapter 3  Provision Relating to Competition 
 Chapter 4  Provisions Relating to License 
 Chapter 5  National Transmission Grid and Load Dispatch 
 Chapter 6  Distribution of Electricity and Customer Service 
 Chapter 7  Provision Related to Electricity Trade 
 Chapter 8  Royalty and Tariff 
 Chapter 9  Acquisition of Land and Building and Environment Protection 
 Chapter 10  Study, Research, Monitoring and Inspection 
 Chapter 11  Offence and Punishment 
 Chapter 12  Facilities and Incentives 
 Chapter 13  Miscellaneous 
One of the key points of the amended Electricity Act is the introduction of the principle of 
competition into the power generation business. Currently,  regardless of government -led or

Competition Provisions provides that competition shall take place with respect to the development 
and operation of generation businesses. Power producers are evaluated and selected on the basis 
of proposals submitted based on public notices. However, this does not apply to projects of 20 MW 
or less under the jurisdiction of state or local governments, projects for which 51% or more of

important point is that licenses for domestic and cross-border power trading, currently granted only 
to NEA, will be opened to the private sector. Chapter 7 provides for licenses for the (domestic)

ranking government officials, local staff of international organizations, related law firms, etc.), for 
the time being, it is assumed that an already established subsidiary of the NEA will be in charge of 
centralizing domestic electricity trading, but in the medium to long term there are also plans to 
open up the market to the private sector, and the foundation for this is set out in this bill. 
2.2.2 Laws and Regulations Governing Water Resources 
In December 1992, the Water Resources Act 2049 came into effect as a comprehensive act on water 
resources. This is the legal framework for the use, conservation, management and development of 
water resources. The following year, based on Article 2 4 of the act, the Water Resources Rules 
2050 took effect as its by law. 
The Water Resources Act prescribes that issuing licenses for the use of water resources in 
hydroelectric power generation should be as per the existing law
57 but that other matters shou ld 
be in accordance with the provisions of the act. 
2.2.3 Laws and Regulations Governing Environmental Matters 
The Environment Protection Act , 2053 was enacted in 1997 as a comprehensive act on 
environmental conservation and protection, and it was replaced by the new Environment Protection 
Act, 2076 in 2019. The main feature of the new Act is that it clarifies the environmental protection 
standards that companies should comply with so that the implementation of a business project does 
not cause environmental destruction. The following year, the Environment Protection Rule, 2077, 
which prescribes the detailed rules of the Act, came into effect. 
Although they have yet to be applied to any actual investment projects, the aforementioned new 
Environment Protection Act  and Environment Protection Rule  set out necessary Strategic 
Environmental Assessment (SEA) processes. These environmental legislations a lso govern the 
implementation of other environmental examinations, based on which each investment project is 
classified to conduct either Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) , Initial Environmental 
Examination (IEE), or Brief Environmental Assessment (BEA). 
2.2.4 Laws and Regulations Governing Private Investment 
(1) Foreign Investment and Technology Transfer Act, 2075 
In 1992, Foreign Investment and Technology Transfer Act , 2075 (hereinafter “ FITT Act”) was 
enacted with the aim of promoting long-term industrialization and increasing foreign investment. 
The Act was replaced by new FITT Act in March 2019. In addition to the conventional equity 
                                                   
57 Electricity Act, 2049

2-17 
 
investment, re -investment, and technology transfer, the new Act enabled the following: 
establishment of a branch, lease finance , equity investment through venture capital funds, and 
investment by issuing securities in foreign stock markets. Furthermore , the Act prescribes such 
matters related to foreign investment as the minimum investment amount, authority to approve 
foreign investment, issuance of visas for foreign investors, and investment-restricted industries. 
(2) Private Finance in Build and Operation of Infrastructure Act, 2063 
As a legal framework for infrastructure construction by private capital, the Private Finance in Build 
and Operation of Infrastructure Act, 2063 was enforced in 2006. Furthermore, in 2011, Investment 
Board Act 2068, which establishes IBN  that has jurisdiction over the improvement of the 
investment environment and centralized management of domestic and forei gn investment, came 
into effect. 
(3) Public Private Partnership and Investment Act, 2075 
The Government of Nepal sought to utilize PPP  to realize large-scale infrastructure projects 
including hydropower. Thus, PPP Policy was established in 2015, PPP and Investment Act, 2075 
was enforced in 201958, and in the following year, PPP and Investment Regulations, 2077, bylaw 
of the Act, were issued. The PPP and Investment Act prescribes the following: establishment of 
PPP Unit and Investment Unit in IBN 59 ; and various items for improving the efficiency of 
investment approval process and efficiently promoting the PPP-style projects60. The main contents 
of the PPP and Investment Act are as follows. 
 Target projects: 
An application needs to be submitted to IBN when developing a project with investment of 
over 6 billion NPR or a hydropower project of over 200 MW. 
 PPP schemes: 
PPP should be applied to all infrastructure projects adopting either one of the following methods. 
After the end of the project period, all faci lities of the project will be transferred to the 
designated government agency based on the PDA (Project Development Agreement). 
• Build and Transfer (BT) 
• Build, Operation and Transfer (BOT) 
• Build, Own, Operation and Transfer (BOOT) 
• Build, Transfer and Operation (BTO) 
• Lease, Operation and Transfer (LOT) 
• Lease, Build, Operation and Transfer (LBOT) 
• Development, Operation and Transfer (DOT) 
• Management, Operation and Transfer (MOT) 
• Rehabilitation, Operation and Transfer (ROT) 
 Swiss Challenge61: 
If IBN determines that application of competitive bidding will fit the national interests, in 
response to the application received from a developer, the Swiss Challenge will be conducted.

If the proposer fails to obtain development rights for the project t hrough the Swiss Challenge, 
another selected contractor will compensate for the development costs. On the other hand, IBN 
may negotiate for PDA directly with the developer if it is determined that any of the following 
is applicable: 
• In case competitive bidding cannot be used, due to the nature of the project or the necessity 
in terms of finance structure 
• In case a new concept or a new technology is used in the project 
• In case of a priority development project determined by the government 
• In case application  of competitive bidding is considered inappropriate from other 
viewpoints per the Act 
 Negotiation and signing of PDA: 
After the Swiss Challenge or direct negotiations, IBN will approve it as a PPP project, conclude 
an MOU (Memorandum of Understanding) with the developer, and grant the right to consider 
business development under the PPP and Investment Act. After negotiations based on the MOU, 
a PDA will be signed between the Government of Nepal and the developer. 
 Additional investment by the government: 
For a project approved under the Act, IBN may add government capital in addition to the equity 
structure proposed by the developer. 
 Incentives: 
If the construction is completed before the deadline set by the PDA, if it is completed at a lower 
cost than estimated, or if it benefits the government in other ways, IBN may provide financial 
or non-financial incentives to the developer. 
 One-stop service center: 
Establish a one -stop service center that provides the following services for the projects 
implemented under the Act: 
• Examination and approval of the proposal from a business operator 
• Services related to corporate registration, taxation matters, industrial and other registration, 
acquisition of approval from the Central Bank of Nepal, and acquisition of licenses 
• Services for obtaining work permits and visas 
• Cooperation in acquiring private land for the project 
• Support for procedures on lease of state-owned land for the project 
• Cooperation in obtaining approval for environmental reports 
• Cooperation to secure access to social infrastructure such as access roads, electric power, 
and power transmission lines 
• Cooperation in obtaining financial and non-financial services based on relevant laws and 
project contracts 
• Necessary services related to foreign exchange 
• Other services required for the development and promotion of the target project 
However, it should be noted that, as will be described later, the one -stop service has yet been 
realized
62, and the actual necessary procedure for IPP is complicated. 
(4) Foreign Exchange (Regulation) Act, 2019 
As for foreign exchange, the Foreign Exchange (Regulation) Act, 2019, enacted in 1962, prescribes 
that the Central Bank of Nepal (Nepal Rastra Bank) has full authority to formulate and implement 
foreign exchange policies under the direction of the government. It also prescribes various matters

related to foreign financing and foreign exchange. 
(5) Hedging Related Regulations, 2075 
In 2019, Hedging Related Regulations, 2075 was enacted to facilitate the financing of large-scale 
infrastructure projects by PPP under the PPP and Investment Act. The Regulations prescribes that 
the government provides hedge financing through the Central Bank of Nepal for foreign financing 
of hydropower projects of 100 MW or more or transmission line projects of 30 km or more. Yet,  it 
does not appear to have been applied to any project63. The main contents related to IPPs and power 
PPP are as follows: 
 Target project: 
Hydropower generation project over 100 MW, construction work of transmission lines of over 
30 km (220 kV A or more) 
 Application process: 
The developer submits an application and required  documents to the Central Bank of Nepal. 
After examination, the Bank approves the hedge facility for the project and opens a dedicated 
account. After the developer deposits certain amount of money in a foreign currency in the 
dedicated account, the Bank de posits the same amount of rupees in a commercial bank. (The 
exchange rate of the day when the business operator deposits the foreign currency is adopted.) 
 Fixation of exchange rate: 
The foreign-currency funds deposited by the developer in the dedicated account will be locked 
from the date of deposit, and the exchange rate will not be changed. The Bank provides the 
developer with a hedge facility to cover the difference between the fixed exchange rate and the 
market exchange rate. 
 Hedging period: 
Up to 10 years. It can be extended up to 5 years. In that case, the exchange rate will be 
determined considering the exchange rates of the last 10 years. 
2.2.5 IPP Licensing and Approval Process 
An important role of the DoED is to unify the contact points for various applications and approvals 
of power projects, but a one -window service has yet to be realized in a situation where multiple 
organizations are divided vertically as follows: 
 MoEWRI/DoED: Issues survey, construction, generation and transmission licenses 
 NEA: The only off-taker in the country. Coordination with NEA is necessary for power plant 
to get connected to its grid 
 IBN: Approves foreign investment of over 6 billion NPR and power projects of over 200 
MW 
 ERC: Approves PPA between NEA and IPP 
Below the main  licensing and approval procedures required for IPP projects are presented in 
chronological order. See Table 2.2-2 for details. 
Any private company needs to obtain a generation survey license when conducting an F/S 
(feasibility study)  (Table 2.2-2#2). Applications are submitted to DoED , which reviews them 
before MoEWRI issues the license. The license must be issued within 30 days (in the case of being 
                                                   
63 Based on an interview with IFC in June 2022

Final Report Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan 
Annex 2; Current Status of the Power Sector in Nepal  in Nepal 
 
 
2-20 
 
approved) after receiving an application, and the survey must be initiated within three months after 
license issuance. It is valid for two years in principle and up to five years. Developers who obtain 
a survey license will have the exclusive right to survey the site. In addition, according to the 
Electricity Licensing Directive 2021, generation licenses are granted to developer with generation 
survey licenses unless they transfer them to others. In other words, the grant of a generation survey 
license means the grant of an exclusive development right. 
When developing a power plant larger than 200 MW, developers apply to IBN for PDA 
negotiations under the PPP and Investment Act, 2018, which took effect in March 2019 ( Table 
2.2-2#14)64. If competitive bidding is not suitable due to the nature of the project, or if the project 
is a national priority project, direct negotiations immediately follow, but if it is determined that 
competitive bidding is in the national interest, the Swiss Cha llenge is held, followed by 
negotiations. However, it does not appear that there have been any actual cases of Swiss Challenge. 
Following the negotiations, IBN will conclude a MOU with the developer, followed by a PDA 
between the government of Nepal and the developer. The conclusion of the PDA grants business 
development rights to developers, while developers are obliged to enter into financing and 
construction under certain conditions. 
Using the PDA for Upper Trisuli 1, which was signed on December 29, 2016, as an example, the 
following rights and obligations are defined by the PDA65: 
 The government grants the developer the right to build, generate and transmit electricity for 
35 years after obtaining a generation license. 
 The developer applies for the generation license within 90 days of the PDA date, and the 
government grants licenses within 120 days. The developer also has 30 days to apply for the 
transmission survey license, and the government has 30 days to grant the license. 
 The developer enters into a financing agreement within 24 months of the issuance of a 
generation license (which can be extended up to 12 months). 
 A Performance Security Agreement is entered into within 35 days of the PDA date, and the 
developer submits Performance Security within 30 days of the loan agreement. 
 The developer starts construction within a month of the loan agreement. 
 PPA is signed within six months of the date of the PDA. 
After the PDA is signed, the operator applies to DoED  for a generation license (Table 2.2-2#15). 
The license application must be made within the validity period of the survey license. The license 
will be issued by MoEWRI  after a DoED  review. The application to license period is no longer 
than 120 days, and construction must begin within 1 year of license issuance. It is valid for 35 
years (5 years for construction + 30 years for operation) but can be extended to 50 years. In addition, 
according to the Electricity Rule, 2050, a licensed developer is also granted water rights attached 
to it. 
Developers are required to sign MOU with NEA for grid connection of power plants ( Table 
2.2-2#17). Additionally, a PPA is entered into with the NEA as an off -taker ( Table 2.2-2#19). 
According to the Bylaw Relating to Purchase/Sale of Electricity and Conditions to be Fulfilled by 
the Licensees, 2076, enacted in 2019, before the conclusion of a PPA, sellers of electri city must 
submit an application to ERC (Table 2.2-2#18). According to the ERC regulations, enacted in 2018, 
the selling price of electricity should consist of two step s: a capacity charge (calculated based on 
project financing costs) and an energy charge (Calculated based on equity, returns, taxes, royalties 
and operating and maintenance costs). Under the Bylaws, the unit selling price for hydropower 
projects of up to 1 00 MW is determined based on standard electricity rates, while projects larger 
than 100 MW are determined based on project costs, but the ROE (return on equity) must not

65 The deadlines listed here are set for the Upper Trishuli 1 project and differ from the standard deadlines.

2-21 
 
exceed 17% in any case. 
Under the, 2049 and the Electricity Rule, 2050, power producers must pay the government a 
royalty of 100 NPR/kW + 2% * annual average electricity rate/kWh for the first 15 years of 
operation and 1000 NPR/kW + 10% * annual average electricity rate/kWh for the first 16 years 
and thereafter. 
Similarly, based on these, a fter the end of the business (after the expiry of the license), all land, 
buildings, equipment and related structures become government assets in the project in which the 
foreign investor’s share exceeds 50%. However, if an agreement is reached with the go vernment 
before the license expires, the IPP can continue to operate according to the agreement. On the other 
hand, in a project in which foreign investors own less than 50% of share, the IPP can continue to 
operate according to the terms of the agreement if it is agreed with the government before the 
license expires. 
With respect to income tax, the Income Tax Act, 2058, enacted in 2002, as amended in 2021 by 
the Finance Act, 2078, provides tax benefits for electric utilities. Under Section 11, hydro, solar , 
wind and biomass power producers that start commercial operations by April 2026 are exempt 
from income tax for the first 10 years of commercial operation and 50% for the next 5 years. In 
addition, more than 200 MW of reservoirs and semi -reservoir based h ydropower projects that 
reached financial close by April 2026 will have periods of 15 and 6 years, respectively.
