2.3.1 Policies and Development Plans for the Power Sector in General 
The main policies and development plans of the power sector in Nepal are as follows: 
(1) Hydropower Development Policy, 2001 
The Hydropower Development Policy was formulated in 2001 by the former Ministry of Water 
Resources sets the following basic targets for hydropower generation70: 
 to generate electricity at low cost by utilizing the water resources available in the country; 
 to extend reliable and qualitative electric service throughout the Kingdom of Nepal at a 
reasonable price; 
 to tie-up electrification with the economic activities; 
 to render support to the development of rural economy by extending the rural electrification; 
and 
 to develop hydropower as an exportable commodity. 
This policy is still in effect more than 20 years after its enactment, but the numerical targets to be 
achieved and the time frame for achieving them have no t been clarified. Moreover, the financial 
support and incentives necessary to achieve the goals and the institutional development necessary 
to promote private investment are not fully implemented. 
(2) Water Resources Strategy and National Water Plan 
The Water Resources Strategy formulated by WECS in 2002 sets goals and strategies for 
sustainable water resource development. The aim is to implement an integrated water resources 
strategy in the short term (5 years), to operationalize the strategy in the medium ter m (15) to 
provide real benefits for meeting people ’s basic needs while increasing other benefits related to 
sustainable water use, and to sustainably maximize benefits from water resources in the long term 
(25 years)
71. However, the implementation of this s trategy has not been satisfactory due to the 
delay in the revision of the Electricity Act and the Water Resources Act. 
The National Water Plan sets out short -, medium -, and long- term action plans for the 
implementation of the aforementioned Strategy, comprising an outline, an action plan, investments 
and macroeconomics, an environmental plan, and monitoring, evaluation, and updating the plan. 
The short-, medium- and long-term targets for the hydropower sector are shown in Table 2.3-2. 
To achieve these goals, the following five action programs were formulated: 
 
 A program to develop cost -effective micro and small-  and medium- sized hydropower 
facilities 
 A program to promote rural electrification 
 A program to improve the planning capacity of the power sector 
 A program to encourage private companies to participate in the hydropower generation and 
distribution sectors

(3) National Electricity Crisis Resolution Action Plan 
The Electricity Crisis Resolution Action Plan , which was formulated in 2008 by the former 
Ministry of Energy, prescribe s immediate and long- term strategies to address the deteriorating 
power situation. The strategies address the follow ing: increase electricity imports from India; 
construct thermal power plants; expand transmission capacity; and respond to electricity theft. 
(4) Action Plan on National Energy Crisis Mitigation and Electricity Development Decade 
At a ministerial meeting in February 2016, the Action Plan on National Energy Crisis Mitigation 
and Electricity Development Decade was approved, and an instruction was issued to the former 
Ministry of Energy to formulate a concrete action plan for mitigating power shortage situation in  
one year and reducing power shortage to zero within two years. This action plan has been replaced 
by the Electricity Development Decade Action Plan developed in 2019, according to MoEWRI72. 
(5) Energy, Water Resources and Irrigation Sector’s Current Status and Roadmap for Future 
(White Paper) 
In 2018, MoEWRI published the “Energy, Water Resources and Irrigation Sector’s Current Status 
and Roadmap for Future” (hereinafter referred to as the “White Paper”). The White Paper analyzed 
the current status, problems and issues of the energy, water resources and irrigation sectors, and 
set the future roadmap for each sector.  The roadmaps for the energy sector , including the power 
sector, specified in this white paper are as follows: 
[ Policy roadmap ] 
 Legislate necessary policies and legal frameworks, revise the Electricity Act, and introduce a 
Renewable Energy D evelopment Act, regarding energy- related authorities at the federal, 
provincial and local levels. 
 Adopt an energy mix system policy to ensure energy security. 
 Change energy consumption patterns and expand sectors that consume electricity (promote 
electrification).

2.3.2 Policies/Development Plans for Power Transmission 
NEA formulated the Transmission Master Plan for 2015-2035. The Plan proposed a decentralized 
distribution of hydropower plants to transport electricity optimally along  rivers and construction 
of reliable grids for exporting electricity to India.  Despite its emphasis on developing a 
transmission network for electricity exports to India, a new transmission master plan became 
needed to surely respond to the increasing nationwide demand, while promoting electricity exports 
to India and China. 
RPGCL formulated in 2018 the Transmission System Development Plan of Nepal, which 
incorporated a new vision for grid design, while respecting the framework of the past transmission 
plan. Given the forecast of increasing domestic demand, the new Transmission System 
Development Plan proposes construction of a 400 kV east-west trunk line along the hilly areas and 
connection between this trunk line and the stations in such areas, to meet the power generation and 
load scenario as of 2040. This is an addition to the previously proposed 400 kV east -west trunk 
line along the Terai region. Through the development of this new trunk line, mutually -connected 
mesh grids can be formed together with the radial transmission lines along the river areas. It also 
proposes six international transmission lines between the Terai region and India and two 
international transmission lines between the Himalaya region and China. Furthermore, regarding 
the peak load demand, it assumes and considers three scenarios: (1) maximum load in rainy season, 
(2) minimum load in rainy season, and (3) maximum load in dry season. This way, the 
Transmission System Development Plan formulated by RPGCL contains the concept of reliabl e 
grids to realize the following: build complete grids in Nepal; satisfy the country ’s peak load 
demand; and enable electricity exports to neighbouring countries. 
2.3.3 Policies/Development Plans for Rural Electrification 
In 2006, the former Ministry of Environm ent formulated the Rural Energy Policy of Nepal. It 
prescribed the goals and policies to ensure access to clean, reliable and appropriate energy in rural 
areas. 
In 2012, the former Ministry of Population and Environment established the Renewable Energy 
Subsidy Policy, which prescribes specific policies regarding renewable energy subsidies 
envisioned in the Rural Energy Policy. The policy was updated in 2016. It analyses the current 
situation and challenges of the renewable energy sector in Nepal, and prescribes future directions, 
efforts, and the amount of subsidies for renewable energy projects by power source and capacity. 
Annexed to the policy, RE Subsidy Delivery Mechanism and RE Subsidy Delivery Mechanism for 
Special Program were also formulated in the same year.

Final Report Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan 
Annex 2; Current Status of the Power Sector in Nepal  in Nepal 
 
 
2-32 
 
In 2017, the former Ministry of Population and Environment formulated the Biomass Energy 
Strategy, which prescribes goals, policies, and action plans for promoting the use of biomass energy. 
Following the reorganization of ministries and agencies in March 2018, AEPC moved from under 
the Ministry of Science and Technology to under MoEWRI . After that, the above -mentioned 
various renewable energy75 policies including the Rural Energy Policy were centralized under the 
jurisdiction of AEPC. 
On the other hand, as a conventional initiative of AEPC, the NRREP  (National Rural and 
Renewable Energy Programme) was implemented from 2012 to 2017. Through this program, the 
following were implemented: establishment of the Central Renewable Energy Fund, which 
provides subsidies and credit guarantees to the renewable energy sector; technical support for the 
use of renewable energy services in rural areas; and renewable energy -related business 
development. 
In November 2017, NREF (National Renewable Energy Framework) was formulated in the form 
of inheriting NRREP. NREF aims to integrate and organize policies and programs in the field of 
renewable energy in line with the strategic goals set by AEPC, and to integrate the efforts of 
governments, international aid agenc ies and other organizations under a common vision. NREF 
has the following goals in terms of governance, demand, supply, and finance: 
 Governance: Strengthen the policy framework, governance system and public institutions 
needed to promote the development of the renewable energy sector in Nepal. 
 Demand: Increase demand, access and effective use of renewable energy technologies 
(including grid power and off-the-grid) and services; increase demand by micro enterprises 
and SMEs (small and medium-sized enterprises). 
 Supply: Improve the capacity of stakeholders throughout the supply chain to supply high -
quality and competitive renewable energy technologies and services. 
 Finance: Expand funding means for renewable energy products and services on behalf of 
both buyers and sellers through increasing credit acquisition methods, strengthening 
financing mechanisms and enhancing lenders’ capabilities.

(1) Government of Nepal 
The revenue has steadily increased from 0.97 trillion rupees (2019/2020) to 1.15 trillion rupees 
(2021/2022) and 1.43 trillion rupees (2022/2023). Meanwhile, the expenditure has also increased 
from 1.07 trillion rupees (2019/2020) to 1.27 trillion rupees (2021/2022) and 1.56 trillion rupees 
(2022/2023). As a result, although there was a slight reduction in the budget deficit in 2021/2022, 
it grew again in 2022/2023. Out of the total income (1.79 trillion rupees), 0.29 trillion rupees came 
outside of Nepal (loans of 0.24 trillion rupees, grants of 0.05 trillion rupees). This means that Nepal 
derives about 16% of its total income abroad.

The above table is not a cash flow statement, so it does not accurately reflect all movements of 
cash and cash equivalents, but although cash flow from operating activities and financing activities 
are generally positive, cash flow to investment activities, especially capital investment, is negative. 
Vigorous investment activity is presumed to have had an impact on the decline in cash and cash 
equivalents. 
In addition, the profit and loss statement displays the gross profit decreased, although sales revenue 
in 2023 increased compared to 2022. This is th ought to be due to the high rate of increase in the 
cost of electricity from IPP and India compared to the rate of increase in sales. This is due to the 
fact that the unit price of electricity sold has not changed and the amount of electricity sold has 
increased by about 14%, while the amount of electricity purchased from IPP has increased by about 
19% and the unit price of electricity purchased by about 14%, and the amount of electricity 
imported from India has increased by about 18% and the unit price of impor ts has increased by 
about 6%, resulting in a large increase in the cost of goods sold and a smaller profit margin.

2.4.2 E lectricity Tariff System 
(1) Laws and Regulations Concerning Electricity Tariff System 
The major legislation governing the electricity tariff system in Nepal is the Electricity Regulation 
Commission (ERC) Act 2017, the Electricity Regulatory Commission Rules ( hereinafter “ERC 
Rules”) 2018,  and the Electricity Consumer Tariff Fixation Directi ve (hereinafter “ECTF 
Directive”) 2076. The Article 13 of the ERCA 2017 stipulates the role of the Electricity Regulation 
Commission in electricity tariffs and the purchase and sale of electricity. The ERC Rules 2018 is 
the basic regulation established by the Government of Nepal under Section 41 of the ERC Act  
2017. Regulations 8 and 9 provide for the basic regulations on electricity tariffs . The ECTF 
Directive 2076 (AD 2019) is a directive established by the ERC under Section 43 of the ERC Act 
2017 for the purpose of setting electricity tariffs in accordance with Section 13 of the Act and Rules 
8 and 9 of the ERC Rules 2018.

Table 2.4-11  Major Laws and Regulations Governing the Electricity Tariff System in Nepal 
Laws and regulations Description 
Electricity Regulation 
Commission Act 2017 
・ The act on the basis, role, obligation, authority , etc. of ERC. 
・ Article 13 defines the role of ERC in electricity tariffs and the purchase and sale of 
electricity . 
Electricity Regulatory 
Commission Rules 2018 
・ The basic regulations established by the Government of Nepal under Section 41 of 
the ERCA 2017.

Electricity Consumer 
Tariff Fixation Directive 
2076 
・ Directive issued by ERC under section 43 of the ERC Act  2017 to establish

and Chapter 3 provides the rules for the principles and grounds for setting tariffs. 
Source: Electricity Regulation Commission Act 2017, Electricity Regulatory Commission Rules 2018, 
Electricity Consumer Tariff Fixation Directive 2076 
Chapter 4 of the ERC Rules  2018 stipulates the basic rules on electricity tariffs . The ERC 
determines electricity tariffs based on factors such as the generation cost and the rate of return 
(Chapter 4, 9) upon application from a distribution licensee (Chapter 4, 8). The ERC  can also set 
prices for electricity transactions in the wholesale electricity market, offering the option of a two-
part tariff system consisting of capacity charges 83 for loan investments and energy charges for 
equity, returns, tax, royalty, operation and maintenance costs (Chapter 4, 10). It is also allowed to 
set electricity tariffs between the power producer and purchasing entities, but in doing so, it is 
required to obtain the consent of the Commission and to maintain uniformity in ele ctricity tariffs 
agreed with similar industries or entities (Chapter 4, 11). 
 
Table 2.4-12  Chapter 4 of the Electricity Regulatory Commission Rules 2018 (excerpt)  
Chapter 4  Tariff and Charges 
8. Submission of 
Application for 
Fixation of Electricity 
Tariff 
(1) A Distribution licensee shall have to submit an application to the Commission, mentioning the criteria referred 
to in Clause (a) of Sub -Section (1) of Section 13 of the Act for fixation of tariff prior to distributing electricity 
to the consumers. 
(2) The Commission may, if deems necessary while examining the application submitted pursuant to Sub -Rule (1), 
solicit additional details and documents from the distribution licensee and it shall be the duty of the dis tribution 
licensee to provide the details and documents so solicited.  
(3) The Commission shall, on the basis of the application submitted pursuant to Sub- Rule (1) and also the details 
and documents referred to in Sub-Rule (2), fix the tariff to be paid by the consumers pursuant to Clause (a) of 
Sub-Section (1) of Section 13 of the Act. 
(4) The format of the application to submitted pursuant to Sub-Rule (1) shall be as determined by the Commission.  
(5) Notwithstanding anything contained in these Rules, there shall be no bar for the Commission to fix the electricity 
tariff merely because application from the distribution licensee has not been received.  
9. Criteria for Fixation of 
Electricity Tariff 
(1) While fixing the electricity tariff to be paid by the consumers pursuant to Rule 8, the Commission shall fix on 
the following basis: 
(a) Operation cost of electricity system or production mechanism,  
(b) Depreciation rate, 
(c) Repayment of principal and interest,  
(d) Maintenance cost, 
(e) Mode of construction and operation of electricity structures , 
(f) Reconstruction and rehabilitation and returns to be received on an annual basis in consideration of share 
investment, 
(g) Power Purchase Agreement, 
(h) Changes in consumers’ price index, 
(i) Revenue rate and changes therein,  
(j) Policy adopted by the Government of Nepal  in relation to electricity development,  
(k) Other criteria deemed appropriate by the Commission.  
(2) The Commission shall, also take the marginal cost of the electricity generation, the exchange rate of convertible

2-44 
 
foreign currency, cost of the fuel to be used for the production of electricity and the financial agreement entered 
between the licensee and the financial institution providing loan or investing capital in the concerned electricity 
project as the base while fixing electricity tariff pursuant to this Rule.  
Provided, however, that while fixing the tariff and other fees/charges of electricity to be consumed by the 
Government of Nepal itself or a corporate body with more than fifty percent of share capital of the Government 
of Nepal, the Commission shall fix  the tariff and other fees/charges in such a way as to abide by the agreement 
entered into by the Government of Nepal or by that corporate body with the financial institutions in relation to 
the concerned electricity project.  
(3) The Commission may develop and implement necessary standards in relation to the fixation of electricity tariff. 
10. Fixation of Power 
Purchase/Sales Rate 
(1) The Commission may, pursuant to Clause (b) of Sub-Section (1) of Section 12 of the Act, develop and implement 
necessary standards for the purpose of fixation of power purchase/sales rate between the persons obtaining 
distribution license and the persons obtaining generation license or persons obtaining trade license or a corporate 
body established by the Government of Nepal under the  prevailing law. 
(2) The Commission may, while fixing power purchase/sales rate, also bring into use the two -part tariff system - 
Capacity Charge incorporating loan investment made in the project and Energy Charge incorporating equity, 
returns, tax, royalty, operation and maintenance cost  
(3) The Commission may, while fixing power purchase/sales rate, adopt Availability Based Capacity Charge to 
make the electricity producers responsible by maintaining such a condition in which they are able to produce 
electricity at their rated capacity. 
(4) While bringing into use the Capacity Charge pursuant to Sub -Rule (3), the Commission shall make a provision 
in such a way that the generation licensee is entitled to full Capacity Charge where 100% (cent percent) 
availability is ach ieved as per the standards prescribed by the Commission and enjoy less Capacity Charge 
accordingly in proportion to the degree of availability achieved in case of achievement of less availability.  
(5) While implementing Capacity Charge system pursuant to Sub-Rule (4), Capacity Charge shall not be fixed in 
the case of a project which has not borrowed loans or or has already repaid the loans.  
(6) While fixing power purchase/sales rate pursuant to this Rule, matters like loan interest, depreciation or 
accumulated depreciation, investment returns, general expenses, operation cost, maintenance cost, revenue, tax, 
additional capitalization, loan and equity ratio shall be taken as the base.  
11. Rate May be Fixed on 
Mutual Understanding 
(1) In a case where an agreement has bee n entered into between the generation licensee and electricity consuming 
industry or institution as to purchase/sell electricity, the generation licensee and such an industry or institution 
may specify power purchase/sales rate on a mutual understanding by  obtaining consent of the Commission.  
(2) While specifying power purchase/sales rate pursuant to Sub -Rule (1), it shall be done as to be maintained 
uniformity with the power purchase/sales rate agreed with other industries or institutions of similar types.  
(3) While specifying power purchase/sales rate pursuant to Sub-Rule (1), it shall not be done in collusion with each 
other in a manner to have adverse impacts on the consumers.  
12. Terms and Conditions 
of the Agreement to be 
Submitted 
The licensed persons sha ll, to conclude an agreement for power purchase/sales pursuant to Clause (c) of Sub-
Section (1) of Section 13 of the Act, have to submit also the terms and conditions of the agreement while making 
an application for the consent of the Commission.  
13. Electricity 
Transmission Charges 
(1) The Commission may, while fixing electricity transmission charges pursuant to Clause (e) of Sub -Section (1) 
of Section 13 of the Act, take all or any of the following matters as the base in addition to the criteria to be 
followed for fixation of purchase/sales rate as referred to in Rule 10:  
(a) Consumption capacity of the users or amount and distance,  
(b) Point of Connection, 
(c) Zonal Transmission Charges, 
(d) Investment to be made for the construction or extension of transmission line.  
(2) While fixing electricity transmission charges pursuant to Sub- Rule (1), separate charges may be fixed for 
National Transmission Grid and other transmission line sections.  
(3) Other criteria relating to fixation of electricity transmission charges shall be as  determined by the Commission.  
14. To Cause to Be 
Competed in Tariff 
Rate of Electricity 
Distribution 
(1) The Commission shall make a provision for competition in tariff rate of electricity distribution in such a way 
that the consumers or the proxies of the consumers are permitted to select their service provider.  
(2) Other provisions relating to making competition in tariff rate of electricity distribution and selecting electricity 
distributor shall be as determined by the Commission.  
15. Electricity 
Distribution Charges 
Where any other institutions distribute electricity to the consumers by using the system of an institution licensed 
for electricity distribution, the distribution charges/fees to be paid in consideration of using such a distribution 
system shall be fixed by the Commission. 
16 To Regulate Trading 
Margin 
The Commission shall develop and implement the electricity trade standards to carry out regulation in relation to 
trading margin of electricity trade activities and resale by purchasing electricity between the persons licensed under 
this Rule. 
17. Service Charges May 
be Prescribed 
(1) The Commission may, pursuant to Section 20 of the Act, impose the following service charges/fees on the 
following matters: 
(a) Seven Thousand Rupees while fixing wholesale purchase/sales rate of electricity,  
(b) It shall be as follows while giving consent for power purchase agreement:  
1) One Hundred Thousand Rupees for 100 kilowatt to 1 megawatt,  
2) At the rate of Ten Thousand Rupees per megawatt from above 1 megawatt to up to 1000 me gawatt, 
and 
3) Two Million Rupees in lump sum for above that.

(c) Twenty-Five Thousand Rupees while fixing wheeling charge including any other tariffs or charges of 
similar kinds. 
(d) While giving consent for merger between the licensees, sale of plant, acquisition o r takeover, One 
Hundred Thousand Rupees for base price of up to 100 Million Rupees on the basis of base price of such 
structure or institution and Fifty Thousand Rupees for each up to 100 Million Rupees above that,  
(e) While settling a dispute pursuant to Sect ion 18 of the Act, Five percent of such a claimed price if the

unidentified. 
(2) While imposing service charges/fees pursuant to Sub -Rule (1), the Commission shall make public the matte rs 
on which service charges/fees are imposed and the names of licensed persons or institutions through its website

Chapter 2 of the ECTF Directive 2076 provides procedures for the application and actions for tariff 
fixation and requires electricity distribution licensees to submit the documents listed in the 
Attachment 2 in addition to the application form specified in the Attachment 1 when applying for 
tariff fixation (Chapter 2-3). Chapter 3 of the Directive stipulates rules on the principle and basis 
of setting tariffs, and that the ERC shall set rates after examining the details of the annual revenue 
requirements of licensees (Chapter 3-6). 
 
Table 2.4-13  Chapters  2 and 3 of Electricity Consumer Tariff Fixation Directive 2076 (excerpt) 
Chapter 2 Procedure Relating to Tariff Fixation Application and Action 
3. Application for 
Tariff Fixation 
1) The Distribution Licensee shall submit an application in the format prescribed in schedule-1 of Directives along 
with Rs. 25,000/- for the purpose of tariff fixation or review. 
2) The Documents to be submitted along with the application of tariff fixation shall b e as prescribed in schedule 
2. The Commission, if deem necessary, can request to submit additional documents, particulars or reports.  
3) The application of tariff fixation shall be deemed submitted only if upon submission of all necessary documents, 
particulars and information as stated in Directives by the Distribution Licensee to the Commission.  
4) The Commission, on the receipt of an application submitted by the Distribution Licensee for the fixation of 
tariff, shall approve the application no later than 7 day s and provide application number. The date of issuance 
of application number shall deem to be the date of submission of the application.  
4. Proceedings on 
Application for 
Fixation of Tariff 
1) Public offering and proceedings on application for fixation of tariff shall be as specified in the Act and 
Regulation. 
2) The information about the proposed tariff made on the application for fixation of tariff will be published, for at 
least 2 times, in a national daily newspaper within 7 days from the date of issuing a pplication number. Such 
notice shall include website link where the application for tariff fixation is kept and illustrate the ways of 
commenting on application and the deadline for filing comments.  
3) The Commission, at least one time, shall make public offering about the proceedings on application for tariff 
fixation and shall give minimum of 2 weeks’ time period to the concerned party for submitting any response in 
relation to the application. 
4) For the purpose of public offering and response from the concerned party related to an application, all collected 
documents, particulars and application for tariff fixation shall be kept in the website of the Commission.  
5) The Commission, while carrying out public offering upon the application for tariff fixation, may ca use to 
carryout discussion on different aspects of the application.  
Chapter 3 The Principle and Basis of Tariff Fixation 
5. Directives Principle 1) The Commission shall carry out the proceedings on an application for tariff fixation in accordance to the 
provisions laid down by the Act and Regulation along with the directive principles stated below:  
a) Protection of Consumers rights,  
b) Ensure quality of electricity supply, 
c) Increment in the professionalism and effectiveness of Distribution Licensee,  
d) Necessary mobilization of future investment source for improvement in the system,  
e) Justified consideration to the investment made by the Distribution Licensee.  
6. Basis of Tariff 
Fixation 
1) The Commission shall determine tariff on following basis: 
a) Audit report of last two fiscal years, projected financial details of current year and of upcoming year of 
Distribution Licensee. 
 In the absence of audit report of last fiscal year of Distribution Licensee, the certified financial statement 
by the management of Distribution Licensee may be give taken into consideration.

b) In the year when electricity tariff is to be determined, Commission shall carry out detailed examination of 
annual revenue requirement, inclusive of following estimation and various income and expenditure heads, 
in financial details of that 
I. Income generated from selling to individual Classification of Consumer (Classification 
inclusive of Capacity Demand Charge and energy charge),  
II. Annual consideration gained from the investment,  
III. Income generated from other sources, 
IV. Outcome and cost of electricity as per source of electricity,  
V. Annual expenditure of electricity production plant (except the depreciation and employee 
expenses) 
VI. Annual expenditure of electricity transmission system (except the depreciation and employe e 
expenses) 
VII. Annual expenditure of electricity distribution system (except the depreciation and employee 
expenses) 
VIII. Rate of depreciation and amount, 
IX. Payment of Interest amount, 
X. Employee expenses, 
XI. Annual expenses incurred from main or regional office,  
XII. Other expenses amount, 
XIII. Expected annual consideration amount.  
2) The Commission while fixing tariff, except stated in Sub - section 1, may also consider following basis:  
a) National financial indexes and policy adopted in relation to the development of electricity by the 
Government of Nepal as well as tariff fixed in the past,  
b) Incorporation of income of Distribution Licensee in following years to limit applications for excess 
increment in tariff made by Distribution Licensee,  
c) Consideration of undersupply received by Distribution Licensee while limiting excess increment in the tariff 
of subscribed customers and other customers, and consideration of undersupply that may be created for the 
customer by determination of tariff for such customers.  
d) If the tariff for the fiscal year is fixed after the fiscal year had already started and if such tariff could not be 
applied for all twelve years by the Distribution Licensee, incorporation of the balance amount required to 
meet annual revenue requirement as regulatory assets in follow ing fiscal year 
7. New Tariff to be 
Applicable 
1) Unless new tariff is determined by the Commission in accordance to this Directive, existing tariff shall continue 
to apply. 
2) The Commission shall designate the date for tariff implementation while determining the tariff as per this 
Directive and Distribution Licensee shall apply such tariff while issuing bills to consumer after the date for 
tariff implementation. 
3) Unless determined or amended by the Commission, the latest tariff rate fixed by the Commission shall continue 
to exist. 
8. Publication of Tariff The tariff determined by the Commission in accordance to this Directive shall be published for general public 
information, in at least two national daily newspapers by the Commission and the Distribution Licen see.

To sum up, the tariff should  be determined by taking into consideration the costs of generating, 
transmitting and distributing, and the fair value of distribution licensees. It can be understood that 
the electricity tariff system in Nepal is basically based on the cost accumulation method, but 
specific calculation formulas are not included in these laws and regulations. In particu lar, it is 
necessary to confirm in more detail with local authorities how the appropriate compensation and 
demand tariffs of electric power companies are set.

(2) Electricity Tariff Structure of NEA 
The following subsections look at the current tariff structure of NEA for retail and transmission. 
1) Retail Electricity Tariff 
The retail electricity tariff system of NEA consists of five main tariff types: d omestic 
consumers, other consumers, time -of-day, t ransportation without demand charge, and 
community wholesale consumers. 
Three categories of tariffs exist for domestic  consumers: single-phase low pressure (230 V), 
three-phase low pressure (400 V), and three -phase medium pressure (33/11 kV). Minimum 
charges and energy charges are set according to the amount of electricity used.

2.5 ELECTRICITY DEMAND AND SUPPLY RESULT 
2.5.1 Electricity Consumption 
Electricity sales as electricity consumption are shown in Figure 2.5-1. Electricity sales are on the 
rise. Over the past 12 years, electricity sales have increased by an average of 10%/year. In 2018, 
the electricity sales growth rate increased by approximately 15 %/year, but this growth rate has 
been a downward trend from 2019, in 2020 the electricity sales growth rate decreased significantly 
to 3%. In 2020, the spread of COVID -19 would have limited socioeconomic activities and kept 
electricity consumption low. 
 
 
Source: Prepared by JICA Study Team based on Annual Report of Nepal Electricity Authority 2022/2023 
Figure 2.5-1 Electricity Sales 
2.5.2 Peak Demand 
The peak demand is shown in Figure 2.5-2. The peak demand is increasing. In 2019, as shown in 
Figure 2.5-2, the peak demand decreased. This decrease in peak demand can be attributed to the 
following causes. 
・ Increased imports from India, energy efficiency  policies (e.g., LED lighting), and electricity 
anti-theft campaigns to reduce system losses have reduce d the frequency of interruption and 
made it unnecessary to charge backup storage batteries. 
・ Increased load factor due to the removal of load shedding in 2018. 
After 2020, the peak demand began to increase again. 
 
 
Source: Prepared by JICA Study Team based on Annual Report of Nepal Electricity Authority 2022/2023 
Figure 2.5-2  Peak Demand

Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan  Final Report 
in Nepal Annex 2; Current Status of the Power Sector in Nepal 
 
 
2-53 
 
The peak demand growth rate is shown in Figure 2.5-3. The peak demand growth rate has steadily 
increased at around 5 to 10% from 2011 to 2018. In 2019, the peak demand growth rate was -15% 
but recovered to +6.3% in 2020. 
 
 
Source: Prepared by JICA Study Team based on Annual Report of Nepal Electricity Authority 2022/2023 
Figure 2.5-3  Peak Demand Growth Rate 
2.5.3 Power Interruption 
The power interruption is shown in Figure 2.5-4. The power interruption has been published since 
Annual Report 2018/2019. Since 2018, the power interruption has occurred 20-240GWh/year. The 
power interruption was caused by system fault, not planned interruption. 
 
 
Source: Prepared by JICA Study Team based on Annual Report of Nepal Electricity Authority 2018/2019 ~ 2022/2023 
Figure 2.5-4  Power Interruption 
2.5.4 System Loss 
The system loss is shown in Figure 2.5-5. The system loss is downward as NEA’s loss reduction 
efforts show results. The transmission line loss remained constant at around 5% , suggesting that 
the distribution loss mainly decreased. Examples of efforts to reduce the distribution loss are shown 
below. 
(1) Replacement of electromechanical meters, with smart digital meters 
(2) Upgrading of overloaded conductors and transformers 
(3) Action was taken for electricity pilferage (Theft control public awareness media campaign)

Final Report Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan

Despite the above measures, t he distribution loss in 2021 is 11.64%, higher than the 10.28% in 
2020. The continued lockdown has hampered regular meter readings as well as proper management 
of loss control. 
 
 
Source: Prepared by JICA Study Team based on Annual Report of Nepal Electricity Authority 2020/2021

2.6 FINANCING AND PRIVATE INVESTMENT IN THE POWER SECTOR 
According to the World Bank estimates 86 , Nepal’s power sector will need to invest an annual 
average of USD 1.3 - 2.1 billion between 2018 and 2040 to meet growing demand. In addition, 
USD 500 million - 1 billion may be require d annually for large -scale hydropower projects for 
export. The following sections  summarize financing of NEA, which is responsible for power 
generation, transmission, and distribution, and IPPs. 
2.6.1 NEA 
The NEA expects to continue to make huge investments, muc h of which will be financed by 
borrowing from multilateral development banks and domestic financial institutions. NEA plans to 
invest a total of NPR  426,814,000,000 over 5 years from 2019/20 to 2023/24 (see  Figure 2.6-1). 
The breakdown is NPR  134 billion for power generation, NPR  156 billion for transmission and 
NPR 135 billion for distribution, each account for roughly one-third. 
 
 
Source: Prepared by JICA Study Team from NEA “Corporate Development Plan 2019/20 -  2023/24” 
Figure 2.6-1  NEA’s Five-Year Investment Plan (2019/20-23/24) 
For investments in power generation, transmission and distribution, the plan is to finance  70 to 
80% by borrowing from multilateral and domestic development banks (see Figure 2.6-2). For run-
of-river and peaking-run-of-river projects by subsidiaries, the plan is to raise about 10% from the 
public through the stock market. Projects by NEA’s generation subsidiaries raise at least one-fourth 
of their shares from local communities, employees of sponsors (investors) or the public through 
IPOs (initial public offerings). As shown in Table 2.1-4, a similar trend is observed with regard to 
SPCs under VUCL. This reflects the policy of “People in Nepal invest in Nepal’s water resources 
and make all Nepalese shareholders of hydropower business” stated in the 2018 White Paper.

As can be seen from the fact that 17 of the 22 projects are 50 MW or less with total investments in 
the millions or tens of millions of USD dollars, many of the IPPs are small to medium hydro of 
run-of-river or peak -run-of-river type. In addition, despite the absence of foreign investment 
restrictions in the power sector, as mentioned in 2.1.2 (4), most of the IPPs are locally owned, with 
limited projects involving foreign investors, and most of the foreign capital is also from India and 
China. High risks, such as country and foreign exchange risks, partly contribute to this. 
However, the development of large reservoir type hydropower plants will be important in the future, 
and the need for large-scale financing, including foreign capital, will increase. IPP income is based 
on a take-or-pay PPA with NEA, the only off-taker. For the past three years, PPA for run-of-river 
type has been frozen because while most applications for PPA are tun- of-river type, maximum 
capacity set by the governments for this type has been reached. As a result, 9,000 MW is waiting 
for PPA. But that is showing signs of improving. In June 2022, a review  of the upper capacity 
limits for each type of power generation was conducted, and it was decided that the ratio of run-
of-river type would be increased from 35% to 45%, while the ratio of reservoir type would be 
reduced from 35% to 25% of the target of 15,000 MW of new construction over the next 10 years. 
The remaining 30% is peaking run-of-river type.90 With this, development of small- and medium-
sized hydropower run-of-river and peaking run-of-river hydropower is also expected to increase, 
but the need for stable development of reservoir hydropower continues to be great. 
Funding in hydro IPPs in Nepal generally consist of 70% debt and 30% equity91. In fact, from 2010 
to 2018, IPPs financed projects totalling more than 406 MW and USD 700 million, with about one-
third in equity and the remaining two-thirds in loans from international aid agencies and domestic 
and international financial institutions92. 
First, with regard to debt, borrowing from domestic banks is common. Nepal has 26 commercial 
banks (Class A) that provide loans in local currency to IPP operators
93. Strictly speaking, however, 
it can’t be called project finance because it requires a personal guarantee94. 
The Central Bank of Nepal requires commercial banks to gradually increase the energy sector ’s 
share of their loan portfolios to more than 10% by 2023/24 95. As of 2021, the electricity, gas and 
water sectors accounted for 5.45% of commercial banks’ outstanding loans96. NMB Bank, which 
is also funded by the Dutch Development Finance Corporation, plans to raise USD 15 million from 
the OPEC Fund for International Development in 2021 to expand its financing of renewable energy 
projects, particularly hydropower97.  
According to an interview with IPPAN, the interest rate on loans for hydro IPPs is 12.5% 98. The 
average base interest rate at commercial and development banks was 7.23% in 2020/21, a 
significant drop from 2019/20 (9.28%) and 2018/19 (9.86%) as a result of reduced borrowing 
demand due to the pandemic99. 
To date, there have been no particular problems for domestic IPPs in obtaining loans for small and 
medium-sized hydropower projects100, but as they promote larger projects in the future, they may

to lend more than USD 40 million on their own101. In addition, many commercial banks have high 
deposit-credit ratios102 and are reluctant to lend new money. The Central Bank has asked domestic 
financial institutions to keep the ratio below 90%, but as of June 2022, half of commercial banks 
had failed to do so103. 
On the other hand, due to the underdeveloped bond market, bond financing has not been commonly 
used. The government issues long -term development bonds, which are traded on the NEPSE 
(Nepal Stock Exchange) but are rarely traded104.  
As mentioned above, domestic institutional investors are active in financing and investi ng in 
hydropower projects. Given that the total size of institutional assets is more than 15% of GDP 105, 
IPP hydropower projects are also expected to mobilize funds from these sources.  
In addition, there are ways to raise equity through the market through IPOs. Numerous hydropower 
projects are publicly listed in Nepal, and 60 hydropower IPPs were listed on the NEPSE as of 
October 2022 106. IPP shares are also heavily bought by individuals, who are no less willing to 
invest during the pandemic, with many IPPs oversubscribed by 5 to 10 times107. 
Under Rule 9 (4) of the Securities Registration and Issue Rules, 2073 and Rule 4 (2) of the 
Securities Issue and Allocation Directives, 2074, if a hydro IPP undertakes an IPO, it must issue 
up to 10% of its outstanding shares to people living in areas affected by the project. Under Rule 9 
(1) of the former, the public offering is limited to 10-49% of the outstanding shares. SPC sponsors 
are prohibited from selling their holdings for the first three years, and SPC directors are prohibited 
from selling their holdings for the first year after leaving office108.

2.7 ASSISTANCES FROM EACH DEVELOPMENT ASSISTANCE AGENCY IN THE POWER 
SECTOR 
Many Development Aid Agencies  have conducted  various development surveys, technical 
assistances and project construction for the Nepalese power sector. The main development partners 
in Nepalese power sector are the Asian Development Bank (ADB), the World Bank (WB), the 
United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), the Japan International Cooperation Age ncy 
(JICA), the European Commission (EC), the European Investment Bank (EIB), the Danish 
Government, the Norwegian Government, the German Development Bank (KfW), and the 
Netherlands Development Organisation (SNV).  Among these, the WB, ADB, JICA, KfW and 
Norway government are the most active in the on-grid sub-sector. Other development partners are 
more focus on off-grid power development. Development partners are regularly coordinating with 
each other regarding sector investment and support to establish the  institutional and regulatory 
frameworks that enable these investments. 
 
However, regarding development plans, there are the following issues, and sufficient results have 
not been achieved, resulting in the development plans not functioning effectively. 
 
① Lack of an integrated power development plan 
② Weak mutual collaboration among C/P agencies 
③ Lack of efforts towards implementation promotion. 
The WB and ADB are jointly supporting the formulation of power distribution and rural  
electrification master plan development and the development of power trading strategies. They are 
also collaborating with Millennium Challenge Corporation (MCC) and USAID to support capacity 
building in power sector regulation and power trading.  However, in Nepal, power sector -related 
agencies are noted for overlapping functions, unclear authorities, and multifaceted roles. 
Additionally, the World Bank and the Department for International Development  (DfID) jointly 
supported the Investment Board Nepal (IBN) in reviewing a nd negotiating project development 
agreements (PDAs) for four (4) exported oriented large-scale hydropower projects . As a result, 
agreements were signed for Upper Karnali (900 MW) and Arun 3 (900 MW). The WB, ADB, and 
DfID jointly support policy dialogue a nd capacity building for regional integration, and a Power 
Trade Agreement (PTA) was signed between India and Nepal. 
The recent support areas of various international aid agencies in the Nepalese power sector are 
summarized in  Table 2.7-1. Notably, USAID's Urja Nepal Program, under the initiative of 
MoEWRI, has established working groups for integrated planning through an Integrated Resource 
and Resilience Planning (IRRP) approach. These working groups are designed to facilitate the 
coordination and cooperation of various power sector agencies in developing integrated plans, and 
to continue providing ongoing support. It has been found that the support areas, approach methods, 
and C/P agencies often overlap and share commonalities with JICA projects. Therefore, 
information sharing and exchange have been conducted to discuss the possibilities and necessity 
of coordination. The WB and USAID are also coordinating the scope of their support.

2.7.1 W orld Bank (WB) 
(1) Power Sector Reform and Sustainable Hydropower Development Project (PSRSHD) 
The objectives of this project are (a) to strengthen the capacity of power sector institutions to plan 
and formulate hydropower and transmission line projects in accordance with international 
standards and best practices, and (b) to improve the preparednes s of power sector institutions for 
regulatory and organizational reforms. The implementing agencies in Nepal are NEA and WECS. 
PSRSHD comprises the following three (3) components.  
Component A:  Development of investment plans for hydropower and transmission lines  
(18 million USD) 
Component B:  Policy recommendations and Review and formulation of sector reform  
(3.9 million USD) 
Component C:  Capacity development for safeguard management and hydropower development 
(0.1 million USD) 
 
(Source: Combined Project Information Documents /Integrated Safeguards Datasheet (PID/ISDS),  
Power Sector Reform and Sustainable Hydropower Development (PSRSHD) Projec t, December, 2020, WB)

(2) Preparation of River Basin Plans and Hydropower Development Master Plans and 
Strategic Environmental and Social Assessment 
1) Study Background 
This project is included in Component B of the Nepal Power Sector Reform and Sustainable 
Hydropower Development Project (PSRSHD): Policy recommendations and formulation of 
power sector reforms. In June 2018, the joint venture of Tractabel Engineering GMBH 
(Germany) and CSPDR (Changjiang Institute of Survey, Planning, Design and Research, 
China) was awarded the contract in collaboration with Total Management Services Ovt. Ltd. 
(Nepal).  
2) Objectives of the Study  
The Objectives of the Study are as follows: 
a) To prepare river basin plans through integrated water resources management principles 
for all rivers. (except Bagmati River Basin)  
b) To prepare hydropower development master plan for all the major river basins of Nepal. 
c) To concurrently undertake an integrated Strategic Environmental and Social Assessment 
(SESA) of the river basin and hydropower development master plan and 
d) To carry out integrated water resources development and management planning at basin 
level to meet local, regional and national needs utilizing appropriate knowledge and 
information management system, analytical and modeling tools, and planning 
methodologies. 
3) Implementation Organization of the Study 
To implement PSRSHDP , the Government of Nepal established a Project Steering Committee 
(PSC) chaired by the Secretary of Energy, including members from the Ministry of Finance 
(MoF), D oED, WECS, IBN, NEA, EFTC, and AEPC. WECS also established a Project 
Coordination Committee (PCC) chaired by the responsible Secretary of WECS, including the 
directors of DOI, DoED , and 
Department of Water Supply and Sewerage Management  
(DWSSM), the Deputy Secretary of M oE, and the President of NEA, to coordinate and 
cooperate with relevant institutions. The PCC provides overall guidance for the river basin 
planning proc ess, including the strategic environmental and social assessment (SESA)
110 . 
Within WECS, a Project Management Unit (PMU) headed by the project manager was 
established to support the project. 
4) Scope of the Study 
To achieve the above objectives, the following scope is implemented. The master plan will be 
formulated up to 2050. 
A) Preparation of River Basin Plan 
Task-1: Baseline context, mapping and analysis of the river basin 
Task-2: Stakeholder analysis and engagement 
Task-3: Decision Support System

5) Project Outcomes and Information 
The river basin plans and hydropower development master plans duplicate many scopes with 
the IPSDP study, with WECS as the C/P agency, and the members of various committees are 
almost the same. This project preceded the IPSDP study, requiring information collection. In 
February 2023, a meeting with Tractabel's PM was held to hear about the progress, and in April 
of the same year, WECS  provided the Draft Hydropower Development Master Plan Report 
(HPDMP) as a reference. However, since WECS has not approved the Final Report, the results 
of the report could not be incorporated. The River Basin Plans Report and the Strategic 
Environmental and Social Assessment report have not been shared. 
A) Overview of the Hydropower Development Master Plan   
As a prerequisite for formulating the hydropower development master plan, at the project 
start (January 2019), WECS and the contracted consultants agreed that the selection method 
for hydropower projects would be based on the analysis of new developable gr eenfield 
projects rather than previously identified hydropower sites, and that projects with a 
Generation/Construction License issued by the Government of Nepal would be selected as 
locked projects without additional analysis. Subsequently, in February 2020, WECS 
confirmed the investigation of promising new reservoir-type hydropower projects in basins 
currently occupied by small-scale locked projects and that these projects need to have the 
economic feasibility to absorb the costs of the locked projects. The Consultant identified 
hydropower potential sites in each river basin, conducting technical, economic, and 
environmental-social analyses and evaluations.  
To formulate the hydropower development master plan, a GIS database including various 
information such as topography, geology, seismology, hydrology, and environmental-social 
aspects was established , adding location information of existing hydropower, locked 
projects, greenfield projects, and hydropower projects of national importance. Greenfield

in Nepal Annex 2; Current Status of the Power Sector in Nepal 
 
 
2-63 
 
projects were considered at the Pre- F/S level in desk study . During the study , three (3) 
pumped storage candidate sites were identified.  
Economic and financial benefits were evaluated for each hydropower project under 
domestic supply scenarios, domestic supply and power export scenarios, and power export 
scenarios. The economic viability of each hydropower project was assessed by EIRR. 
However, hydropower project s with an installed capacity of 100 MW or more were 
considered under the power export scenario.  
A five-year hydropower development plan was formulated up to 2050 for the base case of 
power demand (15th Plan (2019) by the Nepalese Planning Commission) and two (2) 
alternative cases. The alternative cases were set considering the potential effects of recent 
international crises and the impact on international trade and energy markets.  
Considering that hydropower projects having the issuance of licenses or under investigation 
may not proceed as originally scheduled and may not be implemented for various reasons, 
it is desirable that the optimal hydropower development plan for the basin, including new 
developable greenfield project sites considered in this study , be utilized in the future to 
maximize the utilization of hydropower potential.  
As of May 2024, the  committee has approved it, and it is expected that the Secretary of 
WECS will sign it. Th is study schedule has been delayed by about three (3) years due to 
the impact of COVID-19, and the Final Report of the hydropower development master plan 
has not been obtained at this time, making it difficult to incorporate the results into IPSDP 
at present. 
(Source: Draft Hydropower Development Master Plan Report, July 2022, WECS)  
(3) Nepal Energy Sector Development Policy Credit Project 
The objective of this project is to provide financing to strengthen the finances and operations of 
the energy sector and support the GoN in improving the financial viability and governance of the 
power sector. This includes improving the financial capacity  of NEA, the sole off -taker, 
establishing a regulatory framework, promoting power trading, and restructuring NEA to enable 
private sector participation in power projects. The first loan of $100 million USD was disbursed in 
September 2018, the second loan o f $100 million USD in June 2020, and the third loan of $150 
million USD in May 2022. The repayment period is 40 years.  
(Source: WB project documents of Energy Sector Development Policy Credit)  
(4) Nepal Infrastructure Sector Assessment, April 2019 
The purpose of this report is to identify ways to maximize the funds available to Nepal in the power 
sector. It outlines a roadmap to identify short - and medium-term financing needs and constraints 
in the power sector and to overcome these constraints to achieve sectoral transformation gradually. 
Nepalese power sector requires new sources of funding from public and private sources, both 
domestic and international capital markets, and investors. Public sector entities and foreign 
investors, with the support of multilateral development agencies, can play a strong role in realizing 
large-scale hydropower projects. There is also potential for private investment in transmission. 
To meet the financing needs of the power sector, coordinated policy efforts in four areas are 
necessary: 
Pillar 1: Establishing an institutional and regulatory environment. 
Pillar 2: Strengthening the financial viability of the sector. 
Pillar 3: Enhancing the availability of long-term financing. 
Pillar 4: Creating an environment conducive to foreign investment.

2.7.2 United States Agency for International Development (USAID) 
(1) Urja Nepal Program (Nepal Energy Program) 
1) Study Background 
Nepal has historically experienced energy shortage s, which has led to a lack of emphasis on 
least-cost generation planning or diversifying the energy mix. Future plans must focus on 
optimizing various accessible hydropower (run -of-the-river, peaking run -of-the-river, 
reservoir-type, pumped storage), solar, biomass generation, power imports, and new 
technologies such as Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) and hydrogen power generation. 
Additionally, energy transition initiatives like electric vehicles, e -cooking, and energy 
efficiency measures could alter the demand characteristics of Nepalese energy sector.  
2) Objectives of the Study 
The Urja  Nepal Program aims to support the development of an Integrated Resource and 
Resilience Planning (IRRP) approach in Nepal to improve long -term power planning and 
enhance the resilience of the power system against potential risks and uncertainties. The 
project's consultant is Deloitte Consulting LLP, with a project period from 2020 to 2025 and a 
budget of $18.5 million USD. 
3) Scope of the Study 
Major scope of the Study is as follows:  
1. Improve Utility and Sector Performance 
• Policy and regulatory frameworks for the decentralization of NEA improved 
• Electricity Regulatory Commission (NERC) technical capacities increased 
• Improved commercial and operational performance of NEA and its provincial canters 
[ Implemented partner]: MoEWRI, NEA, NERC 
2. Deploy Advanced Energy Systems 
• Strategic, Least Regrets Energy Planning Process Established and Institutionalized 
• Sustainable Energy Technologies Incentivized and Deployed by Local Governments 
[ Implemented partner]: MoEWRI, MoPIT, MOF, NRB, NEA 
3. Increase Private Sector Engagement 
• GoN Capacity to Conduct Competitive Bidding Strengthened 
• Program for the Procurement of new Renewable Energy Projects Developed 
[ Implemented partner]: MoEWRI, DoED, IBN, NERC, IPPAN 
4. Institutionalize Cross-Border Electricity Trading 
• Technical Systems, Regulatory Regimes, and Power Agreements Required to Conduct 
Cross Border Electricity Trade (CBET) Developed 
• Transparent Agreements Between Cross-Border Buyers and Sellers Completed 
[ Implemented partner]: MoEWRI, NERC, PTCN, SARI/EI

Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan  Final Report 
in Nepal Annex 2; Current Status of the Power Sector in Nepal 
 
 
2-65 
 
4) Implementation Organization of the Study 
MoEWRI is the optimal institution to lead and implement the necessary functions for the IRRP. 
An institutional framework for the IRRP and a roadmap for its implementation are proposed. 
The first stage involves establishing an IRRP Committee within MoEWRI. The committee will 
form working groups composed of members from relevant institutions to carry out tasks. 
In the second stage, an IRRP Cell will be established within MoEWRI. This cell will be a 
specialized departme nt within MoEWRI, consisting of dedicated staff, scholars, and 
consultants. 
In the third stage, an independent technical planning agency will be established within 
MoEWRI. Once the IRRP project's outcomes are established, this agency will support 
MoEWRI in continuously updating the plans. This stage is expected to occur 3 to 5 years later. 
The following roles are proposed for the working groups under the IRRP Committee: 
• Generation Planning 
• Transmission Planning 
• Power Demand Assessment 
• Distribution, Demand-Side Management, and Energy Efficiency 
• Energy Access, Off-Grid, and Renewable Energy 
• Climate, Gender, and Social Inclusiveness

projects. The responsible consultant has five (5) years of experience drafti ng contracts and 
contract negotiating on behalf of the GoN, IBN, MoEWRI, DoED, etc., and supports 
institutionalizing competitive bidding and creating standard documents for the power sector,

The Urja Nepal team had already established the IRRP Committee, but the working groups for

The discussion points are as follows: 
April 2022 Discussion:  Urja Nepal noted that various agencies conduct their own power 
demand forecasts using different parameters, leading to different results. If the assumptions 
are standardized, the differences between agencies can be minimized. Urja Nepal does not 
perform demand forecasting itself but provides methodology support to NEA. 
The IPSDP study team noted differences between the 2017 power demand forecast conducted 
by WECS and actual results, indicating a need for review. IPSDP will first derive prediction 
formulas from past data based on GDP growth rates and population growth r ates highly 
correlated with demand in various sectors (household, industrial, commercial, and others). The 
second step involves adding demand induced by government interventions (EV , e -cooking, 
energy efficiency). Urja Nepal requested data and assumptions used by IPSDP to support 
NEA's demand forecasting, leading to the sharing of the IPSDP progress report with Urja 
Nepal in June 2022. The IPSDP is ahead in considering power demand forecast , power 
development plan, and power system plan and start activities with a WG Kick-off meeting in 
June 2022.

2-67 
 
June 2022 Discussion: Urja Nepal's progress was delayed due to COVID -19, and they were 
just starting to establish sub-committees for generation, transmission, power demand, energy 
saving, and electrification. The technical transfer of the OptGen optimal power planning model 
for NEA was not progressing. They held a workshop on power demand forecasting in April 
2022 and planned to hold a comprehensive IRRP workshop (EV , e-cooking, energy efficiency) 
in August 2022. 
Cross-border power trade with India is also a key theme for Urja Nepal. They provide short -
term support for NEA (toward India) and long-term support (toward Bangladesh, etc.), sharing 
information as needed. A draft guideline for Cross -Border Electricity Trad ing (CBET) was 
submitted to MoEWRI, but progress was lacking. 
February 2023 Discussion: Comments from Urja Nepal on power trade included: 
Trading only with India, which has lower generation costs, is risky; discussions on activating 
power trading with BBIN are necessary. 
Transmission line development will be key in the coming years, and finding other players 
interested in transmission is crucial. Specifically, they propose a model where transmission 
facilities is owned, and royalties are earned over twenty (20) years. 
Nepal's supply capacity is very small compared to India's power demand, so while there is 
room for entry, Nepal's position needs to be strategic and cautious. 
June 2023 Discussion: Key points of discussion: 
(IPSDP) There is a view that electricity prices in India will decrease due to the large -scale 
introduction of solar power. Optimistic selling plans are risky, but recent trading prices on IEX 
are 4-5 IRP/kWh. Many ongoing hydropower projects in Nepal are economically viable at this 
price. 
(IPSDP) The investment amount for the projects is very large and not considered realistic; how 
to realize them is a major challenge. 
(Urja Nepal) International power trade is expected to be realized within ten ( 10) years, so 
immediate action is needed. Urja Nepal collaborates with NEA, Bangladesh agencies, etc., on 
this issue and shares information as appropriate. 
December 2023 Discussion: Key points of discussion: 
(Urja Nepal) Recognizes the importance of long -term planning like IPSDP and intends to 
collaborate with JICA in the same direction. 
(Urja Nepal) Continuously faces challenges in securing project funds. 
(Urja Nepal) Believes that foreign direct investment will be key, and there are moves to 
establish a trading company in Nepal, such as Indian Energy Exchange Limited ( IEX). To 
attract foreign direct investment, an open access system needs to be established. 
(Urja Nepal) Considers the unit price of exports to India crucial. Analysis considering future 
price declines is necessary. 
(Urja Nepal) Project screening by USAID is expected to begin after September in 2024.

August 2024 Discussion: Hearings were conducted regarding IPSDP's outcomes and results 
of finance analysis. The main findings from the hearings are as follows: 
Urja Nepal's comments on IPSDP overall: 
 
• It is especially important to open cross-border projects to private companies, and 
establishing market mechanisms is crucial. 
• The establishment of Independent Transmission is necessary, and System Operation 
is vital. 
• Regarding RPGCL, ADB is also believed to be investing, but it is not functioning. 
• Planning Management is important, but neither NEA nor RPGCL is fulfilling this 
role. An institution capable of performing Planning Management is needed. 
• Bangladesh is perceived to have credit risk. 
• Is the reduction in CO2 expected due to a decrease in imports from India? 
→ That is correct. 
Urjja Nepal's comments on results of finance analysis: 
 
• The analysis is interesting as a starting point for Nepal's future development. 
• The set values of ROA at 5% and ROE at 10% do not seem particularly problematic. 
• It is common for commercial banks to lend for 10 to 15 years, with a grace period 
during the construction phase. 
• The analysis on funding allocation is interesting as a hypothesis. However, it is 
important to know how much JICA can actually contribute in the future. 
• The amount that commercial banks can lend is not large. 
In March 2024, Urja Nepal announced a tender for network analysis and load flow studies of 
transmission lines connecting 33kV substations and above across Nepal. RPGCL is 
formulating a transmission master plan up to 2040, aiming to verify the power development 
plan for 2035 and assess the adequacy of transmission lines. Urja Nepal does not formulate 
power development and transmission plans itself but outsources the transmission line studies 
to external consultants.

2.7.3 Millennium Challenge Corporation (MCC)  
MCC is a bilateral USAID agency established by the US Congress in 2004. It provides grants to 
countries determined to have sound economic policies and potential for economic growth. 
Countries' eligibility is assessed based on their performance in twenty (20) policy indicators, and 
those meeting the criteria are selected as compact-eligible countries. As of April 2018, 18 countries 
were compact-eligible, including 12 in Africa and 4 in Asia. 
(1) Detailed Feasibility Study of Nepal Transmission Line Project 
In September 2017, MCC signed a 500 million USD funding agreement with the GoN. This 
agreement aims to maintain road quality, increase the availability and reliability of electricity, 
promote cross-border electricity trade between Nepal and India, and support investment promotion, 
economic growth acceleration, and poverty reduction. The program period is from 2018 to 2023. 
The Government of Nepal contributed an additional 130 million USD. 
On February 27, 2022, the Nepalese  Parliament ratified the MCC agreement, allowing the 
implementation of transmission line and substation construction and road rehabilitation projects. 
This is the largest grant to Nepal has ever received for a single project. 
The transmission line project's objective is to expand and strengthen the high-voltage transmission 
network to harness Nepal's hydropower potential and promote new investments in hydropower 
projects, facilitating increased power trade between India and Nep al. The project includes 
constructing a 315 km, 400 kV high- voltage transmission line connected to the Indian border to 
promote power trade . It also includes constructing three  (3) 400 kV GIS substations to convert 
voltage levels and enable further transmission and distribution. 
To strengthen the newly established Electricity Regulatory Commission (ERC), technical support 
is provided in areas such as tariff setting, regulatory formulation, dispute resolution, and economic 
and technical rules. Technical assi stance is also provided for improving NEA's transmission 
operations, preparing bidding documents, and transmission analysis for transmission project 
formation. 
In January 2017, the Detailed Feasibility Study (DFS) was submitted. The DFS outlines the project 
implementation framework where the EPC contractor is responsible for land acquisition, permits, 
resettlement, engineering, procurement, construction, testing, commissioning, and completion. 
The transmission line construction involves more than 600 km of 132 kV , 220 kV , and 400 kV 
lines, including 10 transmission lines. The substation construction includes 8 new substations and 
6 upgrades. The new 400 kV GIS indoor substations are located at Ratmate, New Butwal, and New 
Damauli. 
(Source: MCC Project Documents, Detailed Feasibility Study)

(2) Project Information (Progress of Nepal Transmission Line and Substation Construction) 
The progress of the construction of the 315 km 400 kV transmission line and three 400 kV GIS 
indoor substations is as follows: 
On November 28, 2022, the bidding process was initiated for the following sections: 
 
• Lot-1: 117 km 400 kV D/C transmission line from Lapsiphedi to Ratamate and New 
Hetauda 
• Lot-2: 90 km 400 kV D/C transmission line from Ratamate to Damauli 
• Lot-3: 108 km 400 kV D/C transmission line from New Damauli to New Butwal, with 
an optional section from New Butwal to the Nepal/India border 
The scope includes the design, procurement, transportation, installation, testing, and 
commissioning of the transmission lines. By the submission deadline of May 22, 2023, six (6) 
companies had submitted bids. However, upon opening the price proposals in September 2023, it 
was found that the bids were approximately 60% higher than the estimated costs, leading to a 
notification of failure to all bidders. Consequently, MCA -Nepal began consultations with the 
Ministry of Finance to re-procure the project. 
Given the critical importance of this section to the GoN and NEA, the MCA-Nepal Board decided 
in March 2024 to prioritize the 18 km section of the Butwal -Gorakhpur transmission line, 
separating it from the 315 km transmission line project. MCA -Nepal continues to work on 
procuring the services of design and construction contractors to complete the remaining 297 km of 
transmission lines within the agreed schedule. 
On June 30, 2023, the bidding process for the con struction of three (3) 400 kV GIS indoor 
substations began. The selected companies will be responsible for the design, supply, delivery, 
installation, testing, and commissioning of the substations. On October 19, 2023, seven (7) 
companies submitted bids for the construction of the three (3) substations, and the bid opening was 
conducted. 
The acquisition of land and forest clearance for the transmission lines and substation construction 
is targeted for completion by the end of August 2024. 
(Source: MCA-Nepal-Annual-Report-FY-2022-23, Republica) 
2.7.4 Japan International Corporation Agency (JICA) 
(1) Data Collection Survey for Regional Grid Integration in BBIN (Bangladesh, Bhutan, India 
and Nepal) 
1) Survey Background 
Joint venture of TEPCO Power Grid, Inc., Tokyo Electric Power Company Holdings, Inc. and 
Mitsubishi Research Institute, Inc. was awarded the contract in August 2020. The survey was 
started and duration of the survey is around 19 months from September 2020 to March 2022, 
but the survey was delayed due to pandemic of COVID-19 worldwide and in Nepal. 
2) Objectives of the Survey 
The survey aims to collect information on the export and import of electricity (international 
power trade) through interconnection lines in the BBIN countries (Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, 
Nepal). It analyzes the challenges, risks, and possibilities in developing these international

Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan  Final Report 
in Nepal Annex 2; Current Status of the Power Sector in Nepal 
 
 
2-75 
 
interconnection transmission line projects and conducts a supply-demand balance analysis for 
the region, including Myanmar and Sri Lanka. Additionally, the survey collects information 
on the activities and outcomes of other donors to propose directions for cooperation to realize 
international power trade in the South Asian region. 
3) Scope of the Survey 
The survey was conducted with the following points in mind: 
 
• Formation of projects to develop hydropower resources in Bhutan and Nepal and sell 
the generated electricity to the governments of Bangladesh or India. 
• Formation of projects related to system operation and other soft aspects necessary for 
implementing international power trade 
Based on the survey results, the current situation and issues are organized and analyzed, and 
proposals are made for a vision, roadmap, incentives, and JIC A cooperation. The survey 
utilizes existing frameworks for regional cooperation in South Asia, such as SARI/EI and 
SASEC. The survey proposes the vision (for 2030, 2040, and 2050), roadmap, and 
implementation framework, including: 
 
• Proposals for visions, roadmaps, and implementation frameworks for each country 
• Analysis of each country's intentions, political and diplomatic interests, and other 
factors hindering the activation of international interconnection 
• Utilization of existing consultation frameworks (SARI/EI, SASEC, etc.) 
• Identification and creation of incentives to promote the necessary 
multilateral/bilateral dialogues to advance project formation 
4) Information on the Survey 
In this survey, the Sunkoshi 2 and 3 sites in Nepal were proposed as potential power export 
sites to Bangladesh. However, the survey in Nepal revealed that the social and environmental 
costs of the Sunkoshi 2 site (987 MW) were very high, making development difficult. As an 
alternative, Nepal proposed the nearby Khimti Siwalaya site as a potential power export site 
to Bangladesh. To develop these sites as power export candidates to Bangladesh, the results of 
the ongoing hydropower feasibility study by the G overnment of Nepal need to be reviewed 
from a power export perspective. 
The study also examined the potential for transmitting power from the Sunkoshi 3 site (683 
MW) and the Khimti Siwalaya site (800 MW) to Bangladesh, comparing methods that utilize 
the Indian grid versus those that do not. Since power transmission from Nepal to Bangladesh 
inevitably involves passing through Indian Territory, the study proposed issues and 
considerations to be addressed for constructing interconnection lines to facilitate t his power 
import. 
The Nepalese grid is already synchronously interconnected with the Indian grid, and active 
power trading is conducted. If frequency regulation is entirely dependent on the Indian grid, 
the interconnection line flow between the grids will always vary to adjust for the fluctuations 
in the Nepalese grid. India has introduced the Deviation Settlement Mechanism (DSM), which 
imposes penalties based on the deviations of actual generation from scheduled generation 
within each 15-minute time block. To avoid such penalties, tie line load frequency bias control 
(TBC) is recommended to keep interconnection line flows at planned values and minimize 
DSM penalties. Technical cooperation is proposed to ensure NEA secures the necessary 
regulating capacity.

As a result of information exchange with the consultant in charge of BBIN in January 2022, it 
was confirmed that the overlap ped scope will be adjusted and information sharing will be 
carried out in the future. 
Key points of the discussions included: 
・ Confirming that the collection and examination of information on domestic power 
demand, power development plan, and power system plan in Nepal fall within the scope 
of the IPSDP study. The BBIN survey team requested the sharing of 2040 power demand 
forecast and power development plan to use consistent data in future studies. The IPSDP 
study team indicated difficulties in sharing power development plan (power source lists) 
and power demand forecast within the requested timeframe. 
・ The BBIN survey team confirmed their focus on the benefits of power trade with India 
and advancing regional interconnection involving India. 
・ The BBIN survey focuses on power trade from Sunkoshi 2 and 3 hydropower plants to 
Bangladesh and India, and requested updates on both projects. The IPSDP survey team 
noted that during the field survey in December 2021, Sunkoshi  2 was considered 
challenging due to extensive environmental impacts from the reservoir area, while 
Sunkoshi 3 was viewed positively, with DoED preparing a feasibility study. However, 
using Indian transmission lines is the only viable method for power transmission to 
Bangladesh, and engagement with India has not progressed favorably. 
・ As an additional scope, the BBIN survey explores new power trade  forms, such as 
hydrogen and ammonia-based power storage, and inquired about their potential in the 
IPSDP study. The IPSDP study noted that all surplus hydropower is currently expected 
to be exported  as electricity. For hydrogen and ammonia production, the economic 
feasibility of utilizing surplus hydropower needs to be confirmed, considering the need 
to maintain high plant operation rates. 
・ The IPSDP study team views the use of Nepalese hydropower as clean energy and 
hydrogen/ammonia utilization as key points. The BBIN survey team provided 
information that India's current administration aims for carbon neutrality by 2070 and 
has set a target of 500 GW of renewable energy by 2030, despite current power demand 
being 200 GW, indicating a strong commitment to climate change measures. 
2.7.5 Asian Development Bank (ADB) 
The ADB has been key development partner for Nepal ’s power sector and particularly so for 
NEA’s capacity enhancement in generation, transmission and distributions. Since 1999, ADB has 
conducted six (6) loan projects, one (1) grant project, and twenty one ( 21) technical assistance 
projects. 
ADB's Strategic Focus: 
ADB's primary objective is to make Nepal's energy sector a major driver of inclusive economic 
growth through investments in both on -grid and off -grid projects. On- grid investments include 
support for developing large -scale hydropower projects and related transmission infrastructure. 
ADB examines appropriate PPP methods and provides transaction  advisory services to the 
government to promote private sector investment in large -scale hydropower development. 
Transmission investments involve strengthening the domestic network for power transmission 
from generation sites to load centres within the country and supporting cross-border transmission

for power trade with India. 
Key Projects and Collaborations: 
In hydropower development, ADB co-financed the 144 MW Kali Gandaki A Hydropower Project 
with JICA in 2002 and implemented the 140 MW Tanahu Hydropowe r Project, a reservoir -type 
hydropower plant, in 2013 through co- financing with JICA, the European Investment Bank, and 
the Abu Dhabi Development Fund. 
Off-Grid Support and Renewable Energy: 
In remote areas, ADB supports government initiatives to improve access to modern energy 
resources by developing off -grid power using renewable energy sources and enhancing energy 
efficiency. 
Institutional Capacity Development: 
For institutional capacity development in the sector, ADB engages in policy dialogue with the  
government to: (i) Improve governance and efficiency in the energy sector. (ii) Establish an 
independent regulatory authority for energy and water. (iii) Support the enactment of relevant 
legislation, full implementation of NEA's financial regulation plan , and the introduction of 
appropriate structural reforms, including periodic electricity tariff revisions.
