(1) Power Transmission and Distribution Efficiency Enhancement Project: PTDEEP 
The primary objective of the Power Transmission and Distribution Efficiency Enhancement 
Project (PTDEEP) is to increase the distribution capacity in the Kathmandu V alle y by reducing 
overload and technical and commercial (electricity sales) losses in the distribution system, thereby 
improving the reliability and quality of power supply. This is achieved through the reinforcement 
of the transmission grid, capacity expansio n, rehabilitation of the distribution network in the 
project area, and enhancement of the operational and financial performance of NEA's distribution 
centers. NEA is the implementing agency for this project. 
The Government of Nepal and the ADB signed a loan agreement for PTDEEP on June 29, 2017. 
The project is scheduled for completion by June 2024. 
The project activities include: (i) Construction of six new (6) substations, upgrading Teku SS, and 
expanding Syuchatar S/S. (ii) Upgrading and undergrounding distribution lines and other network 
components where possible. (iii) Upgrading overhead distribution lines where undergrounding is 
not feasible. (iv) Procuring distribution transformers. (v) Replacing traditional meter s with smart 
meters.

(2) Nepal Electricity Grid Modernization Project: EGMP 
The primary objective of the Nepal Electricity Grid Modernization Project (EGMP) is to 
modernize the transmission and distribution systems to ensure reliable electricity for consumers. 
The project includes ten (10) components, such as the construction of new transmission lines, new 
substations, new distribution lines, enhancement of existing transmission lines, automation of new 
and existing substations, improvement of the load dispatch center, installation of smart meters, and 
strengthening NEA’s capacity for implementing institutional and corporate development plans. 
The project is supported by a 156 million USD loan from ADB and 39 million USD in counterpart 
funding from the GoN. EGMP was approved in 2020, became e ffective in February 2021, and is 
scheduled for completion by 2026. The implementing agency is NEA.

(3) Energy Sector Assessment, Strategy, and Road Map (ASR) 
This report (ASR) assesses the current energy sector by ADB and proposes investment priorities 
by the Government of Nepal and ADB. It aims to provide guidance on ADB's future investments 
and technical assistance activities. The report has been prepared with the cooperation of MoEWRI, 
DoED, NEA, AEPC, ERC, and others. 
In February 2016, the Cabinet approved an action plan to end the energy crisis within two (2) years 
and instructed the MoE to develop a concrete action plan to eliminate the power shortage within 
one year and achieve zero power shortage within two years. The action plan consists of 99 specific 
activities, including (i) legal amendments, (ii) policy decisions, (iii) administrative decisions and 
procedural reforms, and (iv) structural provisions and reforms. The main programs of the action 
plan are summarized below, indicating the period, responsible agencies, and supporting agencies. 
In February 2016, the GoN recognized the urgency and importance of mitigating the impacts of 
climate change and implementing climate adaptation actions. It submitted its Intended Nationally 
Determined Contributions (INDC) to the UNFCCC Secretariat, fully committing to the 
implementation of the INDC to contribute to global efforts in reducing greenhouse gas emissions.

Final Report Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan 
Annex 2; Current Status of the Power Sector in Nepal  in Nepal 
 
 
2-82 
 
 
Action Plan : Major Programs <Summary> 
 
I. Legal Reform Provisions 
(a) Formulate a new Electricity Act and submit it to the Parliament together with the National Electricity Regulatory 
Commission Bill. 
(b) Approve and implement the Energy Crisis Bill. 
(c) Simplify the licensing of projects and make the procedures more systematic to ensure their affiliation with the 
electricity transmission and distribution system. 
(d) Provide for the development of electricity projects of at least 500 MW . 
(e) Provide for income tax exemptions under the Electricity Act of 1992. 
(f) Amend the Electricity Theft Control Act of 2002. 
 
II. Policy Decisions 
(a) Formulate and implement a National Energy Security Policy. 
(b) Fund hydroelectric projects by mobilizing internal resources. 
(c)  Restructure the Ministry of Energy and its Department of Electricity Development. 
(d)  Complete the construction of high-priority hydroelectricity projects as follows: 200 MW within 1 year, an additional 
850 MW within 2 years, and 400 MW more within 3 years. 
(e) Construct reservoir-based projects as a priority . 
(f) Determine and adjust electricity tariff rates, power purchase rates, and wheeling charges in accordance with the 
weather and the time. 
(g) Ensure payment in return for purchase of power . 
(h) Follow take-or-pay instead of take-and-pay provisions. 
(i) Construct transmission lines and substations under the build–transfer model through a consortium of promoters. 
(j) Make electricity generation, transmission, and distribution projects national priority projects. 
(k) Make special provisions for the acquisition of land for electricity projects, transmission lines, and substations. 
(l) Review land compensation. 
(m) Provide a community support program. 
(n) Gradually install time-of-day meters to be able to charge all consumers peak and off-peak tariff rates. 
(o) Ensure that electricity services reach all the people of Nepal within the next 10 years. 
(p) Gradually implement the smart meter and smart grid concepts. 
(q) Develop and implement an electricity distribution master plan. 
(r) Develop an action plan for controlling electricity distribution system leakage and implement the plan. 
(s) Activate the National Transmission Grid Company. 
(t) Connect solar or wind power to the national grid. 
(u) Invite competitive bids for photovoltaic solar and wind electricity projects. 
(v) Negotiate and finalize purchase agreements for solar electricity and wind electricity based on the take -or pay 
principle. 
(w) Conduct an electricity conservation program to control technical and nontechnical leakage. 
(x) Carry out technical audits of large electricity consumers. 
 
III. Administrative Decisions and Procedural Reforms 
(a) Simplify exchange facilities for paying consultants and contractors in foreign currency. 
(b) Simplify the granting of work visas and labor permits. 
(c) Develop hydroelectric projects in the government basket. 
(d) Conclude power purchase agreements in convertible currency. 
(e) Provide inflation benefits to hydroelectric projects with a capacity of 25 MW equal to the benefits for projects with a 
capacity of over 25 MW and up to 100 MW. 
(f) Simplify the provisions relating to environmental impact assessment (EIA) or initial environmental examination (IEE) 
for applications to the NEA for a power sale or purchase agreement. 
(g) Assign high priority to the completion of transmission lines under construction. 
(h) Complete the new 220 kV Marsyangdi–Matatirtha transmission line as quickly as possible, as a matter of high priority. 
(i) Complete the construction of the new 132 kV Kataiya –Kushaha and Raxaul –Parwanipur transmission lines and 
substations to enable the country to import an additional 100 MW of electricity. 
(j) Strengthen and extend the national electricity system. 
(k) Give high priority to the construction of 400 kV substations. 
(l) Make suitable arrangements for any required access to forest areas for EIAs or IEEs.

Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan  Final Report 
in Nepal Annex 2; Current Status of the Power Sector in Nepal 
 
 
2-83 
 
(m) Simplify EIA or IEE studies of hydroelectric and transmission lines and their approval. 
(n) Simplify the process of obtaining approval for the felling of trees and the taking of a lease of land for the development 
of hydroelectric and transmission projects. 
(o) Provide for a 10% equity share in projects for affected communities and individuals in the project districts. 
(p) Forecast load demand on the basis of actual electricity demand. 
(q) Expand and strengthen the NEA in an organized way. 
(r) Develop an underground distribution system and modern distribution kiosk technology. 
(s) Remove obstructions to the construction and extension of 11 kV and 33 kV lines. 
 
I V. Structural Provisions and Reforms 
(a) Form a Central Energy Crisis Prevision Coordination Committee and a District Level Energy Crisis Prevention 
Coordination Committee for the effective implementation of the Energy Crisis Prevention Decade. 
(b) Make the necessary arrangements for interagency coordination at the Joint Secretary level for the development of 
electricity projects. 
(c) Form a Facilitation Committee chaired by the chief secretary and with the secretaries of the related ministries as 
members. 
(d) Provide for a mechanism for the development and construction of reservoir-based projects. 
(e) Restructure and revitalize the Water and Energy Commission. 
(f) Carry out a rural electrification campaign. 
(g) Establish a National Electricity Generation Company. 
(h) Create an empowered and resourceful consulting company at the appropriate government level. 
(i) Carry out the financial and institutional restructuring of the National Transmission Grid Company. 
(j) Establish a National Power Trade Company. 
(k) Form power generation companies as holding companies of the NEA.

ANNEX 3 CURRENT SITUATION OF THE ELECTRIC POWER FACILITIES 
3.1 E XISTING POWER PLANTS 
Regarding the existing power plants in Nepal on 202 3, NEA (Nepal Electricity Authority)  has 
1,056.7MW of hydropower plants, 53.4MW of thermal power plants and 25MW of solar power 
plants and IPPs have 1,544.5MW of hydropower plants  and solar power plants . Total installed 
capacity is 2,680.1MW. 
 
 
Source: NEA Annual Report 2022 /2023 
Figure 3.1-1  Installed Capacity by Developers in Nepal (MW) 
The peak demand for domestic power in 2023 is 1,870 MW. While the total installed capacity 
significantly exceeds the demand, power imports are necessary during the dry s eason when 
hydropower output declines. 
3.1.1 Existing Power Plants 
Most of Nepal's power plants are hydropower plants, and the existing generation methods include 
ROR (Run of River), PROR (Peaking Run of River), and STO (Storage). Additionally, PS (Pumped 
Storage) is also planned. An overview of each generation method is shown in Table 3.1-1. 
The lists of NEA and IPPs power plants are shown in Table 3.1-2 and Table 3.1-3 - Table 3.1-6 
respectively.

3-2 
 
Table 3.1-1  Generation Type of Hydropower 
Type Characteristics 
ROR: Run of River 
 
This method involves diverting river water directly to the 
power plant for generation. The amount of electricity 
generated varies with the changes in water volume during the 
rainy and dry seasons. While it cannot store water and faces 
the challenge of low generation in the dry season and wasted 
water in the rainy season, it has the advantage of relatively 
low construction costs. 
 
PROR: Peak Run of River 
 
This method involves storing water in a regulating pond to 
adjust water volume for generation. It can store enough water 
for one day to one week, allowing for the adjustment of 
generation according to short -term weather chang es 
(sunshine or rain) and power demand variations. It is more 
efficient than run-of-river hydropower plants.
 
 
STO: Storage 
 
This method involves storing water in a large reservoir for 
power generation. Water is stored during the rainy season and 
released during the dry season for generation. The method 
allows for complete control over water flow, enabling stable 
generation throughout the year.  On the other hand, 
construction cost and impacts on environment are relatively 
larger due to the dam and reservoir.
 
PS: Pumped Storage 
 
This method utilizing the head between two regulating pond. 
In daytime with high demand, upper pond release  the water 
and generate electricity. In night time with low demand, 
water is pumping from lower pond to upper pond using 
surplus electricity. Owed to large installed capacity and quick 
response, it can serve flexible regulating capacity for power 
system operation.
 
Source: The Federation of Electric Power Companies of Japan （https://www.fepc.or.jp/enterprise/hatsuden/water/index.html）

in Nepal Annex 3; Current Situation of the Electric Power Facilities 
 
 
3-3 
 
Table 3.1-2  List of Existing Power Plants owned by NEA 
 
Source: NEA Annual Report 2022 /2023 
NEA owns 20 hydropower plants with the installed capacity of 573.3MW, designed annual power 
generation of 3,360,830MWh and average plant factor of 67%. The majority generation scheme of 
hydropower plants is ROR or PROR. Hydropower plants by NEA had been developed mainly on 
the Gandaki river system including Kaligandaki A  (144MW ), Mid-Marsyangdi (70MW ), 
Marsyangdi (69MW) and Upper Trishuli 3A. Only Kulekhani I and Kulekhani II cascade plants 
are operated as STO (Storage) in Nepal. 
There are only two diesel thermal power plants with the installed capacity of 53.4MW including 
Mutifuel (39MW) and Hetauda Diesel (14.4MW). These are currently stand -by owed to the 
increase of the power trade with India. 
There were 126 operating IPP hydropower projects of over 1 MW as of 2024, with a total capacity 
of 2,055.2 M W. While the majority of the projects are small and medium sized hydropower with a 
capacity of 30 MW or less, 11 power plants with a capacity of more than 30 MW (totaling 929.9 
MW) ha ve commenced operations in the past three years, starting with the PROR Upper 
Tamakoshi (456 MW) in July 2021.  It is expected that IPPs continue to operate large -scale 
hydropower plants in the future.

3.1.2 Power Generation  
Available amount of annual power by operators in Nepal is shown in Figure 3.1-2. Available 
amount of annual power has increased 2. 64 times in nine years fro m 4, 687GWh in 201 4 to 
12,369GWh in 2023. In recent years there has been a particularly significant increase in the power 
output of IPPs and the NEA subsidiary  with 5.6 times increase from 919 GWh to 5,148 GWh for 
IPPs and 16.477 times increase from 151 GWh to 2,488 GWh for the EA subsidiary  between 
2014 and 2023. 
The monthly electricity production of NEA -owned power plants in 2022/2023 in Figure 3.1-3 
shows that the highest electricity production is in the rainy season in Shrawan (mid -July to mid-
August) at 311.5 GWh and the lowest in the dry season in Magh (mid-January to mid-February) at 
155.4 GWh in the dry season. As a comparison of monthly electricity production, the dry season 
only accounts for 49.9% of that of the wet season.  
On the other hand, major power plants in NEA, such as Kaligandaki A (144 MW), Mid-Marsyangdi 
(70 MW), Marsyangdi (69 MW) and Upper Trishuli 3A (60 MW), have a PROR of nearly 70% 
plant factor in both designed and actual. These are relatively less fluctuation of seasonal decrease

in the dry season. Although efforts have been made in order to reduce this seasonal fluctuation of 
power generation between rainy and dry season by promoting the de velopment of STO plants, it 
should be noted that there is still a large difference as the nature of hydropower which is depended 
on characteristics of natural flow and topographic conditions.

3.2 E XISTING TRANSMISSION LINE AND SUBSTATION 
The current power system consists of 220kV , 132kV , and 66kV transmission line and substation. 
The main transmission line is the 132kV transmission line that runs east to west through southern 
Nepal, and this main transmission line connects Anarmani in the east to Mahendranagar in the west. 
The main demand areas are located in the central hills, including the Kathmandu basin, and in the 
south, including industrial areas such as Butwal, Birgunj and Biratnagar. In addition, many 
hydropower plants are located in the central and northern parts of Nepal, and these power plants 
mainly supply electricity to demand areas in the central and southern regions through 132kV 
transmission lines. 
Table 3.2-1 shows the outline of the facility , and the power system diagram ( including under 
construction and planned) and the location map are shown in Figure 3.2-1 and Figure 3.2-2. Table 
3.2-2 and Table 3.2-3 show the list of existing transmission lines and the list of existing substations.  
The power system of Nepal is interconnected with the system of India by the international 
interconnection line, and if the power plants  in Nepal cannot supply the demand, the shortage 
power is supplied by importing power from India through these interconnection lines.  Detail s 
will be given in the chapter on power interchange. 
Currently, there are only seven 220kV transmission lines (one of which is 400kV specification), 
but in the future, with the development of power plants and the increasing demand, the introduction 
of bulk systems such as 400kV will be expanded.

3.3 S YSTEM OF POWER SYSTEM OPERATION 
3.3.1 System of Power System Operation 
In Nepal, LDC (Load Dispatch Centre) operations, monitors and controls  (Supply and Demand 
adjustment / grid control) all Nepali power grids. LDC is at Siuchatar in Kathmandu city and 
belongs to System Operation Department of Transmission Directorate. 
LDC monitors frequency of power grid using a grid monitoring panel, and adj usts an output of 
reservoir typed hydropower of NEA and the amount of import from India (monitoring power flow 
in an international connection line). LDC has 5 persons per team and 3 shifts as the operation 
system, and each member operates power stations, substations/transmission lines and international 
connection lines. Operators in power stations and substations control their facilities by a contact 
from LDC, and LDC collects information of the facilities in real time through SCADA 
(Supervisory Control And Data Acquisition) system. The SCADA system has a remote control 
function, but it is not used for actual operations. 
 
 
Source: JICA Study Team  from NEA annual report 2020/2021  
Figure 3.3-1  Outline of Transmission Directorate Organization Chart 
LDC is involved in an actual practice for power trade with India, and conducts daily power trade 
through IEX (Indian Exchange Market) as one of media of power trade with India. The 
management work for the results of the trade is carried out by Power Trade Department, and LDC 
periodically hands in the report to Power Trade Department.

3.4 R ESULTS OF POWER TRADE WITH INDIA 
Figure 3.4-1 shows the results of power trade with India (based on Peak demand) in FY2021 of 
Nepal (Latter half of July, 2021 A.D. to First half of July, 2022 A.D.). Currently, Nepal only trades 
power with India institutionally and physically, and the results shown in the figure mean only 
power trade with India. 
Nepal exports power to India in the rainy season because developing new hydropower plants leads 
to the increment of the generation capacity year by year. On the other hand, in the dry season, 
Nepal imports power from India to meet Nepali domestic demand because the river discharge 
cannot be ensured. This because there are a lot of ROR typed hydropower plants in Nepal. Yearly 
comparison etc. are mentioned in Chapter 8, Power Trade.

3.5 ISSUES ON THE ELECTRIC POWER FACILITIES 
3.5.1 Power Generation Facilities 
(1) Large Gap of Monthly Power Generation between Dry and Rainy Seasons 
As shown in Section 3.1, the power generation sources in Nepal are composed of mainly 
hydropower plants except some thermal power and renewable energy plants. Therefore there are a 
large gap of power generation between rainy season (from May to October) and dry season (from 
November to April). As a result, although annual balance of supply is positive owed to the rich 
power generation in rainy season, it is necessary to import el ectricity from India in dry season 
when outputs of most hydropower plants decline to a third against rated capacity. Nepal have 
enhanced to mitigate this seasonal gap of power generation by the promotion of STO development 
but only Tanahu hydropower project is progressed to the construction stage. 
(2) Insufficient Regulation Capacity 
As Nepal is the landlocked country whose borders do not across with sea and access to outside 
must be via India. Thus, it is difficult to develop large scale interconnected thermal  power plants 
of gas or coal and regulation of power supply including compensation to the shortage against the 
domestic demand is depended on the power trade from India. 
3.5.2 Transmission Line and Substation 
(1) Vulnerability of 132kV system 
The country of Nepal stretches about 700 km from east to west, and the existing bulk power system 
is a 132 kV transmission line from east to west and the backbone is 1 route. However, in response 
to future growth in power demand, this 132 kV 1 route bulk power system has limits in terms of 
capacity and reliability. 
(2) Necessity of transmission of power sources unevenly located in the east and west in the future 
Existing hydro power plants are mainly located in the Gandaki River system in central Nepal, but 
in the future, large-scale hydroelectric power plants are expected to be developed on the Karnali 
River in the west and the Koshi River system in the east. For this reason, the hydroelectric power 
sources, which are unevenly distributed in the east and west, should be located around Kathmandu 
in the central area, where the main demand is.  Reinforcement of the grid is an urgent issue for 
power transmission.  
(3) Need for multiple interconnection lines in the future 
Currently, there are about 8 transmission lines with voltages of 400kV , 132kV , 33kV , and 11kV in 
the international interconnection line. However, the 400kV transmission line with a large 
transmission capacity is only one location (Dhalkebar-Muzzaffarpur). In the future, power exports 
will increase with the expansion of power de velopment. Therefore, multiple 400kV 
interconnection lines will be installed and it is necessary to secure capacity for international trade.

ANNEX 4 REVIEW OF EXISTING DEVELOPMENT PLANS 
 
 
This Chapter review existing development plans in Nepal and summarize necessary inputs for IPSDP 
(Integrated Power System Development Plan) prepared in this Project. It shall be noted that contents and 
descriptions in this Chapter are sourced from original studies and are shown as references. 
4.1 HYDROPOWER DEVELOPMENT 
In this section, existing hydropower development plans are summarized as follows. 
 
i) Nationwide Master Plan Study on Storage-type Hydroelectric Power Development 
ii) Review in Data Collection Survey on Hydropower Development Project 
iii) Water Basin Development Master Plan and Hydropower Master Plan 
iv) Assessment of Hydropower Potential of Nepal 
4.1.1 N ationwide Master Plan Study on Storage-type Hydroelectric Power Development 
The study commenced in February 2012 and the final report was report ed to GoN in February 
2014. In t he final report, ten (10) projects are reported as promising storage  type hydropower 
projects. Summary of the MP 2014 is outlined as follows. 
(1) Target of Study 
Estimation of future power demand for 20 years beginning in 2013 was made and eligibility of 
storage type hydropower plants during this period was confirmed. The JICA Study Team reviewed 
65 storage type  hydropower projects in the long -list presented by NEA from viewpoints of 
technical, environmental and social, economic, funding aspects etc. and proposed promising 
projects. Promising projects were sorted out by way of priority by evaluating order of development, 
magnitude of project, commissioning timing and financing source etc. As a result, a proposal of 
master plan is presented for development of storage type projects for 20 years from 2013. 
(2) Power Demand Forecast 
Power demand forecast was made by estimating actual demand growth of each sector (domestic, 
industrial, commercial/service, irrigation and others) from 1991 to 2011 considering GDP growth 
forecast for each sector. Base case scenario indicates growth rate of electricity demand (kWh) and 
peak demand (kW) as 5.8% for first 10 years, 7.7% for last 10 years and 6.8% in average. The 
actual demand growth from 2012 to 2017, however, shows higher growt h rate than this demand 
forecast, such as 8.7% in electricity demand and 7.1% in peak demand according to NEA Annual 
Report 2017. 
(3) Selection of Promising Projects 
Following three (3) steps of studies were made on 67 storage -type hydropower projects listed up 
by NEA.  
 
First Step 
31 projects were selected by excluding projects which i) development stage had been matured, ii) 
duplicated with other project, iii) had technical difficulties or heavy environmental and social  
impacts.

Final Report Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan  
Annex 4; Review of Existing Development Plans  in Nepal 
 
 
4-2 
 
- Project in detailed design stage or further 
Tanahu (140MW), Budhi Gandaki (600MW), Tamor (380MW), Kaligandaki-2 (660MW), 
Bagmati Multipurpose (140MW), Nisti-Panah (90.4MW) 
- Projects with difficulties for development was excluded in terms of i) installed capacity > 
1,000MW ii) height of dam > 300m iii) project cost > 2 Billion USD iv) annual flow regulation 
rate < 5% v) resettlement in reservoir > 500 houses vi) within national park, wildlife 
conservation area or world heritage. 
 
Second Step 
Evaluation was made on 31 projects by means of the Multi Criteria Analysis and superiority was 
compared by scoring. Evaluation items are followings;  
 
- Hydrology: Reliability of hydrological data, Glacial Lake Outburst Flood  ( GLOF), 
sedimentation 
- Geology: Geological condition, earthquakes 
- Lead time to implementing projects 
- Project benefit 
- Natural environment: Forestry, Conservation Area, Fish, Rare Species  
- Social environment: Impact by transmission line construction, resettlement, loss of farm 
land, indigenous people, tourism 
 
Third Step 
Ten (10) p romising projects were selected from scoring in the second step. Conditions or 
requirements below were considered.  
- Required total capacity; 2,900 MW capacity was scheduled to be developed by 2031/32 from 
demand forecast. Necessary storage type capacity of 2,600MW was determined excluding on -
going development (Tanahu, Budhi Gandaki). 
- Result of scoring indicated many projects in the Western region had superiority. Considering 
demand area and transmission line construction, number of the promising projects were limited 
by five (5) projects in each drainage area.  
- Four (4) projects with which IPP developers held the development license as ROR hydropower 
were excluded. Excluded projects are;   
 Tila-1 (617.2MW), Bhanakot (810WM), Tamakoshi 3 (287MW), Dudh Koshi 2 (156.6MW) 
Ten (10) projects indicated in Table 4.1-1 were finally selected as promising projects.  
Among those, Kokhajor-1 was abandoned due to low project economics found by detailed study 
including site reconnaissance. As Lower Jhimruk is located in the reservoir area of Naumure 
project, Naumure was nominated as a promising project. It was noted that Naumure had a 
possibility to have a multi-purpose dam function integrating an irrigation project led by MoEWRI.

(4) Development Plan 
Levels of project development stage were varied among selected projects such as F/S level, Pre-
F/S level and/or desk-top study level, a development plan was proposed for prioritization.  
The JICA Study Team  conducted technical studies including risk evalua tion of GLOF, 
sedimentation impact, geological site reconnaissance, review of studies in the past, re -evaluation 
of annual energy production and preliminary environmental and social survey.  
Cost of each project was also re-evaluated and necessary physical contingencies were estimated so 
that the level of details to be equal for the comparison purpose. Construction period were also re-
estimated including evaluation of each transmission line and access roads construction. 
A power system development software, Wien Automatic System Planning (WASP) IV , developed 
by International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) was then utilized for analyzing the most 
appropriate installation timeline to minimize total generation cost of the power system. Assumption 
and basic features for economic evaluation are listed below;  
 
- On-going storage type projects, Kulekhani No.3, Tanahu and Budhi Gandaki be completed 
as scheduled, 
- Imported electricity from India be maximum 12MW until 2014/15 then 162MW (based on 
discussion with NEA)  
- ROR hydropower count Tamakoshi V (87MW, COD 2021/22) and Upper Arun (335MW, 
COD 2024/25) , both of  which is under preparation for construction, and 100MW 
commissioning afterwards,  
- Discount rate be 10%, 
- 90% of project cost be depreciated,  
- Foreign currency be 80% and local currency 20%, 
- Allowable Loss of Load Provability be 1.375%, with which 5 days deficit in a year, 
- Cost of Energy not Supplied (ENS Cost) be 0.76USD/kWh

(1) Review in the Data Collection Survey 
The survey was carried out from August 2017 to March 2018 for confirming progress, project

A proposal about technical and financial assistance was made for two (2) most promising projects,

4.1.3 W ater Basin Development Master Plan and Hydropower Master Plan 
(1) Overview of Water Basin Development Master Plan / Irrigation Master Plan 
ADB and GoN have tasked the “ Water Resources Project Preparatory Facility (WRP PF)” under 
MoEWRI to identify and prepare high-priority water resources management projects for potential 
funding by the government with assistance from ADB and other development partners. This 
initiative is consistent with the GoN ’s and ADB’s priorities f or enhancing resilience to climate, 
ensuring food security, and promoting inclusive economic growth.

development, review the IMP1990 and prepare a new IMP2019 based on integrated water resource 
management principles.

4.1.4 Assessment of Hydropower Potential of Nepal 
WECS conducted hydropower potential study based on GIS by utilizing ASTER -GDEM and 
hydrological model for whole Nepal and summarized in “ Hydropower Potential of Nepal 2019” 
(WECS2019). The purposes of the study are; to carry out the assessment of gross ROR hydropower 
potential of rivers in Nepal and arrive at a techno- economically viable potential, and to identify 
and assess the potential of reservoir type projects with installed capacity of 100MW or more and 
run-off river projects with installed capacity 300 MW or more. 
WECS2019 identified 9, 8, and 18 projects on the Koshi, Gandaki and Kalnari river basins 
respectively. The WECS2019 conclude with describing further studies should  be conducted to 
conclude technical and economic feasibility.

Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan  Final Report 
in Nepal Annex 4; Review of Existing Development Plans  
 
 
4-9 
 
Since MoEWRI and WECS do not have intention of reflecting updated plans of WECS2019, to 
the hydropower development plans, the IPSDP in this study does not consider the project sites of 
WECS2019. 
4.1.5 Preparation of River Basin Plans and Hydropower Development Master Plans and 
Strategic Environmental and Social Assessment 
As already described in the section 2.7/1 (2), WECS has been carrying out “ Preparation of River 
Basin Plans and Hydropower Development Master Plans and Strategic Environmental and Social 
Assessment” by WB finance. As this study and IPSDP had been being prepared in parallel, JICA 
Study Team has reviewed "Technical Note 22: Draft Hydropower Development Master Plan" (July 
2022) from WECS, which was not finalized but major outcomes for hydropower were sufficiently 
included. The summary of this note is as follows: 
- Review of past development plans, including those with acquired licenses and multi -
purpose projects mentioned in the irrigation master plan (IMP2019) that invo lve 
hydropower generation. 
- Hydropower potential study for newly development and formulation of a development plan 
as the “Greenfield Hydropower Project”. 
- Formulation of a hydropower development master plan. 
In the above process, hydropower projects issued with “Construction License” are referred to as 
"locked projects". These are excluded from the optimization of river basin development 
consideration, and there are no proposals to change the plans, with the current plans given priority. 
However, hydropower projects issued with “Survey License” are considered for review under the 
Greenfield Hydropower Project. On the other hand, large -scale reservoir-type hydropower and 
multi-purpose dam projects from the irrigation master plan (IMP2019), which have been promoted 
for many years either by the Nepalese government or as bilateral projects, are treated as "locked 
projects" and are not subject to review. The Greenfield Hydropower Projects identified in this 
survey have been reflected in the draft hydropower development master plan. Although some large-
scale storage-type hydropower development projects were recommended to amend the layout 
including dam sites in consideration of  the topography, geological conditions, and their 
relationship with the irrigation master p lan, these recommendation have not been adopted to the 
master plan  
The IPSDP does not adopt updated multi -purposes projects proposed in IMP2019 from the 
following reasons; 
- In the IPSDP formulated by this Survey, the generation planning is based on the DoED data 
base with licensed projects including Construction License , Survey Licenses  and 
Governmental reserved projects. In addition, it should be noted that this WECS master plan 
has not been finally approved yet.  
- Through the progress after the time of this survey of IMP2019, the number of projects with 
Construction Licenses and Survey Licenses has increased, and the newly identified projects 
among Greenfield Hydropower Projects from this survey are limited to a few projects in 
the uppermost parts of each r iver. Additionally, these Greenfield Hydropower Projects  
proposed in IMP2019  have not applied for licenses and are not included in the DoED  
database. Therefore, Greenfield Hydropower Projects were not adopted in this survey.  
Therefore, although the results of this IMP2019 studies including Greenfield Hydropower Projects

The 400 kV backbone system connecting Nepal from east to west consists of five zones, the 
structure of which is shown in Figure 4.2-2. Information on major power sources and cross-border 
connections for each zone is listed  in Table 4.2-2. The zones are defined as Zone 1 to Zone 5, 
starting from the westernmost zone. 
 
 
Source: JICA Study Team 
Figure 4.2-2  Structure of the Backbone System in 2040 
Under the previous plan, a 400kV backbone system would be constructed along the Terai Plain, 
connecting the east and west sides of the country, and major hydropower  sources would be 
aggregated along rivers in the north-south direction and radially connected to the 400kV system. 
The plan was to export electricity to India from substations on this 400kV backbone system. In 
this master plan, a second 400 kV backbone tra nsmission line was added to the north side of the 
hilly region of the country to supply the domestic demand according to the economic growth. The 
plan is to directly interconnect large hydroelectric power sources to this second backbone system. 
This plan will make the 400kV backbone system a mesh system instead of a radial system, which 
will ensure an alternative transmission route in the event of a fault of a hydroelectric power line, 
thereby improving the reliability of the power supply.

4.2.2 Progress of Construction of the Backbone System 
This section describes the progress of the construction of a 400 kV transmission and substation 
facility, which will serve as the backbone of Nepal's power grid. The existing facility is the 
Dhalkebar - Muzzaffarpur transmission line connecting Nepal and India, which has a 400 kV , 2 cct 
transmission capacity of 1,200 MW. 
Next, it is described the ongoing construction of the Hetauda - Dhalkebar - Inaura substations and 
the 400 kV , 2 cct transmission line connecting these substations, which is being funded by the

Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan  Final Report 
in Nepal Annex 4; Review of Existing Development Plans  
 
 
4-15 
 
Nepalese government. The substations will be built in the 1.5CB system with GIS. The facilities 
in Nepal connecting to the 400kV Dhalkebar substation, which is the hub for import and export of 
electric power, were previously 220kV or lower, but with the completion of these facilities, the 
transmission capacity will be expanded and the function of import and export of electric power 
will be improved. 
In addition, a 400 kV transmission line connecting New Kimti - Barhabise - Lapsephedi is under 
construction. 
Furthermore, with the support of the Millenium Challenge Corporation (MCC) of the United States, 
the Hetauda - Ratemate - New Damauli - New Butwal and Ratemate - The New Butwal substation 
will be connected to the planned New Butwal - Gorakhpur 400kV , 2cct and will contribute to 
enhancing the functionality of the second cross border transmission line between Nepal and India. 
These ongoing projects are reflected in the structural diagram of the 400 kV backbone system in 
Figure 4.2-3.

city of Kathmandu, are shown in Figure 4.2-6. The Kathmandu system consists of an outer ring 
line and a load-supply transmission line from the outer ring line to the city center. 
In order to improve supply reliability and maintain adequate supply voltage for further increase in 
demand, construction of a 132 kV outer ring line in the southern half of the Kathmandu area and 
connection to the Lapsiphedi substation (400 kV; in planning), which is closest to the Kathmandu 
area, will be necessary.

5.1 A CTUAL POWER DEMAND  
In the power demand forecast, it is necessary to grasp past actual power demand and consider 
national policies such as future energy and economic policies in the country, which is not reflected 
in past data . Therefore, at first past actual power demand was grasped as the basis for demand 
forecasting and trends were analyzed. 
5.1.1 Actual Power Demand 
As for the actual power demand, the actual electricity sales, monthly power generation, daily load, 
regional power demand, and load factor were analyzed. 
(1) Electricity sales 
Electricity sales are shown in Figure 5.1-1. In 2020, the electricity sales growth rate had decreased 
significantly due to the impact of COVID-19 pandemics, but it has recovered up to approximately 
a 20 % growth rate in 2021 and beyond. Electricity sales are predicted to increase at the same 
growth rate in the future as before.  
 
 
Source: Prepared by JICA Study Team based on Annual Report of Nepal Electricity Authority 2021/2022

In terms of the breakdown of electricity sales, domestic  use accounts for the largest share of 
electricity sales (4 2% in 2022), followed by industr ial use (3 9%). Both of t he domestic and 
industrial sectors account for 81% of the total. 
The transition of elect ricity sales  by sector  is as shown in Figure 5.1-2. There has been no 
significant change in the percentage by sector in recent 12 years. Domestic and industr ial sales 
account for about 80 % of the total. In this demand forecast, information on policies and social 
trends that affect the rates by sector has been collected and reflected in the power demand forecast.

(2) Monthly electricity generation  
There are significant differences in monthly electricity generation between the wet and dry seasons. 
There are a large number of ROR (Run-of River) type hydropower plants in Nepal, and electricity 
generation of ROR  decreases during the dry season when precipitation is low. The monthly 
electricity generation in 2020 is shown in Figure 5.1-4. 
Power imports account for 10 % of total power supply , are  not only in the dry season when 
hydropower generation declines but also in the wet season (July and August). It suggests a shortage 
of power supply to power demand  throughout the year. I n the dry season (January, February, 
December), power imports from India account for 45% of the electricity supply, and the power 
supply-demand gap by season is highly biased. 
 
 
Source: Prepared by JICA Study Team based on Annual Report 2019/2020, Annual Report 2020/2021

The daily load curves  are shown in Figure 5.1-5. The daily power supply- demand balance is 
adjusted by using NEA (Nepal Electricity Authority)'s PROR (Peaking Run-Of-River) and Storage 
hydropower generation. The lighting peak is formed between 18:00 and 20:00 as households turn 
on lights and other appliances with the sunset , showing a typical trend in the early stage of 
economic development. It is expected that the peak shift s to the daytime in the future with the 
economic development.

5.1.2 Analysis of Power Demand Trends 
The actual data related to power demand was reviewed. It is found that despite delays in electric 
power development and the impact of COVID-19 pandemics, as shown in Chapter 3, the amount 
of installed capacity is steadily increasing, and power demand is also on the rise. The system loss 
and the load fact or have improved, but the improvements are not yet sufficient, and they are 
expected to continue to improve.  
In the long term, there is a possibility that power demand  will grow substantially due to the 
promotion of electrification, the spread of electric vehicles, and the development of industrial 
zones and SEZs as the policies of the GoN. Therefore, these events affecting power demand need 
to be considered in addition to forecasts based on historical data.

5.2 EXISTING POWER DEMAND FORECAST IN NEPAL 
Nepal’s power demand forecasts are presented in The Electricity Demand Forecast Report (2015-
2040) by WECS (Water and Energy Commission Secretariat) in January 2017. The validity of the 
electricity demand forecast by WECS is verified based on actual power demand in recent years. 
5.2.1 Power Demand Forecast by WECS 
In the past, power demand has been forecasted by NEA. The projections by NEA were based on 
historical data on grid expansion, population growth, and economic growth. The NEA projections 
did not consider policy interventions that would affect power demand, such as conver ting energy 
sources to electricity. Therefore, WECS conducted power demand forecast in 2017, considering 
new policies and plans, and this forecast is now the basis for power demand forecast in Nepal. 
WECS used the analysis software MAED ( Model for Analysis of Energy Demand) provided by 
the IAEA (International Atomic Energy Agency) for power demand forecast. This forecast is 
characterized by forecasting demand in three  cases, BAU (Business As Usual), Reference, and 
High, and two scenarios considering policies that affect power demand. 
(1) Power demand Forecast Methodology 
IAEA assists member countries in investigating energy and power development plans. To this end, 
IAEA has developed and provided energy analysis models to analyze various issues covering the 
social, economic, technical, and environmental aspects of energy decisions. One of them is MAED. 
MAED assesses future energy demand based on assumptions about the medium - and long-term 
socioeconomic, technological, and demographic developments. Figure 5.2-1 outlines the input and 
output of MAED. 
 
 
Source: Prepared by JICA Study Team based on Power demand Forecast Report (2015-2040) 
Figure 5.2-1  Outline of MAED Input and Output 
Energy demand is computed for the main demand sectors of households, services, industry, and 
transportation. MAED is characterized, among other things, by its response to socioeconomic 
development scenarios. The essential factors that affect energy demand are as follows. 
 
- GDP (Gross Domestic Product) growth rates and their structural changes 
- Population growth and its distribution in the country (urban and rural areas) 
- Changes in lifestyle 
- Population mobility growth 
- Passenger and freight transportation

- Market penetration of competing energy forms 
The application of the MAED model requires detailed information about demography, economy, 
energy-consuming technologies (e.g. , number of vehicles) , and energy consumption. Therefore, 
information about the base year of the study should be collected first.  Selection of the base year 
was made based on the availability of data and the assessment that the data are representative of 
the economic and energy situation of the coun try. After reviewing the available data , the year 
2014/2015 were selected as the base year. 
The economic growth rate has been considered based on National Planning Commission Targets, 
and the following three strategic economic growth scenarios were prepared. 
 
(1) BAU level economic scenario of 4.5% GDP growth rate (Low case) 
(2) Reference level economic scenario of 7.2% GDP growth rate (Middle case) 
(3) High level economic scenario of 9.2% GDP growth rate (High case) 
It is considered that the total population will increase from 28.06 million in 2015 to 39.72 million 
at the end of 2040, with a  growth rate of 1.4%. Population growth is assumed to be the same for 
all three economic scenarios above. 
Scenarios with policy interventions were considered in the Reference level economic scenario and 
the High level economic scenario, and a total of five scenarios were considered, including scenarios 
without policy interventions. 
 
(4) Reference level economic scenario of 7.2% GDP growth rate with policy intervention 
(5) High level economic scenario of 9.2% GDP growth rate with policy intervention 
Assumptions for the policy intervention scenario were set as follows.  The same policies were 
considered in all economic scenarios. 
 
- 75% of the water heating in an urban household will be done by electricity by 2020 
- 100% of the cooking in an urban household will be done by electricity by 2020 
- At least 7% of the cooking in a rural household will be done by electricity by 2020 
- 100% electrification by 2020 
- 18% of the total passenger kilometers demand will be fulfilled by electric vehicles and 7% 
by electric metro in the city by 2025 
(2) Power demand forecast 
The power demand forecast up to 2040 is shown in Figure 5.2-2. In 2040, the BAU case would be 
43,016 GWh, the Reference case 66,096 GWh, and the High case 94,851 GWh, 11 to 25 times the 
2015 value of 3,866 GWh. The policy intervention scenarios were 81,959 GWh for the 7.2% GDP 
growth scenario and 115,294 GWh for the 9.2% GDP growth scenario in 2040, which were 21 to 
30 times the 2015 values.

5.3 P OWER DEMAND FORECAST METHOD 
In this section, we study the power demand forecasting methodology and the population and GDP 
used in the analysis. Regarding the power demand forecasting method, considering the availability 
of necessary data and policy target s, we study the methods suitable for Nepal by referring to  the 
NEA and Japanese demand forecast. It is anticipated that Nepal will expand its power exports to 
India and Bangladesh in the future.  T hese exports will be covered by surplus electricity after 
supplying internal demand. The demand forecast in this chapter targets internal demand. 
5.3.1 Sector Classification 
To improve the accuracy o f electricity demand forecast , we decided to forecast by sector and 
studied sector classification. Table 5.3-1 shows the sector classifications in the demand forecast s 
for Japan, NEA, and the JICA (Japan International Cooperation Agency) Study Team. 
According to the actual electricity sales in Figure 5.1-2, in 2021, the domestic, industrial, and 
commercial sectors accounted for 88% of the total, and the ratio of other sectors was a little.  In 
addition, due to differences in parameters that are highly correlated with electricity demand in each 
sector, the domestic, industrial, and commercial sector should be forecasted separately to improve 
forecast accuracy. So, we classify the sectors other than the three s ectors as other s sector and 
forecast the power demand up to 2040 based on past da ta for four sectors (domestic , industrial, 
commercial, and others).

5.3.2 Growth Scenario 
Three growth scenarios are set for each sector: High, Middle, and Low. The High case is the highest 
growth scenario. The growth rate uses the policy target values.  The Middle case is the scenario 
considered most realistic. The growth rate is set based on data from the past ten years.  The Low 
case represents the lowest growth scenario. The growth rate is set assuming that growth will 
continue as it has for the past ten years. 
As mentioned earlier, policy targets in Nepal are set extremely  high, therefore the policy targets 
have been set as the High case . As an example, Figure 5.3-1 shows a graph of electricity 
consumption per capita. The actual results are increasing at an average of 12 kWh per year, while 
the policy targets (Nepal SDG Status and Roadmap 2016-2030, The Fifteenth Plan) are very high: 
1500 kWh for 2030 and 137 kWh/year as growth, and 3500 kWh for 2044 and 126 kWh/year as 
growth.

The electricity demand forecast method, details of which are described in Section 5.4, is based on 
the creation of predictive equations from historical data.  However, the impact of future policy 
interventions on electricity demand is not reflected in the historical data and must be considered 
separately. Policies that will affect future e lectricity demand need to be considered  and reflected 
in electricity demand. 
5.3.3 Power Demand Forecast Flow 
Figure 5.3-2 shows the power demand forecast flow. 
In Step 1, electricity demand in the four sectors is forecasted based on historical data (without 
policy intervention). 
The impact of policy intervention on electricity demand is studied in Step 2.  The impact of the 
policy intervention determined in Step 2 is added to the electricity demand forecast determined in 
Step 1 to finally forecast electricity demand through 2040, taking into account the policy 
intervention. 
In step 3, peak demand is calculated using the electricity demand obtained in step 2.

5.3.4 Population Transition Forecast 
The population is considered to be highly correlated with electricity demand and is an important 
parameter in electricity demand forecasting.  The population is also used in JICA study team's 
electricity demand projections. Appropriate forecast of future population transition is important in 
electricity demand forecast. 
As a global population trend , i n the United Nations ’ World Population Estimates 2019, it is 
concluded that the world’s population could peak at almost 11 billion people around at the end of 
this century. The world’s population is aging due to longer life expectancy and lower fertility rates. 
And the number of countries with declining populations is increasing, and by the end of this century, 
the population is expected to shift from an increasing to a decreasing trend. 
According to the United Nations projections (https://population.un.org/wpp2019/), the population 
of Nepal is expected to grow between 1.5 and 2% until 2025, with a lower growth rate in 2026. 
Figure 5.3-3 shows the population transition of Nepal. The population has continued to grow since 
1960, the first year of statistical data, and has remained flat from 2010 to 2015. Since 2015, the 
population has been increasing again, and according to United Nations projections, it is expected 
that the upward trend will continue until around 2040.

(3) Assumption of GDP growth rate for electricity demand forecast 
GDP, one of the parameters highly correlated with electricity demand, is used in electricity demand 
forecast. Therefore, future GDP assumptions are necessary for electricity demand forecast. 
As mentioned in Section 5.3.2, the target value of the GDP growth rate of G oN is high, so we 
decided to apply the policy target value to the high case of the growth scenario. Assuming that past 
growth will continue, the average growth rate of the past ten years was adopted for the Low case.

realistic assumption.

used in the demand forecast for the domestic sector, which is discussed in detail in Section 5.4.1.

5.4 ELECTRICITY DEMAND FORECAST 
As described in Section 5.3.1, the electricity demand forecast by the JICA study team is classified 
into four sectors to forecast electricity demand. And three growth scenarios have been set for each 
sector. The e lectricity demand forecast for each sector is based on historical data to create 
predictive equation. 
5.4.1 Domestic Sector 
Electricity demand in the Domestic Sector is considered to be most correlated with population. 
However, using population alone as a parameter is insufficient as a forecast, since only the impact 
of population is reflected in the forecast. In order to reflect factors other than population, electricity 
consumption per capita is added as a parameter, taking into account the existence of policy targets 
and availability of data, with reference to the electricity demand forecast for the Domestic Sector 
in Japan. The predictive equation is developed as follows: 
Predictive equation:  
(Population) × (Electricity consumption per capita for household use [kWh]) 
To forecast electricity demand in the Domestic Sector, it is necessary to determine the population 
and electricity consumption per capita for household use. The population growth rate is assumed 
constant at 0.93% as described in Section 5.3.4 and is common to the three growth scenarios (High, 
Middle, and Low). 
The electricity consumption per capita for households is forecasted based on an increasing rate for 
each growth scenario.  For the Low and Middle cases, the domestic electricity consumption per 
capita was calculated for both growth scenarios by approximating a curve with the past ten0 years 
of residential electricity consumption per capita data to obtain a predictive equation for residential 
electricity consumption per capita after 2022. 
For Low case, a linear approximation is adopted, assuming that historical growth continues.  For 
Middle case, an exponential approximation is adopted, assuming a constant growth rate. For High 
case, the policy target value of electricity consumption per capita is adopted. The policy targets for 
electricity consumption per capita for Nepal are 1500 kWh in 2030 (Nepal SDG Status and 
Roadmap 2016-2030) and 3500 kWh in 2044 (The Fifteenth Plan (Fiscal Year 2019/20 - 2023/24)). 
Based on these values, the electricity consumption per capita for households is obtained until 2040. 
As described above, the electricity consumption per capita for households is obtained from pa st 
performance and policy targets, and the electricity demand for the Domestic Sector is forecasted 
until 2040 using the forecast equation. The results are shown in Figure 5.4-1 and Table 5.4-1. In 
the High case, the electricity demand in 2040 is 13.5 times higher than the value in 2021. The High 
case uses the policy target and has a very high value, as discussed in Section 5.3.2.

5.5 E LECTRICITY DEMAND FORECAST CONSIDERING POLICY INTERVENTION 
This section examines new policies, i.e., factors not included in the existing sectoral electricity 
demand forecasts. The electricity demand forecast considering policy intervention is calculated by 
adding the demand forecast value due to policy intervention calculated in this section, to the 
demand forecast value forecasted for each of the four sectors in the previous section. 
The seven new policies considered in this  report are listed in Table 5.5-1. These were selected 
based on reports published by Go N and discussions in the WG, and JCC  ( Joint Coordination 
Committee).

interventions, and does not examine the budget, time, and feasibility of development to achieve 
the policy goals.

below. 
5.5.1 Electric Vehicles (EV) 
This section describes the electricity demand in the case of widespread adoption of electric vehicles. 
The popularization of electric vehicle is an important policy in Nepal, as discussed in the Electricity 
Demand Forecast Report (2015-2040) by WECS. In Nepal, the following two government-issued 
reports set targets for the penetration rate of electric vehicles. 
- Second Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) 2020 (Hereinafter NDC Report): 
 By 2025, 25% of private car sales and 20% of public car sales will be electric vehicles. 
 By 2030, 90% of private car sales and 60% of public car sales will be electric vehicles.

- Sustainable Development Goals Status and Roadmap: 2016-2030 (Hereinafter SDG Roadmap): 
 50% electric vehicles in public transport systems by 2030. 
The percentage of electric vehicles in 2040 was estimated by applying each of the targets in the 
above reports, and based on JCC discussions, the target wit h a higher percentage of electric 
vehicles in 2040 was adopted. 
The electricity demand forecast by electric vehicles was calculated by dividing into the following 
four steps: 
1) Prediction of the number of future vehicles 
2) Prediction of the percentage of electric vehicles 
3) Examination of electricity consumption by vehicle type 
4) Calculation of future electricity consumption

5.5.3 Smart Street Light 
Smart street lights are being introduced in Nepal. Smart street light in Nepal is a combination of 
LEDs, solar cells, and batteries. In the examination of policies for the introduction of smart street 
lights, this paper refers to the results of the recent achievement in the introduction of smart street 
lights as described in Annual Report 2020/21 issued by NEA.  Smart street lights began to be 
introduced in earnest after fiscal year 2020, and the number of consumers (number of street lights) 
and the electric demand increased significantly.  While the number of consumers has grown 
significantly since 2018, the electricity demand per consumer has been decreasing, which means 
that the number of smart street lights with energy-
saving effects has been increasing. On the basis of this 
result, this section examines the number of installation 
and predicts the electricity demand. 
Electricity demand was calculated by regression 
analysis with the number of consumers as the 
explanatory variable and electricity demand as t he 
objective variable. Table 5.5-9 shows the electricity 
demand for each year calculated based on this 
approach, resulting in electricity demand of 335 GWh 
in 2040. 
5.5.4 Electric Railway 
The introduction of electric railway is also discussed in the Electricity Demand Forecast Report 
(2015-2040) by WECS and is considered one of the most important policies in Nepal. Among the 
government-issued report, The Fifteenth Plan (Fiscal Year 2019/20-2023/24) (hereinafter referred 
to as the 15th Plan) issued by the NPC (National Planning Commission) sets the targets of 348 km, 
900 km, and 2200 km of railroads to be opened in 2024, 2030, and 2044, respectively. 
The year of opening and total length of electric railroads set based on the 15th Plan targets are 
described here. As of 2019, 42 km of railway have already opened, but since these are not electric 
railways, they are not included in the electricity demand. As for the target of open ing 348 km of 
railways in 2024, since it was pointed out in the WG that this target would be unrealistic, they are 
also not included in the electricity demand. Therefore, only the target of opening the 900 km 
railway in 2030 was adopted. 
The concept of ele ctricity demand is described below. The calculation method was based on 
multiplying the total length of railroads by the electric consumption of the electric railway per 
kilometer. For the calculation of electric consumption of electric railways per kilometer, we refer 
to the data on electric consumption of electric railways of Japanese railway companies. Using the 
total length of 4,000 km of railways and an annual operating electric consumption of 1.33 TWh, 
the electricity consumption per kilometer is 0.3325 GWh/km. 
From the above, annual electricity demand will reach 299 GWh after 2030, when the electric 
railway will be in operation.

The concept of electricity consumption in steelworks is discussed. Various types of steel exist, such 
as converter steel, scrap electric furnace steel, and DRI (direct reduced iron) electric furnace steel, 
depending on the production method. Her e, we assume the electricity consumption required for 
the production of the most basic type, converter steel, which uses iron ore as its main raw material. 
According to a document from the RITE (Research Institute of Innovative Technology for the 
Earth), (https://www.rite.or.jp/system/global-warming-ouyou/download-data/Comparison_ 
Energy Efficiency2019steel.pdf), the typical energy required to produce 1 ton of converter steel 
(crude steel) is assumed to be 4.8 GJ/t. 
When converted to electricity consumption according 
to the primary energy conversion method of the IEA 
statistics, the amount is 444 kWh/t (calculated as 1 
kWh = 3.6 MJ / 0.333 = 10.8 MJ). Multiplying this by 
the production weight target, the electricity demand 
per steelworks plant is calculated to be 66.6 GWh. 
In this section, it is assumed that one plant opens every 
two years, and ten plants will be opened in 2040, 
resulting in an electricity demand of 666 GWh. For the 
other years, the consumption would be as shown in

5.5.7 Improvement of Energy Efficiency 
The improvement of energy efficiency measures was adopted from the report "National Energy 
Efficiency Strategy, 2075 (January 2019)" published by GoN. The goals stated in this report are as 
follows 
"To double the average improvement rate of energy efficiency in Nepal from 0.84% per year, which 
existed between 2000 and 2015 AD to 1.68% per year in 2030 AD." 
Based on the goals described in the above report, the year in which energy efficiency improvement 
is achieved and the efficiency rate were set in this study as follows: 
- In 2022, the efficiency rate is the current 0.84%. 
- Increase at a constant rate to 1.68% in 2030. 
- After 2030, the rate is set uniformly at 1.68%. 
The concept of electricity demand forecast is described below. By applying this policy, electricity 
consumption will be reduced by the amount of the energy efficiency ratio in proportion to 
electricity demand. In each fiscal year, after calculating the sum of electricity consumption from 
all sectors and policies excluding energy efficiency measures, the electricity consumption is 
reduced by the amount corresponding to the energy efficiency rate. Therefore, different electricity 
demands are reduced in each of the High, Middle, and Low scenarios. 
Based on the above, Table 5.5-14 shows the reduction in electricity demand in each scenario. In 
the Middle case, 1,066 GWh will be reduced in 2040.

5.6 PEAK DEMAND FORECAST 
This section describes the elements necessary to calculate peak demand and its process. In the 
previous sections, electricity demand has been forecasted at the receiving end, as shown in Figure 
5.6-1. In the peak demand forecast discussed in this section, on the other hand, the peak demand 
at the sending end was forecasted. 
This is because power for station operation varies depending on the type of power source, and the 
power generation capacity is determined after considering the type of power source in the power 
development plan. 
To calculate the peak demand at the sending end, the system losses in the circuit and the load factor 
were considered. 
 
 
Source: Prepared by JICA Study Team 
Figure 5.6-1  Power Flow and Electrical Diagram 
5.6.1 System Loss Forecast 
For the forecast of system loss, we referred to the data listed in NEA ’s Annual Report. The 
forecasting method of system loss is described below. Forecast formulas for both distribution loss 
and transmission loss were prepared using a logarithmic approximation based on the data for the 
period 2012- 2021. The logarithmic approximation was used because although syste m loss is 
decreasing, it cannot be zero. Using the forecast formulas, we calculated the distribution loss and 
the transmission loss up to 2040. System loss was calculated as the sum of the distribution loss and 
the transmission loss. 
Figure 5.6-2 and Table 5.6-1 show the results of the forecast of the system loss through 2040. The 
system loss was 17.18% in 2021 and forecasted to be 11.7% in 2040. NEA’s forecasts also show 
the same trend of annual decreases, reaching 8.4% in 2040.

5.6.2 Load Factor Forecast 
For the load factor forecast, we referred to the data provided in NEA ’s Annual Report. Based on 
the load factor data for the period 2012 -2021, we developed a logarithmic approximation and 
calculated the load factor up to 2040. The logarithmic approximation was used because although 
the load factor tends to increase, it does not become 100%. 
Figure 5.6-3 and Table 5.6-2 show the results of the forecast of the load factor through 2040. The 
load factor was 69.0% in 2021 and forecasted to be 70.1% in 2040. In NEA’s forecast, on the other 
hand, load factor is forecasted to be a uniform 65% after 2022, taking into account the values for 
the most recent year.

5.7 THE RESULTS OF POWER DEMAND FORECAST 
Figure 5.7-1 shows the relationship between GDP per capita and electricity consumption per capita 
for each country in South and Southeast Asia. For Nepal, the actual values for 2021 and the Middle 
case forecasts for 2030 and 2040 are shown in orange, respectively. For the other countries, the 
actual values for 2019 are shown in blue. 
The evolution of Nepal's data shows that the plot position moves to the upper right as the years 
pass, resulting in an increase in electricity consumption as the economy grows. The data for Nepal 
is located within the distribution for the other South and Southeast Asian countries, suggesting that 
the demand forecast results are generally reasonable. 
 
 
Source: Prepared by JICA Study Team

peak electricity demand, and electricity demands per capita from 2021 to 2040, as well as their 
annual growth rates and multipliers to the 2021 value. Electricity demand was 7,102 GWh in 2021 
and is forecasted to be 62,390 GWh in 2040, which is 8.78 times to the value in 2021. Peak demand 
was 1,482 MW in 2021,  and is forecasted to be 11,510 MW in 2040, which is 7.77 times to  the 
value in 2021. Electricity demand per capita was 242 kWh/capita in 2021 and is forecasted to be 
1,779 kWh/capita in 2040, which is 7.37 times the value in 2021. 
As shown by comparisons with existing forecast results in Figure 5.6-5 and comparisons with other 
countries in Figure 5.7-1, the Middle case assumes realistic growth, and the Middle case values 
will be used for power supply development planning and grid planning.

6.1 D ATA COLLECTION OF HYDROPOWER DEVELOPMENT SITES 
6.1.1 The DoED Project List 
DoED (Department of Electricity Development ) of MoEWRI (Ministry of Energy, Water 
Resources and Irrigation) has been authorized to manage administrative process of generation and 
transmission projects over 1MW and more such as survey, construction and operation. These power 
generation projects include those promoted by government owned developers such as VUCL 
(Vidhyut Utpadan Co., Ltd. ) and NEA (Nepal Electricity Authority). The projects are classified 
into the category shown in Table 6.1-1 based on the progress of development.  
 
Table 6.1-1  Category of the Do ED Project List based on the Progress 
Category Explanation 
Power Plants Projects under operation 
Survey License Projects licensed to study generation, transmission, 
distribution survey of the project Application for Survey License 
Construction License Projects licensed for construction and operation of the 
project Application for Construction License 
GoN Reserved Projects Projects which are owned by GoN for study / survey and 
construction 
Other Projects “Forwarded to Local Government ” or “Selected for 
Competitive Bidding” 
Source: Prepared by the JICA Study Team based on DoED  List 
Developers who obtained a Survey License need to complete F/S (Feasibility Study) and EIA 
(Environmental Impact Assessment) or IEE (Initial Environmental Evaluation) within five years. 
If the result of studies show that the project is fea sible, the developer apply to DoED  for the 
Construction License. After the evaluation and approval by DoED , MoEWRI issues the 
Construction License for the developer. Power plants that have completed construction and are in 
operation are registered  as Power Plants ”. Sites of which development licenses have not been 
obtained by developers are classified into GoN  (Government of Nepal) Projects which include 
projects where DoED is carrying out studies or which the license has bee n canceled. In addition, 
there is the category of “ Other Projects” on which sites of Forwarded to Local Government  and 
Selected for Competitive Bidding  are registered.  Projects under the jurisdiction of DoED  are 
published on the website http://www.doed.gov.np/issued_licenses.php. Approval procedures for 
licenses are shown in Section 2.2.5. 
A list of each category as at May 2021 is provided in the Appendix, and the number of sites and 
installed capacity (MW) is shown in Figure 6.1-1. At this moment, a total of 829 hydropower sites 
(42,233.3 MW) were registered on the DoED  project list, including Power Plants of 109 sites 
(1,261.7 MW), Construction License of 241 sites (8,317.1MW), Application for Construction 
License of 28 sites (2,735.4 MW), Survey License of 262 sites (16,520.6 MW), Application for 
Survey License of 14 sites (259.4 MW), and GoN Projects of 175 sites (13,139.2 MW).

Source: Prepared by the JICA Study Team based on DoED  List 
The number of sites and installed capacity by the status of progress and river system is shown in 
Table 6.1-3. The Karnali River has only three small hydropower sites operating on tributaries at 
present and there are many sites under Survey License and GoN Reserved. On the other hand, 
development itself is progressing and Construction Licenses have been granted to projects with 
860MW on Tiala Nadi. Phukot Karnali and Betan Karnali, located on the main river, have been 
also surveyed. 
Gandaki river is being developed owed to the location where is near ma ny existing power plants 
and demand areas. There are promising river basin on the main and tributary rivers such as Kali 
Gandaki, Badigad Khola, Myagdi Khola, Marshandi and Trishuli at where many projects have 
been surveyed and been in the construction stage. As the residual head in these river system have 
been already identified and occupied by Survey License, it is assumed that there is less possibility 
to develop new hydropower projects from the aspect of development right. 
The Koshi River is in a simila r situation to the Gandaki River, with the majority of sites in the 
construction phase, especially in the river systems closer to Kathmandu such as Balephi Khola, 
Bhote Koshi, Likhu Khola and Tama Koshi. Although there are relatively less sites with 
Construction Licenses, promising potentials are basically identified and occupied by projects with 
Survey License in Koshi main river, Dudh Koshi, Arun and Tamoricese. 
In other river system, Pancheshor River has five sites that have progressed to Construction Licence, 
with the tributary Chamaliya Nadi also operating an existing power plant. Meanwhile, there are no 
hydropower projects with Construction License. 
Overall trends of new hydropower development by river systems and progress are summarized as 
shown below;

to most of promising sites at Gandaki and Koshi River. Regarding Karnali River, promising 
sites including main river are occupied by Survey Licenses. Promising sites classified into 
GoN Reserved are also being surveyed by mainly DoED.

6-6 
 
 As hydropower sites of 30 MW or more have generally been identified and granted licenses 
throughout the country, the challenge is how to realize the development of these sites. 
 Total installed capacity of hydropower projects with Construction License alone amounts 
to 8,317.1MW which is 5.3 times of the total demand of 1,578 MW in 2020. Utilization of 
surplus generated electricity, i.e. the promotion of power trade is an urgent issue in Nepal. 
6.1.3 Subject and Methodologies of Data Collection 
Hydropower is unique power sources which output and annual power generation are depended on 
natural conditions at project sites. Therefore, accuracy of these information directly affect to the 
quality of power generation development planning and power system planning in Nepal where 
hydropower is majority of the power sources. In this study, key information such as coordinates of 
site, power planning, hydrological information, salient features of facilities, accessibility, 
environmental and social considerations and project cost will be collected in order to provide the 
basis of technical studies. In particular, feasibility of cascade operation is studied on the 
development of hydropower projects with reservoir type, considering reservoir capacity, regulation 
of river flow and supply – demand regulating function such as maximum output and peak hours  
It shall be noted that hydropower development information, particularly IPP projects carried out 
by private companies is highly confidential and must be handled with the utmost care.  
(1) Subject on the Data Collection 
As shown in Table 6.1-2 and Figure 6.1-3, the number of sites is dominated by small and medium 
hydropower of 30 MW or less, but these sites are mostly planned as ROR type and represent a 
small proportion of the total installed capacity in the DoED Project list. Small and medium 
hydropower of 30 MW or less have a relatively low environmental impact. In addition, although 
output and generation varies by each river system, the trend of them is similar to the typical pattern 
of ROR. Although IPSDP is a medium - to long-term development plan in the power sector of 
Nepal and covers power plants connected to integrated Nepal power system, the share of installed 
capacity and annual electricity generation by these small and medium scale hydropower plants is 
relatively small. Therefore, as a rule, the information collected in this study will be applied to sites 
above 30 MW, while for sites below this level, the information provided in the DoED  list of 
projects will be applied. 
Although the impact of the size of the power plant on these small and medium hydropower is minor, 
issues such as ove rlapping development rights with other power plants are expected. Additional 
information will be collected separately for these locations.

6.3 AGGREGATION OF THE HYDROPOWER DEVELOPMENT PROJECTS 
In this section, hydropower development projects are summarized based on river system/river  
basin, inconsideration of location, water level and river discharge.  Possibility of development of 
storage projects and cascade operation are also discussed. 
The optimization for each river system considers the integrated water operation of longitudinally  
cascaded hydropower plants from upstream to downstream , ensuring that discharge from the 
upstream to the downstream are utilized without loss (spillage without power generation). By 
optimizing water operation in cascade management, both power generation and overall benefit in 
the river system  can be maximized. In this context, it is essential to verify the longitudinal 
consistency of hydropower development in plant discharge capacities of upstream and downstream 
power plants. If the plan t discharge of down stream hydropower plants is smaller than that of 
upstream plants, discharge from upstream may be spill downstream without generating power. In 
case such spilled discharge occurred in river system, it is indicate d that  there are longitudinal 
inconsistencies in each river system in this section. 
Ideal optimization involves constructing a storage type hydropower plant upstream to store excess 
flow during the rainy season, which can be utilized  for power generation during the dry season 
when flow is reduced . This allows downstream hydropower plants to fully utilize the regulated  
flow, balancing flows between rainy and dry season and increasing power generation during the 
low-flow dry season. However, the construction of storage type hydropower plants is constrained 
by topographic conditions that can accommodate large reservoirs and by the presence of 
communities or settlements affected by reservoir development. Although the potentials of new 
storage type hydropower projects in the major river systems  were examined in this Study , it is 
assumed that new potentials of the large storage type hydropower developments are limited beyond 
current projects planned in Nepal. 
6.3.1 Karnali River System 
(1) Karnali River (including Mugu Karnali) 
There are 10 projects on the main Karnali River including Mugu Karnali River None of them has 
proceeded to Construction phase. Therefore, there are rooms for optimization study in 
consideration of integrated river system development. 
Only Mugu Karnali Storage ( SL, 1902MW, 654m
3/s) is the storage type hydropower project. 
Additional new storage projects are difficult to be identified on this river in consideration of 
topography. Therefore, cascade operation is recommended in consideration of regulating water by 
Mugu Karnali located at upstream most.  
For the downstream projects Phukot Karnali (ACL, 426MW), Upper Karnali (SL, 900MW), and 
Betan Karnali (ACL, 430MW), while data on design discharge and operation has not been obtained, 
it has been confirmed that there are rooms  for optimization from the perspective of whether they 
are planned with consideration for cascade operation. Specifically, regarding the design discharge 
[m
3/s] in Table 6.3-1, the discharge volume of Mugu Karnali downstream (66.0m 3/s) is smaller 
compared to that of Upper Mugu Karnali upstream (68.7m3/s), indicating room for revision.

(4) Humla Karnali 
There are 3 projects on the Humla Karnali River. None of them has proceeded to Construction 
phase. Therefore, the optimization of each hydropower development plan is anticipated with an 
integrated basin development. For example, by providing the Humla Karnali Cascade (GoN, 
916MW) located at the uppermost stream with flow regulation capability, it would be preferable 
for downstream Humla Karnali 2 and Humla Karnali 1 to operate at a scale matching the upstream 
design discharge and utilize the regulated flow. Although information is limited, these plans are 
designed as run-of-the-river projects with current design discharges increasing downstream from 
Humla Karnali (89.2 m3/s), Humla Karnali 2 (116.3 m3/s), to Humla Karnali 1 (122.6 m³/s). This 
means there is no longitudinal inconsistency for power generation between upstream and 
downstream sites.  
Regarding the location and water level relationships among the power stations, the uppermost 
Humla Karnali Cascade (GoN, 916MW) requires consideration of the Chuwa Khola HEP (SL, 
70MW), which diverts water from the upstream tributary Chuwa Khola and releases it into the Tila 
River. Additionally, as shown in Figure 6.3-4, the lowermost Humla Karnali-1 HEP (SL, 235MW)
