6.3.2 Bheri River System 
(1) Bheri River 
There are 8 projects on the Bheri River. Only Bheri-Babai Diversion Project ( GoN-under 
construction, 48MW), located at bottom of the river. Therefore, there are rooms for optimization 
of projects in consideration of integrated river system development. 
There are 2 storage type projects; one is Bheri 4 HEP (ASL, 300MW) at downstream and the other 
is Bharbung HEP (GoN, 512MW) at upstream. Cascade operation in consideration of regulating 
water by Bharbung HEP is expected.  
Regarding water utilization, design discharges of Dadagaun Khalanga Bheri Storage HEP (ASL, 
128MW) of 141m3/s is smaller than the one of upstream project of Bheri -2 HEP (SL, 256MW , 
158.4m3/s). In addition, design discharges of Upper Bheri PROR HEP (SL, 300MW), Bheri Nadi-
8 (BR -8) HEP (SL, 125MW), and Bharbung HEP ( GoN, 512MW) contiguously locating from 
downstream to upstream are 66.77m3/s, 58,45m3/s, and 79.24m3/s, respectively. This means design 
discharges of downstream 2 projects are smaller than the one of Bharbung  HEP. Therefore, it is 
recommended to confirm design discharges and water operation in consideration of cascade 
operation so as not to waste the discharges.  
Although information on the current power generation plans for this river is limited, there remains 
room for optimization in planning the specifications and operations of the power stations. From a 
cascade operation perspective across the entire river, this would involve utilizing the regulated 
flow from the upstream Bharbung reservoir operation to enhance the efficiency of downstream 
hydropower stations.

(2) Sano Bheri 
There are 4 projects on the Sano Bheri River including Uttarganga Storage (ACL, 828MW) on the 
upstream tributary which is planned with transferring water to the Badigad River. Because none of 
the projects has proceeded to construction sta ge, there are rooms for optimization study in 
consideration of integrated river system development.  
In this optimization, the diverted flow from the Uttarganga Storage on the Uttar Ganga River to 
the Badigad River is crucial. It is essential to consider the distribution and timing of releases to the 
downstream reaches of both the Uttar Ganga River and the Badigad River, taking into account the 
design discharge, water utilization, and operational plans for downstream areas, including the Sano 
Bheri River on the lower Uttar Ganga and the Bheri River further downstream. Maximizing these 
benefits is vital for optimizing water operations and it is desirable to examine cascade operations 
that incorporate the interests of all stakeholders.

Figure 6.3-10  River Longitudinal Profile of the Kali Gandaki  River (Upstream) 
There are 1 existing and 10 planning projects on the Kali Gandaki River. Upstream 4 projects are 
on process of construction stage. 
There are 4 storage type projects on downstream including Kal igandaki-1 Storage HEP ( GoN, 
844MW) and Kaligandaki 2 Storage HEP ( GoN, 477MW)  on the Kali Gandaki River with 
Adhikhola Storage (SL, 180MW) and Lower Badigad Storage (GoN, 350MW) on the tributaries. 
There is also Utterganga  Storage (ACL, 828MW) withdrawing water from Sano Bheri River to 
Badigad River. Because impact on water utilization and environment by Kaligandaki -1 and 
Kaligandaki-2, it is necessary to confirm if the project plan to be revised in consideration of 
optimization. IMP2019 nominates multi-purposes including irrigation projects at downstream of 
the river.  
In consideration of these issues, studies in consideration of integrated river system development 
with cascaded operation both for Kaligandaki River and Bheri River are recommended. In addition, 
the study results by River Basin Plans and Hydropower Development Master Plans and Strategic 
Environmental and Social Assessment (WECS, WB) should be collected and confirmed. 
(2) Modi Khola 
There are 2 existing hydropower plants and 6 planning projects on the Modi Khola River. Because 
the river is filled with ROR projects including 5 under construction projects, there are few rooms 
for revising plans. There is also few discrepancies on design discharges in consideration of cascade 
operation.

There are 7 planning projects on the Myagdi Khola River. Because most of the river is filled with 
ROR/PROR projects including 4 under construction projects, there are few rooms for revising 
plans. From the perspective of cascade operations, there is a  longitudinal inconsistency in power 
discharges between  upstream and downstream. Specifically, the design discharge decreases 
downstream from Myagdi Khola (12.6 m3/s), Myagdi Khola-B (12.5 m3/s), to Upper Myagdi (12 
m3/s), resulting in a partial mismatch. This necessitates a review of the plan to ensure alignment.

Design discharges and water operation should be confirmed from the view point of cascade 
operation – design discharges of Upper Trishuli 3 A (existing, 60MW), Upper Trishuli -1 (CL, 
216MW), and Upper Trishuli -2 HPP (CL, 102MW) contiguously locating from dow nstream to 
upstream are 51m3/s, 76m3/s, and 116m3/s respectively, with design discharge of the upstream is 
larger than downstream ones . The larger design discharges upstream compared to downstream 
causes water to spill without utilizing it for power generation. From the perspective of cascade 
operation, it is necessary to verify whether the water management is optimized.  In addition, 
IMP2019 nominates Trishuli Shaktikhor Diversion at the downstream of the river which diverts 
water from the Trishuli River to other watershed by irrigation. Therefore, including above issues, 
it is necessary to confirm the water operation through the river system. 
(2) Seti Nadi 
There are 1 existing hydropower plants and 10 planning projects on the Seti River; 4 projects are 
on the construction stages. Although there are small scale ROR/PROR projects on the middle and 
upper reaches of the river, storage type is assumed to be difficult due to the settlements along the 
river. Regarding design discharges in consideration of cascade operations,  
Design discharges and water operation should be confirmed from the view point of cascade 
operation – design discharges of Setikhola HEP (CL, 22MW), Supreme Middle Seti HEP  (SL, 
20MW), Upper Seti -1 HEP (AC L, 13MW), and Seti Khola HPP (CL, 25MW) co ntiguously 
locating from downstream to upstream are 39.9m3/s, 52m3/s, 15.1m3/s, and 39.9m3/s respectively, 
with design discharge of the upstream is larger than downstream ones. 
Storage type hydropower, Tanahu HEP (CL, 140MW) is under construction at the lower part of the 
Seti River. Lower Seti (Tanahu) HEP (SL, 104MW) is also under study just downstream of Tanahu 
HEP with cascade operations. Therefore, these planning and operation including sediment 
management should be considered for optimum operations.

(3) Marsysangdi 
There are 3 existing hydropower plants and 9 planning projects (2 of them cancelled). Since many 
projects are in the construction phase with ROR/PROR types, there is little room to add new 
storage projects or modify plans.  
From perspectives of cascade operation, planning operations including design discharges of 
following projects are recommended to be confirmed. The design discharge of Lower Manang 
Marsyangdi (CL, 140MW)  with 52m 3/s is smaller than the one of Manang Marsyangdi (CL, 
282MW) with 74m 3/s even after adding inflow from Dudh Khola at downstream of Manang 
Marsyangdi. In addition, the design discharges of Upper Marsyangdi 1 (CL, 138MW) , Upper 
Marsyangdi A (existing, 50MW), and Marsyangdi Besi (CL, 50MW) contiguously locating from 
upstream to downstream are 106m 3/s, 53.3m 3/s, and 70m 3/s, respectively. Therefore, it is 
recommended to confirm the design discharges and operations of these projects whether water 
utilization is optimized in consideration of cascade operation.

(4) Budhi Gandaki 
There are 9 projects on the Budhi Gandaki River. Although Budhi Gandaki Storage Hydropower 
Project (GoN, 1200MW) is planned as storage type project, the impact on environment is assumed 
to be high with number of resettlement of 45,000. Other than that, there is little room for additional 
new storage type hydropower development. Although information is limited, the discrepancies of 
design discharges in consideration of cascade operation are not found on this river.

6-34 
 
There are 8 projects on the Dudh Koshi River. Other than 1 project in the construction phase, other 
projects are in the survey phases. Only Dudhkoshi Storage HEP (SL, 600MW)  is storage type 
hydropower project. Other than that, there is no additional new potential for storage type projects. 
Although information is limited, the discrepancies of design discharges in consideration of cascade 
operation are not found on this river. 
Although Dudhkoshi Storage HEP (SL, 600MW) has rooms for increasing water level to increase 
reservoir capacity by increasing the dam height, the relationship of the water levels between 
reservoir and the power station of Dudhkoshi- 2 (Jaleswar) HPP located  at upstream should be 
confirmed. In addition, the power station of Dudhkoshi Storage HEP locates at downstream of the 
intake of Sunkoshi Kamala Diversion Project planned in IMP2019. Therefore, it is recommended 
to confirm the water operation plan whether there are no discrepancy between these two projects.  
(3) Likhu Khola 
There are 9 ROR/PROR projects on the Lihku River. Since most of the projects are in the 
construction phase there is little room to add new storage projects or modify plans. 
Regarding water utilization, the design discharge of Likhu Khola HPP  (CL, 30MW) is 22.9m3/s 
and the one of Likhu-4 (Existing, 52MW) is 26.7m3/s, both are smaller than 38.5m3/s of upstream 
project, Likhu Khola 'A'  (Existing, 51MW). Although these 3 projects are thought to be under 
construction with Construction Licenses, it is recommended to confirm whether the design 
discharges of these projects are optimized in consideration of cascade operation.

Figure 6.3-22  River Longitudinal Profile of the Tamakoshi  and Khimiti River 
There are 1 existing hydropower plant and 5 planning projects on the Tamakoshi River. Many of 
projects on the upstream are in the construction stage, therefore, there is little room for 
modification of the plans. Although Tamakoshi 3 HEP ( ACL, 650MW) was planned as storage 
type hydropower project, the plan has been under revision to PROR type project in consideration 
of the impact to upstream. The discrepancies of design discharges in consideration of cascade 
operation are not found on this river except for the design discharge of Lapche-Tamakoshi HEP 
(SL, 22MW) which is smaller than the upstream one.  
There are 1 existing hydropower plant and 4 planning projects on the Khimiti River chich is a 
tributary of the Tamakoshi River. All projects except for Khimti Shivalaya Storage HPP ( GoN, 
1720MW) are in the construction phase. If Khimiti Shivalaya hHEP to be developed as a storage 
type, cascade operation will be possible. On the other hand, the design discharges of Khimti II (CL, 
49MW) and Khimti -I (existing, 60MW) are 16.11m
3/s and 10.6m3/s respectively, both of them are 
smaller than 90m 3/s of Khimiti Shivalaya ’s design discharge. Therefore, it is recommended to 
confirm these design discharges in consideration of cascade operation. 
(5) Balephi 
There are 9 ROR projec ts on the Ballephi River. Since most of them are in construction phases, 
there is little room for modifying the plans. The discrepancy on design discharges in consideration 
of cascade operation is not found on this river.

Naumure Storage Project ( GoN, 377MW) and Madi Storage HEP (SL, 156MW) are planned on

IMP2019 suggests the water from Naumure Dam  (245MW, 184m3/s) “Naumure Dam, by Rapti

planned to be transferred to the irrigation area without coming back to the river. I MP2019 also 
suggests that the water from “ Madi Dang Diversion Project (61MW, 24.4m3/s)” is planned to be 
transferred to the irrigation area without coming back to the river. Both of project layouts seems to 
be different from the plans based on the data of DoED which plans to generate power immediately 
downstream of the dams. Therefore, further information collection is necessary for these projects.

6.4.2 Considerations on Integrated River System Development 
This section presents the consideration on integrated river system development based on the 
information and discussions summarized in the above sections. The results is summarized in Table 
6.4-2 below. Additionally, the focal points in consideration of integrated river deve lopment are 
shown below. 
First, "1. Project Progress," indicates the general status of the project progress (Survey or 
Construction phase). There is little room for revising the plan for river systems or rivers in the 
"Construction" phase, while those in the "Study" phase have more flexibility. 
"2. Issues on Cascade Operation," suggests topics related to cascade operation (to be confirmed: 
considerable issues or few: few issues). For river systems classified as "to be confirmed," it is 
recommended for GoN to confirm power discharge and other operation rules from the perspective 
of cascade operation. 
Furthermore, after organizing the potential for plan revisions and issues from the perspective of 
cascade operation for each river system, "3. Rooms for Review Study on Integrated River System 
Development" classifies the river systems in four categories: Recommended for re -examination 
(Recommended), possibility for major plan revisions exists but is not clearly necessary (Possible), 
little room for revision (Small), and significantly dependent on the IMP2019 plan (Confirmation 
of IMP2019). 
It is concluded that for future integrated river system development, the first step is to gather 
planning information, including water management and layout of the multipurpose projects in 
IMP2019. Additionally, as part of the examination towards optimal operation for integrated river 
system development, there is particularly room for re -examination in the Karnali River system 
(especially the main Karnali River), the Bheri River s ystem, and the lower Kaligandaki River, 
including the Badigad River tributary. The lower Kaligandaki River, the SunKoshi/DudhKoshi 
River, Babai River, Rapti River, and Bagmati River, is greatly influenced by IMP2019, so these 
plans should be considered in future discussions. 
“1. Project Progress ” presents the progress of development through the river. The river of 
“Construction” shows that many of the developments are already proceeded, therefore, there is 
little room for modification of the plans. “ Issues on Cascade Operation” presents whether or not 
there are issues in consideration of cascade operation. “3. Rooms for Review Study on Integrated 
River System Development ” shows; i) review study is recommended (recommended), ii) there 
may be possibility for modification (Possible), iii) there is little room for modification (Small), and 
iv) it will be affected by IMP2019 and necessary for confirmation (Confirmation of IMP2019)  
This result suggests that data/information of IMP2019 is first priority. In additio n, studies on 
integrated river system development are recommended for Karnali River System (especially 
Karnali River), Bheri River System, and downstream of Kaligandaki River including Baddigad 
River.

6.5 S CREENING OF HYDROPOWER PROJECTS 
This section presents the results of the screening for hydropower projects, which will be applied 
for the formulation of the optimal scenarios demonstrated in the subsequent sections. 
6.5.1 Methodology of Screening 
Screening of hydropower projects were carried out in order to prioritize and select hydropower 
projects for Optimum Scenario by uniform evaluation in same criteria.  Total 865 hydropower 
projects listed in DoED Project List were assessed based on salient features collected and GIS data. 
Evaluation criteria are (1) economy, (2) policy, (3) hydrology, (4) seismic, (5) environment and (6) 
progress of the projects. Based on each point, hydropower projects are classified into four (4) grade 
of “High”, “Moderate”, “Fair” and “Low”. Target of screening shall be hydropower project of 
Survey License, Application for Survey License and Government of Nepal. Existing plants and 
committed projects with Construction License and Application for Construction License are also 
evaluated but excluded from the screening. 
Evaluation Criteria 
The evaluation items are as follows, as shown in Table 6.5-1. Each evaluation item is assessed 
based on the salient features and special remarks noted in the database established in this Survey, 
and GIS data which is based on data collected in the Strategic Environmental Assessment (SEA). 
It should be  noted that since  this approach is applied  for a uniform evaluation of hydropower 
projects based on the uniform  criteria, the accuracy of the evaluation is not high. This screening 
can prioritize hydropower projects; how ever, decisions regarding the implementation of each 
project should be made based on individual Feasibility Studies (F/S) or Detailed Project Reports 
(DPR). 
(1) Economy 
"Installed Capacity [MW]," "Dry Season Generation Ratio [%]," and "LCOE  (Levelized Cost of 
Electricity) [cent/kWh]", and "Transmission Line Length [km]"  are applied for evaluation of 
economy of the projects. 
(2) Policy 
To promote stable power supply especially during the dry season, the promotion of Storage Type 
(STO) is crucial. Therefore, the "Generation Type" of the project is assessed, with STO receiving 
the highest evaluation. 
(3) Hydrological Risk 
In terms of hydrological natural disaster risks, the risk of Glacial Lake Outburst Floods (GLOF) is 
evaluated based on the "Climate Change Vulnerability Mapping for Nepal" (Ministry of 
Environment, National Adaptation Programme of Action (NAPA) to Climate Change, September 
2010). 
(4) Seismic Risk 
Seismic natural disaster risk is evaluated based on the rank of "Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA) 
[gal]." The data for seismic risk is derived from the Nepal Seismological Centre's Earthquake 
Hazard Map (2002).

Final Report Project on Integrated Power System Development Plan  
Annex 6; Hydropower Development Plan  in Nepal 
 
 
6-52 
 
(5) Environment 
The project's environmental risk is evaluated using key items such as "Protected Areas [%]," "Key 
Biodiversity Area (KBA) [%]," "Forest Area [%]," "Ag ricultural Land Area [%]," and "Built -up 
Area [km2]". 
Environmental parameters displayed as "%" are evaluated based on their proportion within the 
licensed project area, including protected areas, Key Biodiversity Areas (KBA), forest areas, and 
agricultural lands. Residential areas are evaluated based on "km
2" within the license area. Although 
the licensed area does not necessarily represent the area impacted by the environment, it is applied 
from a perspective of comprehensive evaluation for all projects with uniform evaluation. 
(6) Progress 
The progress of the projects is assessed for evaluation of the project's reliability. Projects that have 
completed their surveys, including those that have been issued for a Construction License, applied 
for a Construction License, are rated highly as "Studied." Following this, projects that have been 
issued for a Survey License  and applied for a Survey License, and under governmental 
investigation are given the next highest rating as "Under-study." 
(7) Uncertainty 
Uncertainty negatively impacts the project, and thus, it is evaluated. If large -scale resettlement, 
political issues, and/or other significant issues such as indigenous peoples, water use, and cultural 
heritage are anticipated, the project's evaluation score are to be reduced.

(1) Economy 
Evaluation of results of "Installed Capacity [MW]," "Dry Season Generation Ratio [%],"LCOE 
(Levelized Cost of Electricity ) [cent/kWh]", “Assumed COD”, and “Transmission Line Length 
[km]” are presented as follows: 
Installed Capacity [MW] 
In Figure 6.5-2, projects with higher installed capacities are shown in orange, green, and gray, 
in that order. Projects with larger head or design flow tend to have higher installed capacities, 
and they are distributed both upstream and downstream along the main river. 
Dry Season Generation Ratio [%] 
In Nepal, the importance of power generation during the dry season is high. Therefore, projects 
with a high ratio of power generation in the dry season are evaluated highly. Figure 6.5-3 shows 
projects with a high dry season generation ratio, indicated by orange, green, and gray, in 
descending order. Specifically, reservoir -type hydropower, which can regulate  the river flow 
during the rainy season and operate in the dry season, is highly valued. 
LCOE [cent/kWh] 
Figure 6.5-4 shows the levelized cost of electricity (LCOE) per kWh for each project, indicated 
by orange, green, and gray in descending order of cost. Many reservoir -type hy dropower

(5) Environment 
The evaluation results of environmental risk s are presented as follows:  Key items such as 
"Protected Areas [%]," "Key Biodiversity Area (KBA) [%]," "Forest Area [%]," "Agricultural Land 
Area [%]," and "Built-up Area [km2]" are presented. 
Protected Area (%) 
In Figure 6.5-10, the risk levels are indicated in ascending order: orange (not including protected 
areas), green (including less than 10% of the project area), and gray (including 10% or more of 
the project area). Although many projects do not fall within protected areas (orange), it should 
be confirmed in individual studies for projects that include protected areas, especially in the 
upstream areas of the Koshi river system where protected areas are distributed. 
Conservation Area (%) 
In Figure 6.5-11, the risk levels are indicated in ascending order: orange (not including 
conservation areas), green (including less than 10% of the project area), and gray (including 
10% or more of the project area). Conservation areas are distributed in the plains, and although 
many projects do not fall within conservation areas (orange), it is noted that  projects near the 
plains include conservation areas. 
KBA (%) 
In Figure 6.5-12, the risk levels are indicated in ascending order: orange (not including KBA), 
green (including less than 10% of the project area), and gray (including 10% or more of the 
project area). Although many projects do not fall within KBAs (orange), it should be confirmed 
in individual studies for projects that include KBAs, especially in the upstream areas of the 
Koshi river system where KBAs are distributed. 
Forest Area (%) 
In Figure 6.5-13, the risk levels are indicated in ascending order: orange, green, and gray. 
Although some small-scale projects have low risk, many projects include significant forest areas 
within their project areas, it should be confirmed in individual studies. 
Cropland Area (%) 
In Figure 6.5-14, the risk levels are indicated in ascending order: orange (not including farmland), 
green (including less than 10% of the project area), and gray (including 10% or more of the 
project area). While some projects in upstream river areas with less croplands  have low risk, 
many projects include significant croplands  within their project areas , which should be 
confirmed in individual studies. 
Built-up Area (km²) 
In Figure 6.5-15, the risk levels are indicated in ascending order: orange, green, and gray. Many 
large-scale projects are indicated in gray, showing that they include built- up areas within the 
project areas, which implies the potential for resident relocation. This figure evaluates whether 
the project area includes built -up areas; it does not necessarily mean that the residential areas 
are within the construction or reservoir -affected areas. However, attention is required for such 
projects due to the potential for resident relocation, and individual studies are necessary.

6.6 F INDINGS OF HYDROPOWER DEVELOPMENT FOR POWER GENERATION DEVELOPMENT 
PLANNING 
In this section, direction and position of hydropower in PGDP ( Power Generation Development 
Planning) are considered summarizing development capacity, power generation and generation 
cost.  
6.6.1 Considerations on Development Capacity (MW)  
Development capacity is categorized into a) Operation, b) Construction (Construction License, 
Application for Construction License), c) Survey (Survey License, Application for Survey 
License) and d) GoN as shown in the DoED Project List based on the progress. 
With regard to the installed capacity for each site, there have been few changes for a) Operation  
and b) Construction. However many sites in c) Survey and d) GoN  are still under survey and the 
latest information collected has been updated from the list. This study shall be based on the newly 
collected information as far as possible to develop a plan that is as close to the actual situation as 
possible. Table 6.6-1 shows the number of hydropower locations and development capacities at 
each category.

The installed capacity is a) Operation 109 sites (1,210.5 MW), b) Construction 269 sites (10,752.3 
MW), c) Survey 276 sites (16,448.7 MW) and d) GoN 175 sites (11,658.7 MW), for a total of 829 
sites (40,070.2 MW). The figures from the DoED Project List mentioned in Section 6.1.1 are 829 
sites (42,233.3 MW), which is 2,163.1 MW less. It is assumed that this is due to the fact that, in 
general, hydropower developments in Nepal tend to estimate a relatively large installed capacity 
at the time of the initial desktop study. As a result of further studies, these are often optimized in a 
smaller installed capacity by the power generation planning. 
The maximum power demand in 2040 shown in Chapter 5 is 10,352.2 MW. In terms of simple 
output alone, 11,962.8 MW aggregated from a) Operation + b) Construction exceeds the demand. 
However, it is necessary to check whether it is possible to supply electricity by the entire power 
system against the maximum power demand throughout the year including seasonal fluctuations 
in hydropower and reduced output during drought conditions. 
In general, hydropower -centred power supply configurations tend to have a larger developed 
capacity relative to maximum demand to compensate for reduced output during the dry season. In 
Nepal, it is important to consider development capacity from the perspective of power exports in 
addition to domestic demand.

6.6.2 Considerations on Power Generation (GWh)  
(1) Monthly Power Generation of Planned Projects  
According to the hydropower database developed in this Study, information of monthly power 
generation (GWh) is collected from 237 sites of ROR, 32 sites of PROR and 16 sites of STO. 
Monthly power generation ratio of each generation scheme is shown in Figure 6.6-1. 
 
 
 Source: JICA Study Team  
Figure 6.6-1  Monthly Power Generation Ratio by each Generation Scheme 
Power generation ratio of ROR reaches 100% in the rainy season from June to September, while 
it drops to 20 - 40% in dry season from January to March. The highest drop occurs in February, 
when power generation is decreased to 30.0% of the peak output in wet season. 
For PROR, although the maximum output can be maintained due to the possibility of hourly 
regulation, the available amount of river flow for power generation in a day is the same as for ROR 
and the monthly power generation ratio show a similar trend. The highest drop in electricity 
generation occurs in February at 30.4%. 
Reservoir store water from April to June in STO and is full in July and August. Power generation 
tends to continue  during the dry season from December to March. As a result, monthly power  
generation tends to be highest from August to October, when the reservoir is at full capacity. The 
highest drop in generation occurs in February with 47.5% which value indicate a higher regulating 
capacity compared to ROR and PROR. There some sites such as Uttarganga, Badigad Khola and 
Andhikhola who have increased their supply capacity during dry season. However, it should be 
noted that even STO can’t avoid a drop in generation during dry season.

previous section are directly applied. For sites for which no information is available, the monthly

power generation is estimated based on the operating performance of the existing plants of NEA 
and the assumed values from available F/S and patterned as the operating conditions of the ROR, 
PROR and STO. The monthly power generation (GWh) for projects of the DoED Project List 
obtained is shown in Figure 6.6-2. 
    
Source: JICA Study Team  
Figure 6.6-2  Monthly Power Generation of the DoED Project List 
Development of a number of PROR and STO sites are planned in the DoED Project List mainly in 
Survey and GoN . Calculating the monthly power generation of these sites, power generation is 
composed of 38.3% by ROR, 29.8% by PROR and 31.9% by STO  in February and it is possible 
to secure the sufficient regulation capacity in power system operation. 
On the other hand, in terms of monthly power generation ratio, only 32.6% of elect ricity is 
generated in February compared to the highest one in August. It should be recognized that there 
are still seasonal differences in the amount of power generation during the wet and dry seasons  
even with the utilization of PROR and STO. 
These seaso nal fluctuations in the amount of power generation (GWh) are a characteristic of 
hydropower as a power source and similar trends are observed in other countries. In tropical 
climates with rainfall throughout the year such as Indonesia, the differences are relatively small 
but in countries such as Myanmar and Laos, where the dry season is severe, the amount of power 
generation may drop to 10% in some areas. It should be noted that in Nepal, although there is little 
rainfall in the dry season, the water reten tion capacity of glaciers in mountainous areas mitigate 
the decline in flow and the seasonal variations are not extremely large as in other countries. 
In the future, it will be important to reduce these seasonal fluctuations  through the utilization of 
reservoir operations and the promotion of cascade operations in each river system. On the other 
hand, these seasonal fluctuations themselves derive from the nature of hydropower characteristics 
and it is not realistic to completely eliminate them. Therefore, when considering power generation 
development plan, it is necessary to accept these seasonal fluctuations in hydropower. 
(3) Drought Y ear 
Hydropower output and generation varies considerably depending on the annual water supply and 
drought. In addition to seasonal fluctuations, it is also necessary to consider annual variation of 
water availability. From the perspective of power generation development planning, it is important 
to take drought years into account which remarkably reduce supply capacity and reliability in 
Nepal, where the power is mainly supplied by hydropower. It is necessary to set the development 
capacity to ensure a stable supply of electricity even in drought years.

(FY2067/2068: 2011 - FY2078/79: 2022) are summarized in Table 6.6-2.

With regard to annual power generation, the deviation between design values and actual records 
varies considerably between power plants. Kaligandaki A, Mid -Marshangdi and Marshangdi 
which play an important role in domestic power supply are equal to or slightly above their design 
values. On the other hand, the total amount of records is below the design value through the 
observation period. The largest deviation from the design values in the past 12 years was in 2011 
when a global drought occurred. The actual record was only 79.9% of the design values. In addition, 
80.4% was recorded in 2016 when drought also occurred. It is assumed that power generation will 
fall by approximately 20% in drought years. 
In Nepal, hydropower dominate main power sources in generation mix. A 20% drop is extremely 
significant and reserve capacity needs to be set for drought years in order to maintain supply 
reliability. From this context, t wo options are envisaged in Nepal: increasing hydropower 
development capacity or supplementing it with electricity imports. In this case, for the former, 
although electricity supply is possible domestically, excess power will be generated during normal 
and high -flow years. For the latter, the hydropower development capacity is smaller, but the 
dependence on electricity imports is greater. The impact of these drought years will also be taken 
into account in the power generation development planning. 
6.6.3 Considerations on Generation Cost  
The generation cost for the DoED  Project List is estimated in this Section. The feasibility of 
domestic supply and power export is also considered from the aspect of cost. In this Study, IEA 
LCOE (Levelised Cost of Electricity) is used as an assessment method for the economic viability 
of generation cost. This method estimates the cost of power generation (cents/kWh) based on 
capital investment costs (USD/kW), maintenance costs (USD/kW -year) and plant factor (%). 
LCOE is widely used as an evaluation method for each power source. On the other hand, it should 
be noted that kW value of each power source and regulation capacity such as ancillary services are 
not reflected in LCOE. Therefore PROR and STO are at a relative disadvantage due to the simple 
kWh value assessment. 
In some cases, the project cost of hydropower development in Nepal has exceeded the initial budget, 
even for ongoing projects. The validation of the cost estimates has been raised as an issue in 
interviews in stakeholders. Furthermore, it should be noted that the accuracy of construction cost 
estimates differs greatly from one site to another. Thus the results of this study are hard to be used 
to simply assume that hydropower is cheaper. The risk of such an i ncrease in project costs is 
examined in the sensitivity analysis of the Optimum Scenario in Chapter 7. 
(1) LCOE in the DoED Project List 
LCOE for the DoED Project List is estimated based on the total investment cos and plant factor. 
Histogram of LCOE for ROR, PROR and STO is shown in Figure 6.6-3

in Nepal Annex 6; Hydropower Development Plan 
 
 
6-71 
 
 
Source: JICA Study Team  
Figure 6.6-3  LCOE Histogram for ROR, PROR and STO 
An average LCOE of 4.0 cents/kWh is obtained for ROR (187 sites), 5.0 cents/kWh for PROR (25 
sites) and 9.2 cents/kWh for STO (16 sites). 
Median values of ROR are 3.0 - 4.0 cents/kWh and most sites fall within the cheaper level of 3.0 
- 5.0 cents/kWh. M edian values of PROR are 4.0 - 5.0 cents/kWh. STO are widely distributed 
between 6.0 and 16.0 cents/kWh, although the sample size is small.  However, it is assumed that 
the project costs are lower in cascade scheme projects at downstream. 
Results of the estimation indicate that LCOE especially for the ROR, is inexpensive compared to 
international standards. Even if the project costs increase by 30%, the LCOE is assumed to be still 
competitive with thermal power sources such as gas (9.1 cents/kWh) and coal (6.5 cents/kWh). 
(2) Generation Cost in Domestic Supply and Power Export  
Distribution map of LCOE and installed capacity for the DoED Project List is shown in Figure 
6.6-4. It also includes the relation with NEA wholesale price of 8.2 cents/kWh for consumers in 
2021 and the average wholesale price of 5.0 cents in 2021 for the Indian electricity market IEX. It 
shall be noted that the wholesale price of IEX is discussed in Chapter 8. 
As these figures show, the wholesale price of IEX is above most of LCOE for the DoED Project 
List. It is assumed that there is potential for hydropower project in Nepal to wholesale power to 
IEX, although overheads such as wheeli ng charges of transmission lines need to be taken into 
account. Regarding some PROR and STO projects, their LCOE are most likely to be above NEA 
2021 wholesale price for consumers. However, kW values and regulation capacity such as ancillary 
services also need to be assessed for these power sources. 
As discussed in the previous section, power export to neighboring countries such as India and 
Bangladesh need to be considered in addition to domestic demand. In particular, it is envisaged

 The installed capacity of the DoED Project List is updated to 42,233.3 MW (829 sites) based on the

Operation + b) Construction's exceed peak power demand of 10,352.2 MW in 2040.  
 In addition to the consideration of significant drop of power generation in dry season, it is also important

(GWh)  
 Power generation of ROR, PROR and STO in dry season falls to about 32.6% of the total in wet season. 
It is important to utilize reservoir and cascade operations but it is still difficult to resolve the difference 
and planning should be based on these seasonal fluctuations. 
 Based on past experience, the annual power generation in a drought year falls by approximately 20% 
from the design value . Thus, it is necessary to set an appropriate reserve margins  to ensure supply 
reliability. 
 With regard to the amount of power generation, development of only b) Construction will require 
electricity imports except June, July and August. If c) Survey is developed, the shortfall will be largely 
eliminated. Finally if GoN is also developed, it will be possible to supply electricity on its own, including 
during drought years. 
 If hydropower is developed, surplus electricity will need to be exported, mainly during the wet season, 
and if not developed, the shortfall will need to be imported, mainly during the dry season.

wholesale price (5.0 cents/kWh) are above LCOE of most of sites.
